Neon gas at 300 K and 1 bar (state 1) is compressed isothermally to high pressure P_H (typically 20–30 bar for Ne, since it has a relatively high inversion temperature and high J–T coefficient). The compressed gas is cooled in an aftercooler, then in an LN2 bath heat exchanger to 77 K (state 2). It then passes through the main regenerative heat exchanger where it is further cooled to a temperature T_JT-inlet by the cold return stream. It is finally throttled through the J–T valve to 1 bar, producing a mixture of saturated liquid neon (27 K) and vapor. The liquid is withdrawn as product; the vapor returns through the heat exchangers, precooling the incoming stream and leaving the system at state 1.

**Step 3 — Derivation (2 marks)**:
[Use the derivation in Section 3.1.3 above to obtain $x = (H_1 - H_2 - q_L)/(H_1 - H_3)$.]

#### **Model Solution — Q(b) [7 marks — 2 + 2 + 3 split]**

**Part (i) — Fraction Liquefied**:
$$x = \dfrac{H_1 - H_2 - q_L}{H_1 - H_3} = \dfrac{8.20 - 3.05 - 0.30}{8.20 - (-1.20)} = \dfrac{4.85}{9.40}$$
$$\boxed{x \approx 0.516 \text{ or } 51.6\%}$$

**Part (ii) — LN2 Cooling Duty per kmol**:
The LN2 precooler must remove heat from 77 K to whatever temperature the gas enters (we assume 300 K). But our $H_2$ value already reflects the gas *after* the precooler. The heat removed in the LN2 HX per mol is:
$$q_{LN2} = H_{2,\,in} - H_{2,\,out}$$
We approximate $H_{2,\,in} \approx H_1 + RT \ln(P_H/P_L)$ for ideal gas = $8.20 + 8.314 \times 300 \times \ln(25) / 1000$ = $8.20 + 2.49 = 10.69$ kJ/mol.
$$q_{LN2} = 10.69 - 3.05 = 7.64 \text{ kJ/mol}$$
Per kmol of feed: $\boxed{Q_{LN2} = 7.64 \text{ MJ/kmol}}$.

**Part (iii) — Isothermal Compression Work per kmol (ideal gas)**:
$$W_{comp} = RT_1 \ln\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right) = 8.314 \times 300 \times \ln(25) \text{ J/mol}$$
$$= 8.314 \times 300 \times 3.2189 = 8028 \text{ J/mol}$$
$$\boxed{W_{comp} \approx 8.03 \text{ MJ/kmol}}$$

**Overall observation**: LN2 removes ~7.64 MJ/kmol while the compressor does ~8.03 MJ/kmol of *work*. This shows the energy stored in liquid Ne is dominated by the LN2 cooling, not the compression work — *opposite* to the situation at higher temperatures.

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 model]**

**Q (a)** [7 marks]: With the help of a schematic, describe the **Claude cycle** with LN2 precooling as used for **hydrogen** liquefaction. Explain why it is more efficient than the pure Linde–Hampson cycle, with specific reference to the expansion engine.

**Q (b)** [7 marks]: A hydrogen Claude-cycle liquefier pre-cools the gas to 77 K (LN2), then expands a fraction f = 0.6 of the stream from 80 bar to 1.2 bar isentropically in an expansion engine (η_s = 0.80, γ = 1.40, C_p = 14.3 J/(mol·K), T_engine_in = 80 K). The remainder undergoes a J–T expansion from 80 bar to 1.2 bar at 30 K. Calculate: (i) the engine outlet temperature, (ii) the cooling provided by the engine per mole of feed, and (iii) the cooling provided by the J–T expansion per mole of feed (using $\mu_{JT} \approx 0.30$ K/atm for H₂ at 30 K).

---

#### **Model Solution — Q(a) [7 marks]**

**Schematic (3 marks)**: [Use Section 4.2 diagram — feed → compressor → aftercooler → primary HX → LN2 precooler → split → expansion engine + J–T branch → mixer → return.]

**Description (2 marks)**: H₂ gas is compressed isothermally to high pressure (≈ 200 bar) with inter-stage cooling, then progressively cooled in the primary regenerator and the LN2 bath to 77 K. The stream is split into two: a fraction *f* (~0.5–0.7) is sent to an expansion engine at an intermediate pressure (~15–30 bar); the remaining fraction continues to the secondary heat exchanger, then through the final J–T valve to liquefy. The cold engine exhaust mixes with the return stream to cool the incoming gas.

**Why Claude is more efficient than L–H (2 marks)**:
The Linde–Hampson cycle uses *only* the isenthalpic J–T expansion, which is irreversible and discards all the work potential of the high-pressure gas. The Claude cycle supplements this with an *isentropic* expansion engine that produces cooling *and* recovers shaft work, which can be used to offset compressor work. The engine's refrigeration effect per unit work input is much higher than the J–T valve's because the isentropic process approaches the ideal Carnot cooling. Furthermore, because the expansion engine can be staged at progressively lower temperatures, the cycle can reach much lower temperatures (essential for He, beneficial for H₂), making it the *only practical choice* for sub-30 K cryogenics.

#### **Model Solution — Q(b) [7 marks]**

**Part (i) — Engine Outlet Temperature** (isentropic, ideal gas):
$$T_{out,s} = T_{in}\left(\dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} = 80 \times \left(\dfrac{1.2}{80}\right)^{0.4/1.4}$$
$$= 80 \times (0.015)^{0.2857} = 80 \times 0.2721 \approx 21.77 \text{ K}$$

With isentropic efficiency $\eta_s = 0.80$ (for expansion engines, this is defined as actual enthalpy drop / isentropic enthalpy drop, so actual T is *higher* than isentropic):
$$\Delta T_{actual} = \eta_s \Delta T_{isentropic} = 0.80 \times (80 - 21.77) = 0.80 \times 58.23 = 46.58 \text{ K}$$
$$\boxed{T_{out,actual} \approx 80 - 46.58 = 33.42 \text{ K}}$$

**Part (ii) — Cooling from Engine per mol of feed**:
The cooling per mol of *expanded* gas:
$$\Delta h_{engine,per\,mole\,expanded} = \eta_s C_p (T_{in} - T_{out,s}) = 0.80 \times 14.3 \times 58.23 \approx 666 \text{ J/mol}$$

Per mol of *total feed* (multiply by f = 0.6):
$$q_{engine} = f \times 666 = 0.6 \times 666 \approx \boxed{400 \text{ J/mol feed}}$$

**Part (iii) — Cooling from J–T per mol of feed**:
J–T cooling: $\Delta T_{J-T} = \mu_{JT} \Delta P = 0.30 \;\text{K/atm} \times (80 - 1.2) \;\text{atm} = 0.30 \times 78.8 \approx 23.6$ K.

Heat removed per mol of *expanded* gas: $q_{J-T,per\,mole} = C_p \Delta T_{J-T} = 14.3 \times 23.6 \approx 338$ J/mol.

Per mol of *total feed* (multiply by (1 − f) = 0.4):
$$q_{J-T} = 0.4 \times 338 \approx \boxed{135 \text{ J/mol feed}}$$

**Overall comparison**: $q_{engine} = 400$ J/mol vs $q_{J-T} = 135$ J/mol. The expansion engine provides ~3× more cooling per mol of feed than the J–T valve — *demonstrating why Claude is more efficient*.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students lose marks on this topic (compiled from past valuation patterns):**

1. **Confusing L–H with Claude in schematic** [−2 marks]: Drawing a J–T valve where an expansion engine should be, or vice versa. The J–T valve is a *throttle* (no moving parts, isenthalpic); the expansion engine is a *turbine or reciprocating expander* (moving parts, isentropic). Label them clearly.

2. **Forgetting the J–T inversion issue for He and H₂** [−2 marks]: Stating "L–H cannot liquefy He at all" is wrong — it *can*, but only after the gas is precooled below the inversion temperature (~30 K for He). Write *why* precooling is required.

3. **Sign error in $W_{min}$ equation** [−2 marks]: $W_{min} = T_1(S_1 - S_L) - (H_1 - H_L)$. With $S_1 > S_L$ (entropy decreases on cooling) and $H_1 > H_L$ (enthalpy decreases on cooling), the equation is positive. Double-check signs in your derivation.

4. **Using ideal-gas enthalpy for cryogenic conditions** [−1 to −2 marks]: At 77 K and 200 bar, hydrogen is *not* an ideal gas. Use real-gas tables (NIST, Perry's) for accurate enthalpies. In exam problems, you will be given enthalpy values — use them as-is without further correction.

5. **Forgetting the mechanical efficiency $\eta_m$ of the expansion engine** [−1 mark]: Real engines return ~70–90% of their isentropic work to the compressor. If $\eta_m$ is not given, state the assumption ($\eta_m \approx 0.85$).

6. **Not mentioning the "split fraction" *f*** [−1 mark]: In Claude cycle diagrams, label the stream that goes through the engine with the symbol "*f*" and the J–T branch with "(1 − *f*)". Examiners specifically look for this.

7. **Mixing up the order of heat exchangers** [−1 mark]: In an LN2-precooled system, the LN2 precooler is *between* the compressor aftercooler and the cryogenic regenerative HX. Drawing them in the wrong order suggests conceptual confusion.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Linde–Hampson** = pure J–T throttle cycle. *Isothermal compression + isenthalpic expansion.* No moving parts in cryogenic region. **Best for**: high-inversion gases like Ne, N₂, Ar; small-scale plants.
- **Claude** = expansion engine + J–T throttle cycle. *Isothermal compression + isentropic (engine) + isenthalpic (J–T) expansion.* **Best for**: H₂, He; large-scale plants.
- **LN2 precooling** is *mandatory* for H₂ and He; *optional but beneficial* for Ne.
- **J–T inversion temperatures**: Ne ~ 250 K, H₂ ~ 202 K, He ~ 30 K. He is uniquely difficult because the inversion temperature is below LN2 temperature (77 K).
- **Critical temperatures**: T_c(Ne) = 44.4 K, T_c(H₂) = 33.2 K, T_c(He) = 5.2 K. To liquefy, you must cool below T_c *and* apply pressure.
- **Key formula**: $W_{min} = T_1(S_1 - S_L) - (H_1 - H_L)$ — *always* asked at least once per exam.
- **Key formula**: $x = (H_1 - H_2 - q_L)/(H_1 - H_3)$ for L–H yield with heat leak.
- **Key formula**: $T_{after} = T_{before}(P_{after}/P_{before})^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$ for engine outlet (isentropic).
- **Split fraction *f*** in Claude cycle: typically 0.5–0.7 routed through engine.
- **Mechanical efficiency** $\eta_m \approx 0.85$, isentropic efficiency $\eta_s \approx 0.75–0.85$ for cryogenic expanders.
- **Real liquefiers**: helium uses a *Collins cycle* (Claude variant) with LN2 precooling + 2 expansion engines (e.g., Linde L70, Stirling Cryogenics).
- **Hydrogen liquefiers**: NASA, Air Liquide, Linde use LN2-precooled Claude; Praxair uses a *mixed refrigerant* variant.
- **Typical specific work**: L–H for Ne ≈ 15–25 kJ/mol; Claude for H₂ ≈ 45–60 kJ/mol; Claude for He ≈ 5–10 kJ/mol.
- **Carnot efficiency for He**: $T_{sink}/(T_{room}-T_{sink}) = 4.2/(300-4.2) \approx 1.4\%$. Real liquefiers achieve ~5–15% of this minimum (i.e., overall efficiency ~0.07–0.20%).
- **Exam Tip**: Always draw a *labeled* P–H or T–S diagram alongside your schematic. KTU examiners award up to 2 marks for a *correctly annotated* thermodynamic diagram.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ortho to Para Conversion Arrangement in Hydrogen Liquefaction System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET861 - CRYOGENIC ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Ortho to Para Conversion Arrangement in Hydrogen Liquefaction System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are Ortho and Para Hydrogen?

Hydrogen is a homonuclear diatomic molecule (H₂) composed of two identical protons (each with nuclear spin quantum number I = ½). Because the protons are identical fermions, the total wavefunction must be **antisymmetric** under exchange of the two nuclei. This symmetry restriction gives rise to two physically distinct forms of hydrogen:

- **Ortho-Hydrogen (o-H₂):** The nuclear spins of the two protons are **parallel** (↑↑). Total nuclear spin quantum number I = 1, giving a nuclear spin degeneracy of **(2I + 1) = 3**. It must be combined with the odd rotational states (J = 1, 3, 5, ...).

- **Para-Hydrogen (p-H₂):** The nuclear spins are **antiparallel** (↑↓). Total nuclear spin quantum number I = 0, giving a nuclear spin degeneracy of **1**. It must be combined with the even rotational states (J = 0, 2, 4, ...).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ortho and Para are NOT isomers in the chemical sense.** They are **nuclear spin isomers** with identical chemical properties but different magnetic and thermodynamic behavior. The ratio of o-H₂ to p-H₂ is fixed at any given temperature by quantum statistical mechanics, NOT by chemical equilibrium.

## 1.2 Why is Ortho-Para Conversion Critical in Hydrogen Liquefaction?

At ambient conditions (**300 K**), the equilibrium mixture is approximately **75% ortho and 25% para** (this is called "normal hydrogen" or **n-H₂**). At liquid hydrogen temperature (**20 K**), equilibrium demands **~99.8% para-hydrogen**.

The transition from normal hydrogen to nearly pure para-hydrogen is **strongly exothermic**, releasing approximately **527 kJ/kg** (per kg of H₂ cooled from 300 K to 20 K and fully equilibrated). This is a HUGE amount of energy — comparable to the latent heat of vaporization of hydrogen (445 kJ/kg)!

> [!NOTE]
> If a hydrogen liquefaction plant simply cooled normal hydrogen to 20 K without a converter, the heat of ortho-para conversion would continuously boil off the liquid product. Worse, if normal hydrogen is stored as a cryogenic liquid, it slowly self-converts in storage, generating boil-off gas and causing the LH₂ tank to lose product. This is the famous **"ortho-para boil-off problem"** in LH₂ storage.

## 1.3 The Real-World Analogy

Imagine two dancers on a ballroom floor:
- **Ortho-hydrogen** = two dancers spinning in the **same direction** (parallel spins, more energetic).
- **Para-hydrogen** = two dancers spinning in **opposite directions** (antiparallel spins, more stable, lower energy).

At a hot, energetic party (300 K), most dancers spin in the same direction (75% ortho). When the music slows and the room cools down (20 K), the dancers gradually pair up to spin in opposite directions (99.8% para) — but switching directions is awkward and slow without a "dance instructor" (the **catalyst**).

> [!NOTE]
> **Crucial Physical Constant for KTU:**
> - Rotational constant of H₂: **B = 85.4 K** (energy difference between J=0 and J=1 states is 2B = **170.8 K**).
> - Heat of conversion (n-H₂ → p-H₂ at 20 K): **~527 kJ/kg** (or 1.41 kJ/mol per ortho molecule converted).
> - Self-conversion time at 300 K: **years to decades** (very slow without catalyst).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Statistical Mechanics Basis

The equilibrium distribution of ortho and para hydrogen at temperature T is determined by the **Boltzmann distribution** applied to rotational levels, weighted by nuclear spin degeneracy.

### Step-by-Step Logic of Why Equilibrium Changes with T

1. **At very high T (T → ∞):** All rotational levels are equally populated. With degeneracy 3 for ortho (odd J) and 1 for para (even J), the ratio is 3:1 → 75% ortho, 25% para.

2. **At very low T (T → 0):** Only the J = 0 level is populated. J = 0 is even → para state. So 100% para at absolute zero (Third Law of Thermodynamics).

3. **At intermediate T:** Both populations are governed by exp(−BJ(J+1)/T), giving a temperature-dependent equilibrium.

## 2.2 The Need for a Catalyst

Direct conversion between ortho and para requires **flipping a nuclear spin**, which is forbidden for electric dipole radiation (selection rule: ΔI = 0 for the *molecule's* overall nuclear state requires a *magnetic* interaction). The options are:

- **Self-conversion in the gas phase** (homogeneous): extremely slow, takes years at 300 K.
- **Heterogeneous catalytic conversion**: a paramagnetic surface (Fe₂O₃, Cr₂O₃, Ni, etc.) provides the inhomogeneous magnetic field needed to flip the proton spins. Rates become practical (seconds to minutes).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Paramagnetic Catalysts Work:** The inhomogeneous magnetic field of a paramagnetic ion (e.g., Fe³⁺, Cr³⁺, Gd³⁺) couples to the proton nuclear spins via the hyperfine interaction, breaking the spin-symmetry restriction. This is why materials like **hydrated ferric oxide (Fe(OH)₃)**, **chromia-alumina (Cr₂O₃/Al₂O₃)**, **nickel on silica**, and **rare-earth oxides** are used as catalysts.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield)

| **#** | **Quantity** | **Formula / Expression** | **Units / Notes** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Equilibrium ratio (two-level) | $\dfrac{n_{ortho}}{n_{para}} = 3\,\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{2B}{T}\right)$ | $2B = 170.8\ \text{K}$ (rotational) |
| 2 | Para-hydrogen fraction | $x_{para} = \dfrac{1}{1 + 3\exp(-2B/T)}$ | $x_{ortho} = 1 - x_{para}$ |
| 3 | Energy gap per molecule | $\Delta E = k_B \cdot 2B$ | $k_B = 1.381 \times 10^{-23}\ \text{J/K}$ |
| 4 | Energy gap per mole | $\Delta E = R \cdot (2B)$ | $\approx 1.42\ \text{kJ/mol}$ |
| 5 | Heat of conversion at 20 K (n-H₂ → p-H₂) | $Q_{conv} \approx 527\ \text{kJ/kg}$ | Comparable to LH₂ latent heat (445 kJ/kg) |
| 6 | Full conversion heat (100% o → 100% p) | $Q_{full} \approx 703\ \text{kJ/kg}$ | Used in deep calculations |
| 7 | First-order rate of para formation | $\dfrac{dx_{para}}{dt} = k\,(x_{para,eq} - x_{para})$ | $k$ depends on $T$ and catalyst |
| 8 | Integrated rate law | $x_{para}(t) = x_{eq} - (x_{eq} - x_0)\,e^{-kt}$ | $x_0$ = initial para fraction |
| 9 | Time for fractional conversion $\alpha$ | $t = -\dfrac{1}{k}\ln\!\left(1 - \alpha\,\dfrac{x_{eq}-x_0}{1-x_0}\right)$ | Solve for catalyst bed sizing |
| 10 | H₂ rotational constant | $B = 85.4\ \text{K}$ | Fundamental molecular constant |

> [!NOTE]
> **Important Numerical Values for Exams:**
> - Latent heat of vaporization of H₂ at 1 atm: **445 kJ/kg**
> - Specific heat of H₂ gas at 300 K, 1 atm: **14.3 kJ/(kg·K)**
> - Boiling point of H₂ at 1 atm: **20.27 K**
> - Boiling point of N₂ at 1 atm: **77.36 K**

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The ortho-para conversion system is a **non-negotiable** component in every industrial hydrogen liquefier. Modern large-scale plants (e.g., Linde, Air Liquide, Praxair LH₂ plants) integrate **three catalytic converter beds** into the cold box:

1. **Ambient temperature bed** – removes ~50% of conversion heat at 300 K, where the catalyst is most active.
2. **Liquid-nitrogen temperature bed (~80 K)** – removes the next portion using cheap LN₂ cooling.
3. **Liquid-hydrogen temperature bed (~20 K)** – final polishing to achieve >95% para, using the cold return stream from the J-T expander.

The integrated design is also called an **"integral converter"** when the catalyst is dispersed within the heat exchanger matrix (e.g., on plates of a plate-fin heat exchanger).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Equilibrium Ortho/Para Ratio from Statistical Mechanics

**Setup:** Treat ortho and para hydrogen as two non-interacting species in thermal equilibrium. Use Boltzmann distribution on rotational levels, weighted by nuclear spin degeneracies.

**Step 1 — Identify the populations of each rotational level:**

The number of molecules in rotational level J is proportional to the product of rotational degeneracy (2J + 1) and nuclear spin degeneracy ($g_{nuc}$):

$$n_J \propto (2J+1)\cdot g_{nuc}\cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_J}{k_B T}\right)$$

where $E_J = B J(J+1)$ (using k_B·B form, with B = 85.4 K).

**Step 2 — Identify the nuclear spin degeneracies for ortho (odd J) and para (even J):**

For two spin-½ protons, the symmetric spin state has degeneracy $(2I+1) = 3$ (corresponds to ortho, odd J), and the antisymmetric spin state has degeneracy 1 (corresponds to para, even J).

$$g_{nuc}^{ortho} = 3,\quad g_{nuc}^{para} = 1$$

**Step 3 — Apply the two-level approximation (highest populated levels at moderate T):**

For T < 200 K, only J = 0 and J = 1 contribute significantly. So the total populations are:

$$N_{ortho} \approx 3\cdot 3 \cdot e^{-E_1/k_B T} = 9\, e^{-2B/T}$$

$$N_{para} \approx 1\cdot 1 \cdot e^{-E_0/k_B T} = 1 \cdot e^0 = 1$$

Wait — careful. The factor (2J+1) for J=1 is 3, and for J=0 is 1. So:

$$N_{ortho} \approx 3 \cdot 3 \cdot e^{-2B/T} = 9 e^{-2B/T}$$

$$N_{para} \approx 1 \cdot 1 \cdot e^{0} = 1$$

This gives an extra factor of 3 in the numerator from the (2J+1) for J=1.

**Step 4 — Taking the ratio (combining with spin degeneracy):**

$$\frac{N_{ortho}}{N_{para}} = \frac{g_{nuc}^{ortho}\cdot(2J_{odd}+1)}{g_{nuc}^{para}\cdot(2J_{even}+1)}\cdot e^{-(E_{ortho}-E_{para})/k_B T}$$

$$\frac{N_{ortho}}{N_{para}} = \frac{3 \cdot 3}{1 \cdot 1}\cdot e^{-2B/T} = 9 e^{-2B/T}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Correction (commonly seen in KTU textbooks):** Most KTU cryogenic engineering textbooks (including Barron's *Cryogenic Systems* and Flynn's *Cryogenic Engineering*) use a **simplified two-level model** that ignores the (2J+1) weighting difference and gives the **classical 3:1 high-T limit** directly. They write:
>
> $$K_{eq} = \frac{N_{ortho}}{N_{para}} = 3\exp\!\left(-\frac{2B}{T}\right)$$
>
> This approximation is valid for T < 100 K and is **the form you should use in the exam** unless specifically asked for the full statistical treatment.

**Step 5 — Final KTU exam form (using the simplified model):**

$$\boxed{\;\frac{N_{ortho}}{N_{para}} = 3\exp\!\left(-\frac{170.8}{T}\right)\;}$$

$$x_{para} = \frac{1}{1 + 3\exp(-170.8/T)}$$

**Verification at limits:**

- $T \to \infty$: $K_{eq} \to 3$, so $x_{para} \to 1/4 = 25\%$. ✓ (matches normal hydrogen)
- $T \to 0$: $K_{eq} \to 0$, so $x_{para} \to 100\%$. ✓ (Third Law)

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Heat Released During Conversion

**Setup:** Calculate the enthalpy released when 1 kg of normal hydrogen (75% o-H₂, 25% p-H₂) is cooled to 20 K and fully equilibrated to ~100% p-H₂.

**Step 1 — Moles converted per kg of H₂:**

Molar mass of H₂ = 2.016 g/mol, so 1 kg = 496 mol.

Initial ortho moles in 1 kg = $0.75 \times 496 = 372$ mol.
These all convert to para, releasing $\Delta E$ per mole.

**Step 2 — Energy released per mole of ortho converted:**

The energy gap between the J=0 (para) and J=1 (ortho) states is $2B k_B$ per molecule, or $2B R$ per mole:

$$\Delta E_{mol} = 2B \cdot R = 170.8 \times 8.314 = 1420\ \text{J/mol} \approx 1.42\ \text{kJ/mol}$$

**Step 3 — Total heat released per kg of H₂:**

$$Q_{conv} = n_{ortho,initial} \cdot \Delta E_{mol} = 372 \times 1.42 = 528\ \text{kJ/kg}$$

$$\boxed{\;Q_{conv} \approx 527\ \text{kJ/kg}\;}$$

**Step 4 — Comparison to latent heat (a key KTU insight):**

$$\frac{Q_{conv}}{h_{fg,H_2}} = \frac{527}{445} \approx 1.18$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The heat of ortho-para conversion is GREATER than the latent heat of vaporization of hydrogen.** This is why an unconverted LH₂ storage tank boils off completely within a few days/weeks — the heat liberated by para-conversion inside the liquid evaporates the liquid. Converting to >95% para during liquefaction is the **only practical way** to enable long-term storage of LH₂.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Catalyst Bed Sizing from First-Order Kinetics

**Setup:** The catalyzed ortho-para conversion follows first-order kinetics with respect to the deviation from equilibrium:

$$\frac{dx_{para}}{dt} = k(T)\,(x_{para}^{eq}(T) - x_{para})$$

**Step 1 — Separate variables and integrate:**

$$\int_{x_0}^{x}\frac{dx_{para}}{x_{para}^{eq} - x_{para}} = k\int_0^t dt$$

$$-\ln\!\left(\frac{x_{para}^{eq} - x}{x_{para}^{eq} - x_0}\right) = k\,t$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $x(t)$:**

$$x_{para}(t) = x_{para}^{eq} - (x_{para}^{eq} - x_0)\,e^{-kt}$$

**Step 3 — Time required to reach a target para fraction $x_{target}$:**

$$t = -\frac{1}{k}\,\ln\!\left(\frac{x_{para}^{eq} - x_{target}}{x_{para}^{eq} - x_0}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Apply to typical values: $x_0 = 0.25$, $x_{para}^{eq}(20K) = 0.998$, target $x_{target} = 0.95$:**

$$t = -\frac{1}{k}\,\ln\!\left(\frac{0.998 - 0.95}{0.998 - 0.25}\right) = -\frac{1}{k}\,\ln\!\left(\frac{0.048}{0.748}\right) = -\frac{1}{k}\,\ln(0.0642)$$

$$t = \frac{2.746}{k}$$

**Step 5 — Interpret the design implication:** For $k = 0.01\ \text{s}^{-1}$ (typical for a good catalyst at 77 K):

$$t = 274.6\ \text{s} \approx 4.6\ \text{min}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Takeaway:** The rate constant $k(T)$ is **strongly temperature-dependent**. Good catalysts at 80 K give $k \approx 0.05$–$0.1\ \text{s}^{-1}$, while at 20 K $k$ drops to $\sim 10^{-3}\ \text{s}^{-1}$ or lower. This is why **multiple catalyst beds at progressively lower temperatures** are used — doing the bulk of conversion at higher T (fast kinetics) and only final polishing at 20 K (slow kinetics, but only a small residual fraction remains).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Integrated Ortho-Para Conversion in a Hydrogen Liquefaction System

The diagram below maps the three-stage catalytic conversion arrangement as it is integrated into a typical Claude-cycle or Linde-Hampson hydrogen liquefier. Each catalyst bed is **co-located with a heat exchanger** to remove the exothermic heat of conversion at that temperature level.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    compA["H2 Compressor\n(1-200 bar)"] --> coolB["Aftercooler\nCool to 300 K"]
    coolB --> cat1C["Catalyst Bed 1\nAmbient Temperature\nRemoves ~50% conv. heat\nParamagnetic Fe(OH)3"]
    cat1C --> hx1D["Heat Exchanger 1\nCooled by LN2\nStream T: 300 K to 80 K"]
    hx1D --> cat2E["Catalyst Bed 2\nLiquid N2 Temperature\nRemoves bulk conversion\nCr2O3 / Ni on silica"]
    cat2E --> hx2F["Heat Exchanger 2\nCooled by H2 Return\nStream T: 80 K to 25 K"]
    hx2F --> cat3G["Catalyst Bed 3\nLiquid H2 Temperature\nFinal polishing to >95% para\nHydrated Fe2O3"]
    cat3G --> jtH["J-T Valve / Expander\nFinal cooling to 20 K\nLiquefaction"]
    jtH --> tankI["LH2 Storage\n(para-rich, low boil-off)"]

    ln2src["LN2 Refrigeration Loop\n(80 K sink)"] -.-> hx1D
    h2ret["Cold H2 Return Stream\n(from J-T & tank)"] -.-> hx2F
```

## 4.2 Function of Each Block (Engineering Notes)

| **Block** | **Function** | **Typical Equipment** |
|---|---|---|
| Compressor | Pressurize feed H₂ to liquefaction pressure (typically 10–200 bar) | Reciprocating or centrifugal multistage |
| Aftercooler | Remove heat of compression, drop T to ~300 K | Water-cooled shell-and-tube |
| **Catalyst Bed 1** (300 K) | Initiate para conversion; catalyst activity is highest here, kinetics fastest | Fixed bed of hydrated ferric oxide, 0.5–2 m thick |
| HX 1 (LN2 cooled) | Sensible cooling from 300 K to ~80 K, also absorbs conversion heat from Bed 1 | Plate-fin heat exchanger |
| **Catalyst Bed 2** (80 K) | Bulk conversion; equilibrium has shifted to ~50% para, so this is where the largest enthalpy change occurs | Cr₂O₃ on alumina, integrated into HX matrix |
| HX 2 (LH2 cooled) | Further cooling to ~25 K, also absorbs conversion heat from Bed 2 | Plate-fin or coil-wound HX |
| **Catalyst Bed 3** (20 K) | Final polishing from ~85% para to >95% para; small enthalpy change but slow kinetics | Rare-earth oxides or highly active Ni catalysts |
| J-T / Expander | Final expansion produces liquid; some systems use a Claude expander before J-T | J-T valve, or expander + J-T |
| LH₂ Storage | Product collection; only para-rich H₂ stores long-term | Double-wall vacuum-insulated dewar |

## 4.3 Equilibrium Composition vs Temperature (Text-Based Plot Description)

| **T (K)** | **$x_{para}$ (equilibrium)** | **Comment** |
|---|---|---|
| 300 | 25.0% | "Normal" hydrogen, room temperature |
| 200 | 31.6% | Slow self-conversion begins |
| 100 | 38.5% | Below this, catalyst beds become essential |
| 80 | 48.5% | Typical LN₂ cold sink temperature |
| 60 | 65.0% | — |
| 40 | 88.6% | — |
| 30 | 96.4% | — |
| 25 | 98.8% | — |
| 20.4 | 99.79% | LH₂ boiling point — target |
| 14 | 99.99% | Cryogenic storage |

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading guide:** The curve falls on a sigmoidal shape. Below 80 K, the para fraction rises steeply. This is why the catalyst beds are placed strategically: doing conversion **before** further cooling means the released heat can be rejected at **higher temperature levels** (Carnot-efficient), and the catalyst's high-temperature activity is exploited.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. What is ortho-para hydrogen? Why is ortho-para conversion necessary in a hydrogen liquefaction system?**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]`

**Model Answer:**

Ortho-hydrogen has the two proton nuclear spins aligned (parallel), while para-hydrogen has them anti-parallel. At room temperature (300 K), the equilibrium mixture is 75% ortho and 25% para, but at 20 K equilibrium demands ~99.8% para.

Conversion is necessary because:
1. The ortho → para transition releases **~527 kJ/kg** of heat, comparable to the latent heat of vaporization (445 kJ/kg).
2. If normal hydrogen is liquefied and stored without conversion, the slow self-conversion in the liquid continuously boils off the LH₂ (the **"boil-off problem"**).
3. Industrial LH₂ must be **para-rich (>95%)** for storage, transport, and rocket fuel applications.

(3 marks: 1 mark definition + 2 marks justification with numbers)

---

### **Q2. List the common catalysts used for ortho-para conversion of hydrogen. Why is the conversion done in multiple catalyst beds at progressively lower temperatures?**
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Supplementary Exam]`

**Model Answer:**

**Common catalysts:** Hydrated ferric oxide [Fe(OH)₃], chromia on alumina [Cr₂O₃/Al₂O₃], nickel on silica, rare-earth oxides (e.g., Dy₂O₃), and activated charcoal.

**Why multiple beds?**
1. **Kinetics:** Catalytic rate constant $k(T)$ decreases rapidly at low T. Doing most conversion at 300 K and 80 K exploits the high reaction rate.
2. **Thermodynamics:** Releasing conversion heat at higher T is thermodynamically more efficient (lower entropy production, smaller refrigeration load).
3. **Practicality:** No single catalyst maintains high activity over the entire range 300 K → 20 K. Different catalyst formulations are optimized for different T windows.

(3 marks: 1 mark catalyst list + 2 marks multiple-bed justification)

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

#### **Part (a)** — Derivation + Numerical (7 Marks)

**(a)** Derive the equilibrium ratio of ortho- to para-hydrogen as a function of temperature using the simplified two-level statistical model. Hence, calculate the equilibrium para-hydrogen fraction at **80 K** and **30 K**. (Take 2B = 170.8 K.)

**Model Solution:**

**Derivation (5 marks):**

The two-level model considers only J = 0 (para) and J = 1 (ortho). Applying Boltzmann distribution with nuclear spin degeneracies $g_{ortho} = 3$, $g_{para} = 1$ and energy levels $E_{ortho} = 2B$, $E_{para} = 0$:

$$\frac{N_{ortho}}{N_{para}} = \frac{g_{ortho}}{g_{para}} \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_{ortho} - E_{para}}{k_B T}\right)$$

Substituting values:

$$K_{eq} = \frac{N_{ortho}}{N_{para}} = 3 \exp\!\left(-\frac{2B}{T}\right) = 3 \exp\!\left(-\frac{170.8}{T}\right)$$

The para-hydrogen fraction is:

$$x_{para} = \frac{1}{1 + 3\exp(-170.8/T)}$$

**Numerical evaluation (2 marks):**

At T = 80 K:

$$K_{eq} = 3\exp(-170.8/80) = 3\exp(-2.135) = 3 \times 0.1183 = 0.355$$

$$x_{para} = \frac{1}{1 + 0.355} = \frac{1}{1.355} = 0.738 = 73.8\%$$

At T = 30 K:

$$K_{eq} = 3\exp(-170.8/30) = 3\exp(-5.693) = 3 \times 0.00338 = 0.01014$$

$$x_{para} = \frac{1}{1.01014} = 0.9899 = 98.99\%$$

**Final Answer:** $x_{para}(80\ \text{K}) = 73.8\%$, $x_{para}(30\ \text{K}) = 99.0\%$.

---

#### **Part (b)** — Numerical (7 Marks)

**(b)** A hydrogen liquefier processes 200 kg/h of normal hydrogen (75% ortho, 25% para) from 300 K to 20 K. Calculate the total heat of ortho-para conversion that must be removed by the catalyst beds. Use the simplified two-level model. State one engineering implication.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Initial ortho fraction converted to para:**

In 1 kg of normal H₂, 0.75 kg is ortho. The molar mass of H₂ is 2.016 g/mol, so 1 kg = 496.0 mol.

Initial ortho moles per kg: $n_{ortho,0} = 0.75 \times 496 = 372\ \text{mol/kg}$.

**Step 2 — Energy released per mole of ortho converted:**

$$\Delta E_{mol} = 2B \cdot R = 170.8 \times 8.314 = 1420\ \text{J/mol} = 1.42\ \text{kJ/mol}$$

**Step 3 — Total heat per kg of H₂ processed:**

$$Q_{conv} = 372\ \text{mol/kg} \times 1.42\ \text{kJ/mol} = 528.2\ \text{kJ/kg}$$

**Step 4 — For 200 kg/h plant:**

$$\dot{Q}_{conv} = 528.2 \times 200 = 105{,}640\ \text{kJ/h} = 29.3\ \text{kW}$$

**Step 5 — Engineering implication:**

The conversion heat (29.3 kW) is comparable to the liquefaction duty of the H₂ itself. **If conversion is not performed, ~half the refrigeration capacity of the plant would be consumed** by the conversion heat — making the plant uneconomical. This is why every commercial LH₂ plant MUST have catalytic conversion beds.

**Final Answer:** $\dot{Q}_{conv} \approx 29.3\ \text{kW}$ (or 105,640 kJ/h).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

#### **Part (a)** — Descriptive with Sketch (7 Marks)

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the catalytic ortho-para conversion arrangement in a typical hydrogen liquefaction system. Explain the role, location, and choice of catalyst at each stage.

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

A modern hydrogen liquefier integrates **three catalytic converter beds** within the cold box, each at a different temperature level. The arrangement is as follows (refer to Section 4.1 diagram above):

**Stage 1 — Ambient Temperature Catalyst (300 K):**
- **Location:** Immediately downstream of the aftercooler, upstream of the first cryogenic heat exchanger.
- **Catalyst choice:** Hydrated ferric oxide [Fe(OH)₃] or chromia-alumina.
- **Role:** Initiates the conversion at the highest practical temperature. About 50% of the ortho fraction converts here. Kinetics are very fast at 300 K (rate constant $k \sim 1\ \text{s}^{-1}$), so a small catalyst bed suffices.
- **Heat removal:** Conversion heat is dumped to ambient cooling water (300 K sink) — most thermodynamically efficient location.

**Stage 2 — Liquid Nitrogen Temperature Catalyst (~80 K):**
- **Location:** Between the LN₂-cooled HX and the next HX.
- **Catalyst choice:** Cr₂O₃ on alumina, or nickel on silica; these remain active at 80 K.
- **Role:** Converts the majority of the remaining ortho (from ~50% para to ~80% para). The largest single enthalpy change occurs here.
- **Heat removal:** Heat is rejected to the LN₂ refrigeration loop (cheap cooling at 80 K).

**Stage 3 — Liquid Hydrogen Temperature Catalyst (~20 K):**
- **Location:** Final cold stage, after the LH₂-cooled HX, just before the J-T expansion.
- **Catalyst choice:** Highly active rare-earth oxides (e.g., Dy₂O₃, Ho₂O₃) or specially formulated Ni catalysts.
- **Role:** Final "polishing" to bring para fraction from ~80–90% to >95–99%. Kinetics are slow at 20 K, but only a small residual amount needs to convert.
- **Heat removal:** Heat is absorbed by the cold H₂ return stream from the storage tank (very efficient cold recovery).

**Integral Converter Concept:** In modern plate-fin heat exchangers, the catalyst is sometimes **dispersed as a coating on the fin surfaces** ("integral converter"), combining heat exchange and conversion in a single compact unit.

---

#### **Part (b)** — Numerical on Kinetics (7 Marks)

**(b)** The catalyzed ortho-para conversion in a hydrogen stream follows first-order kinetics: $dx_{para}/dt = k(x_{para}^{eq} - x_{para})$. For a catalyst operating at 77 K, the rate constant is $k = 0.05\ \text{s}^{-1}$ and $x_{para}^{eq} = 0.485$. The feed is normal hydrogen ($x_0 = 0.25$). Calculate the **residence time** required in this catalyst bed to reach 47% para-hydrogen.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Set up the integrated first-order rate law:**

$$x_{para}(t) = x_{eq} - (x_{eq} - x_0)\,e^{-kt}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the given values:**

$x_{eq} = 0.485$, $x_0 = 0.25$, $x_{target} = 0.47$.

$$0.47 = 0.485 - (0.485 - 0.25)\,e^{-kt}$$

$$0.47 = 0.485 - 0.235\,e^{-kt}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $e^{-kt}$:**

$$0.235\,e^{-kt} = 0.485 - 0.47 = 0.015$$

$$e^{-kt} = \frac{0.015}{0.235} = 0.06383$$

**Step 4 — Take natural log:**

$$-kt = \ln(0.06383) = -2.752$$

$$t = \frac{2.752}{k} = \frac{2.752}{0.05\ \text{s}^{-1}}$$

$$\boxed{\;t = 55.04\ \text{s}\;}$$

**Step 5 — Physical interpretation:**

A residence time of about 1 minute at 77 K achieves 47% para. This is feasible with a small catalyst bed. By contrast, at 20 K, $k$ drops to $\sim 10^{-3}\ \text{s}^{-1}$, giving $t \sim 2750\ \text{s} \approx 46\ \text{min}$ for the same conversion — confirming the engineering wisdom of doing **bulk conversion at higher T** and only **polishing at 20 K**.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Top Reasons Students Lose Marks on This Topic (KTU 2024 Scheme pattern):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the 75/25 starting ratio.** Many students assume $x_0 = 0$ and "convert" the entire stream, leading to an inflated heat value. The correct initial ortho is **75%** of the mass flow, not 100%.
>
> 2. **Using the wrong formula form.** There are TWO common forms: the **two-level simplified** ($3\exp(-170.8/T)$) and the **full statistical** ($9\exp(-2B/T)$). KTU exams usually expect the simplified form (3:1 high-T limit). Mixing them up gives 25% error in heat calculations.
>
> 3. **Confusing $\Delta E$ per mole vs per kg.** $1.42\ \text{kJ/mol}$ is the energy gap, but the **heat released per kg of normal H₂** is $527\ \text{kJ/kg}$. Many students write one and mean the other.
>
> 4. **Confusing ortho/para fraction in the rate law.** In the rate equation $dx_{para}/dt = k(x_{eq} - x)$, $x$ is the **para** fraction, not ortho. Sign errors cost 2–3 marks.
>
> 5. **Forgetting the role of paramagnetism.** Writing "catalysts lower the activation energy" is incomplete. The correct reason is that **paramagnetic catalysts provide the inhomogeneous magnetic field** required to break the nuclear-spin-symmetry selection rule.
>
> 6. **Mixing up the latent heat of H₂.** It is **445 kJ/kg**, not 50 kJ/kg (which is N₂'s) and not 906 kJ/kg (which is O₂'s). Wrong numbers lose direct marks.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

**A. Definitions (must memorize verbatim):**
- **Ortho-hydrogen:** parallel nuclear spins (↑↑), I = 1, degeneracy 3, odd J states.
- **Para-hydrogen:** antiparallel nuclear spins (↑↓), I = 0, degeneracy 1, even J states.
- **Normal hydrogen (n-H₂):** 75% ortho + 25% para at 300 K.

**B. Key Numbers (must know):**
- Rotational constant B = 85.4 K; energy gap 2B = 170.8 K.
- Heat of conversion (n-H₂ → p-H₂ at 20 K): **527 kJ/kg** (or 1.42 kJ/mol per ortho molecule).
- Latent heat of H₂ at 1 atm: 445 kJ/kg.
- $Q_{conv} > h_{fg,H_2}$ — the critical engineering insight.

**C. The Master Formula (write this on every question you attempt):**

$$\frac{N_{ortho}}{N_{para}} = 3\exp(-170.8/T)\quad\Rightarrow\quad x_{para} = \frac{1}{1 + 3\exp(-170.8/T)}$$

**D. Three-Bed Conversion Architecture (draw the block diagram):**
1. **300 K bed** — Fe(OH)₃ catalyst, removes 50% of conv. heat, dumps to cooling water.
2. **80 K bed** — Cr₂O₃ catalyst, bulk conversion, heat to LN₂ refrigeration.
3. **20 K bed** — Rare-earth oxide, final polishing >95% para, heat to cold H₂ return.

**E. Why catalysts work (theory):** Paramagnetic ions (Fe³⁺, Cr³⁺, Gd³⁺, etc.) provide inhomogeneous magnetic fields that couple to proton nuclear spins via hyperfine interaction, breaking the spin-symmetry selection rule.

**F. Why conversion must happen (engineering):** Self-conversion in storage releases 527 kJ/kg, comparable to h_fg. Unconverted LH₂ boils off in days/weeks. **Converting to >95% para is the only solution to the LH₂ storage boil-off problem.**

**G. Kinetics:** $x(t) = x_{eq} - (x_{eq} - x_0)e^{-kt}$ — first-order approach to equilibrium; k(T) drops ~50× from 80 K to 20 K. This is the design driver for **multi-bed staged conversion**.

**H. Common Exam Sub-Questions:**
- "Find para fraction at T = ___ K" → plug into $1/(1+3e^{-170.8/T})$.
- "Find heat of conversion for ___ kg/h" → $527 \times \dot{m}_{H_2}$.
- "Time to reach ___% para" → $t = -\frac{1}{k}\ln\!\left(\frac{x_{eq}-x_{target}}{x_{eq}-x_0}\right)$.
- "Why catalyst needed?" → paramagnetic field breaks nuclear-spin selection rule.
- "Why multi-bed?" → kinetics faster at high T, thermodynamic efficiency.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Simon Helium Liquefaction System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMET861 - CRYOGENIC ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Simon Helium Liquefaction System # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Simon Helium Liquefaction System** — A continuous, single-cycle regenerative expansion-type cryogenic liquefaction system, conceived by **Franz Simon (Clarendon Laboratory, Oxford, 1932)**, that produces liquid helium (LHe) at its normal boiling point of **4.22 K** by combining **liquid hydrogen (LH₂) bath precooling** with a **final Joule–Thomson (isenthalpic) expansion** through a throttle valve. It is historically significant as the first practical liquefier that **bypassed the multi-fluid cascade** of earlier helium liquefaction technology. The system exploits the thermodynamic principle that **no real gas can be liquefied by isenthalpic expansion alone from ambient temperature**, because helium's **inversion temperature (≈ 43 K)** is far below ambient — yet once precooled below the inversion curve by an external cryogen (LH₂ at 20.4 K), the Joule–Thomson coefficient becomes positive and liquefaction becomes feasible. | Property | Symbol | Value | Unit | |---|---|---|---| | Normal Boiling Point of He | $T_{b,He}$ | **4.22** | K | | Critical Temperature of He | $T_{c,He}$ | **5.20** | K | | Critical Pressure of He | $P_{c,He}$ | **2.26** | bar | | Triple Point of He | $T_{tp}$ | **0.95** | K | | Maximum Inversion Temperature of He | $T_{inv,max}$ | ≈ **43** | K | | Normal Boiling Point of H₂ | $T_{b,H_2}$ | **20.27** | K | > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Simon system is categorized under *Liquefaction Systems for Helium* in Module 2 and is frequently compared with the **Cascade**, **Collins (Modified Claude)**, and **Kammerlingh Onnes** systems in KTU examination questions. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition Imagine you are trying to **freeze a water droplet** in summer using only your breath. You can puff warm, humid air at it — it will never freeze. But if you first **cool the droplet in a refrigerator** (bringing it close to freezing) and then gently puff on it, the small isenthalpic expansion can push it across the phase boundary. The pre-cooling step (refrigerator) does the heavy lifting; the final puff (J-T valve) delivers the last bit of cooling. In Simon's helium liquefier: - **Liquid hydrogen bath (≈ 20 K)** plays the role of the refrigerator — it pre-cools the high-pressure helium stream below its inversion temperature. - **J–T throttle valve** plays the role of the "puff" — it delivers the final isenthalpic cooling that condenses a fraction of the stream into liquid helium. > [!TIP] > **Why not just use J-T expansion directly?** Because helium at 300 K is far **above** its maximum inversion temperature (~43 K). The J–T coefficient $\mu_{JT} = \left(\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial P}\right)_H$ is **negative** at 300 K, meaning expansion actually **heats** the gas. Pre-cooling below 43 K flips the sign, allowing expansion to cool and ultimately liquefy the gas. --- ## 1.3 System Schematic (Conceptual Block Overview) ``` [He Compressor] → [After-cooler] → [HX-1] → [LN₂ Bath] → [HX-2] → [LH₂ Bath] → [HX-3] → [J-T Valve] ↑ ↓ └────────── Un-liquefied He vapor returns through heat exchangers ──────────── [Liquid He Dewar] ``` *(Detailed Mermaid architecture is rendered in SECTION 4.)* # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operating Principle — Step-by-Step Logical Flow The Simon system operates as a **recuperative (regenerative) heat-exchange cycle** with cryogenic pre-cooling. The high-pressure helium stream is progressively cooled by three heat exchangers in series before the final expansion. **Step 1 — Compression (1 → 2):** Helium gas is drawn from the low-pressure return line and compressed isentropically (or polytropically in real machines) in a multi-stage reciprocating compressor from $P_1 \approx 1$ bar to $P_2 \approx 10$–$30$ bar. **Step 2 — Aftercooling (2 → 3):** Hot, high-pressure helium is cooled back to ambient $T_1$ in a water-cooled heat exchanger. Without this step, subsequent heat exchangers would have to absorb enormous sensible loads. **Step 3 — First Recuperative Heat Exchange (3 → 4):** The high-pressure stream is cooled by the cold, low-pressure returning helium vapor inside the first heat exchanger HX-1. **Step 4 — Liquid Nitrogen Bath Precooling (4 → 5):** The stream passes through a coil immersed in a **liquid nitrogen (LN₂) bath at 77 K**, dropping its temperature to approximately 77 K. This reduces the load on the more expensive LH₂ bath. **Step 5 — Second Recuperative Heat Exchange (5 → 6):** Further cooling by the returning vapor inside HX-2. **Step 6 — Liquid Hydrogen Bath Precooling (6 → 7):** The stream passes through a coil immersed in a **liquid hydrogen (LH₂) bath at 20.4 K**, dropping its temperature to ≈ 20 K. **This is the critical Simon innovation** — bringing helium below its inversion temperature. **Step 7 — Third Recuperative Heat Exchange (7 → 8):** Final recuperative cooling, dropping the stream further toward ≈ 14–16 K, well within the inversion region. **Step 8 — Joule–Thomson Expansion (8 → 9):** The high-pressure, low-temperature helium passes through the J–T throttle valve. The isenthalpic expansion cools the stream further, dropping it onto the two-phase dome where a fraction liquefies. **Step 9 — Phase Separation & Collection (9):** The two-phase mixture enters a collection dewar. Liquid helium (saturated liquid at 1 atm, 4.22 K) is withdrawn; the un-liquefied saturated vapor returns upward through the heat exchangers, providing the recuperative cooling for Steps 3, 5, and 7. --- ## 2.2 Why the LH₂ Bath Is Mandatory (The Thermodynamic Argument) Helium's **maximum inversion temperature** is ≈ 43 K. The Joule–Thomson coefficient is: $$\mu_{JT} = \left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial P}\right)_H = \frac{1}{C_p}\left[T\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P - V\right]$$ > For an **ideal gas**, $\mu_{JT} = 0$ identically (Joule's law). > For a **real gas**, $\mu_{JT} > 0$ **only below the inversion temperature**. For helium at 300 K and 30 bar, $\mu_{JT} \approx -0.060$ K/bar → **expansion heats** the gas by ≈ 1.7 K per 30 bar of pressure drop. **No amount of compression can liquefy helium by pure J–T expansion from room temperature** — this is the impossibility theorem that motivated the Simon design. After LH₂ pre-cooling to ≈ 20 K, $\mu_{JT} \approx +0.10$ K/bar → expansion now cools the gas, and a small pressure drop (≈ 25–30 bar) suffices to reach 4.22 K. --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **All formulas below are routinely tested in KTU Part A (3 marks) and Part B (14 marks) questions.** | # | Formula | Description / Use | |---|---|---| | 1 | $W_{ideal} = T_1 (s_1 - s_{LHe}) - (h_1 - h_{LHe})$ | Minimum (ideal) work of helium liquefaction from $(T_1, P_1)$ to saturated liquid at 1 atm. | | 2 | $y = \dfrac{h_1 - h_9}{h_1 - h_{LHe}}$ | Mass (or molar) fraction liquefied per unit mass circulated. $h_1$ = high-pressure stream enthalpy at state 1 (entering system); $h_9$ = enthalpy of returning low-pressure vapor at exit of last HX; $h_{LHe}$ = saturated liquid enthalpy at 1 atm. | | 3 | $h_2 - h_1 = C_p (T_2 - T_1)$ | Sensible heat pickup in ideal-gas recuperative HX (approx). | | 4 | $W_{comp} = \dfrac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1} R T_1 \left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} - 1\right]$ | Isentropic compression work per mole of helium (ideal gas baseline). | | 5 | $W_{actual} = \dfrac{W_{ideal}}{\eta_{carnot}} \cdot \dfrac{1}{\eta_{mech}}$ | Actual compressor work accounting for Carnot-ideal-vs-real and mechanical efficiencies. | | 6 | $\mu_{JT} = \dfrac{1}{C_p}\left[T \beta - V\right]$, where $\beta = (1/V)(\partial V/\partial T)_P$ | Joule–Thomson coefficient; sign determines cooling/heating on expansion. | | 7 | $COP = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{LHe} \cdot L_v}{W_{comp,in}}$ | Coefficient of performance; $L_v$ = latent heat of vaporization of He ≈ **20.9 kJ/kg**. | | 8 | $T_{inv,max} = \dfrac{2a}{Rb}$ | Maximum inversion temperature from van der Waals constants $a, b$. | | 9 | $h_{3'} - h_4 = (1-y)(h_{v,sat} - h_4)$ | Energy balance on the returning stream (low-pressure side) — relates liquefied fraction to enthalpies. | | 10 | $W_{actual} = \dfrac{1}{\eta_{s}} \left[ (1-y) W_{ideal} \right]$ | Actual compressor work per kg of **circulated** helium (accounts for un-liquefied fraction returning). | **Critical Material Constants for KTU Problems:** | Constant | Symbol | Value | Unit | |---|---|---|---| | Specific heat of He (ideal gas, 300 K) | $C_p$ | **5.193** | kJ/(kg·K) | | Latent heat of He at 1 atm | $L_v$ | **20.9** | kJ/kg | | Density of LHe at 4.22 K | $\rho_{LHe}$ | **124.8** | kg/m³ | | Density of He gas at STP | $\rho_{He,STP}$ | **0.1785** | kg/m³ | | Universal gas constant for He | $R_{He}$ | **2077** | J/(kg·K) | | Mass of He molecule | $M_{He}$ | **4.003** | g/mol | --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Large-scale helium liquefiers** for **MRI magnet cooling** (clinical 1.5 T and 3 T superconducting magnets). - **Particle accelerator cryogenics** (CERN LHC, Fermilab Tevatron) — though these now use the more efficient **Collins** cycle. - **Space simulation chambers** and **dilution refrigerator precooling**. - **Superconducting quantum interference devices (SQUIDs)** in research laboratories. - Historically, the Simon liquefier was the **first system to demonstrate continuous LHe production**, paving the way for the entire **superconductivity revolution** of the 1960s–80s. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Examination Directive:** All derivations below are presented exhaustively. **Do not skip algebraic steps** — KTU examiners (per the 2024 scheme marking scheme) award partial credit only for *visible intermediate work*. --- ## 3.1 Derivation — Ideal Work of Helium Liquefaction We derive the **thermodynamic minimum work** to liquefy 1 kg of helium starting from $(T_1, P_1) = (300\text{ K}, 1\text{ atm})$ and ending at the saturated-liquid state at 1 atm (4.22 K). ### Statement (KTU Board Style — "Derive the expression for the ideal work of liquefaction of helium."): For a **reversible isothermal compression** (the most efficient cooling sink — a true Carnot refrigerator), the minimum work input equals the difference between the **exergy destruction avoided** and the **enthalpy drop** of the stream: $$W_{ideal} = T_1 (s_1 - s_2) - (h_1 - h_2)$$ where subscript 1 denotes the inlet gas condition and subscript 2 denotes the saturated-liquid condition at 1 atm. **Step 1:** Identify the boundary conditions. - State 1: He gas at $T_1 = 300$ K, $P_1 = 1$ atm → saturated vapor far from saturation. - State 2: Saturated liquid He at $T_2 = 4.22$ K, $P_2 = 1$ atm. **Step 2:** Read thermodynamic property values from He tables (standard reference data). $$h_1 = 1568.0 \text{ kJ/kg}, \quad s_1 = 31.71 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$$ $$h_2 = -2.51 \text{ kJ/kg}, \quad s_2 = 4.41 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$$ *(Note: Property values use the convention that saturated liquid at 0 K has zero enthalpy/entropy. Values vary slightly with source.)* **Step 3:** Substitute into the ideal-work expression. $$\begin{aligned} W_{ideal} &= T_1 (s_1 - s_2) - (h_1 - h_2) \\ &= (300\text{ K})(31.71 - 4.41)\text{ kJ/(kg·K)} - \big[1568.0 - (-2.51)\big]\text{ kJ/kg} \\ &= (300)(27.30) - (1570.51) \\ &= 8190.0 - 1570.51 \\ &= 6619.49 \text{ kJ/kg} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 4:** Convert to kWh/kg (a more intuitive industrial unit). $$\begin{aligned} W_{ideal} &= \frac{6619.49 \text{ kJ/kg}}{3600 \text{ kJ/kWh}} \\ &= 1.839 \text{ kWh/kg} \end{aligned}$$ **Result:** $$\boxed{W_{ideal,He} \approx 6620 \text{ kJ/kg} \approx 1.84 \text{ kWh/kg}}$$ > [!TIP] > **KTU Trick Question Alert:** Some textbooks use $W_{ideal} \approx 3700$–$3900$ kJ/kg because they use a different reference state (e.g., saturated liquid at 4.22 K as zero). **Always state your reference state explicitly** in the exam. KTU prefers the **300 K, 1 atm gas → 4.22 K saturated liquid** convention as the canonical baseline. --- ## 3.2 Derivation — Mass Fraction Liquefied in the Simon System ### Setup (refer to state numbering in SECTION 4 diagram): - **State A**: Inlet to the compressor (returning vapor), $P_A = P_{low}, T_A$. - **State 1**: High-pressure stream after compression and aftercooling, $P_1 = P_{high}, T_1 = 300$ K. - **State 8**: High-pressure stream entering J–T valve, $P_1, T_8$. - **State 9**: Two-phase mixture exiting J–T valve, $P_{low}, T_{sat,He} = 4.22$ K. - **State 10**: Saturated liquid He (collected), $h_{10} = h_{LHe}$ at 1 atm. - **State 11**: Saturated vapor He (returning through HX), $h_{11} = h_{v,sat}$ at 1 atm. **Mass Balance** on the liquefaction section: $$\dot{m}_{1} = \dot{m}_{10} + \dot{m}_{11} = y\dot{m}_1 + (1-y)\dot{m}_1$$ **Energy Balance** on the J–T throttle + dewar (assuming adiabatic): $$\dot{m}_1 h_8 = \dot{m}_{10} h_{10} + \dot{m}_{11} h_{11}$$ Substituting $\dot{m}_{10} = y\dot{m}_1$ and $\dot{m}_{11} = (1-y)\dot{m}_1$: $$\begin{aligned} h_8 &= y h_{10} + (1-y) h_{11} \\ h_8 &= y h_{LHe} + (1-y) h_{v,sat} \end{aligned}$$ **Solving for $y$:** $$\begin{aligned} h_8 - h_{v,sat} &= y(h_{LHe} - h_{v,sat}) \\ y &= \frac{h_8 - h_{v,sat}}{h_{LHe} - h_{v,sat}} \end{aligned}$$ Note that $(h_{LHe} - h_{v,sat}) = -L_v$ (latent heat of vaporization, negative because $h_{v,sat} > h_{LHe}$). Therefore: $$\boxed{y = \frac{h_{v,sat} - h_8}{h_{v,sat} - h_{LHe}} = \frac{h_{v,sat} - h_8}{L_v}}$$ **Alternative form using inlet enthalpy** (treating the recuperator isothermally — common simplification): $$y = \frac{h_1 - h_9}{h_1 - h_{LHe}}$$ > This is **Formula #2 in the KTU Cheat Sheet**, frequently quoted verbatim in KTU Part B questions. --- ## 3.3 Derivation — Coefficient of Performance (COP) for the Simon System The **coefficient of performance** of a liquefaction system is defined as the ratio of the desired cooling output to the work input. **Refrigeration effect** (per kg of He circulated) achieved by the system equals the enthalpy difference that the returning low-pressure stream can absorb in the recuperative heat exchangers plus the heat absorbed at the LN₂ and LH₂ baths: $$q_{cold} = y \cdot L_v + (h_1 - h_8)$$ **Work input** (per kg circulated) is the compressor work (assuming isentropic): $$w_{comp} = \frac{\gamma}{\gamma-1} R T_A \left[\left(\frac{P_1}{P_A}\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} - 1\right]$$ For helium ($\gamma = 5/3$, $R = 2077$ J/(kg·K), $P_1/P_A = 30$): $$\begin{aligned} w_{comp} &= \frac{5/3}{5/3 - 1} (2077)(300) \left[(30)^{0.4} - 1\right] \\ &= 2.5 \times 623100 \times [3.213 - 1] \\ &= 1557750 \times 2.213 \\ &= 3.448 \times 10^6 \text{ J/kg} \\ &= 3448 \text{ kJ/kg circulated} \end{aligned}$$ **COP expression:** $$\boxed{COP = \frac{y \cdot L_v + (h_1 - h_8)}{w_{comp}}}$$ For a representative Simon system with $y \approx 0.05$–$0.10$, the COP is typically **0.002–0.005**, reflecting the enormous thermodynamic difficulty of helium liquefaction. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Simon vs. Collins vs. Cascade) | Parameter | **Simon System** | **Cascade System** | **Collins (Modified Claude)** | |---|---|---|---| | Number of expansion engines | 0 (uses J–T only) | 0 (uses J–T only) | 2–5 expansion engines + 1 J–T | | External precoolant | **LH₂ + LN₂** | None (uses intermediate fluids: N₂, Ne, H₂) | LN₂ only | | First demonstrated | **1932** | 1930s (Kammerlingh Onnes 1908) | 1946 | | Typical yield $y$ | 5–10% | ~2–4% | 20–35% | | LHe throughput (modern) | < 5 L/h | < 10 L/h | 50–500 L/h | | Ideal-vs-actual efficiency | Poor (~1.5%) | Very poor (~1%) | Good (~25–30%) | | Operational complexity | **Low** | High (3 fluids, 3 circuits) | Moderate (but needs turbine maintenance) | | Use today | **Historical / Small lab** | Largely obsolete | **Industry standard for MRI / accelerators** | | Cooling limit | ~4.2 K | ~4.2 K | ~3.5–4.2 K (with further precooling) | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Simon Helium Liquefaction System — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow ```mermaid flowchart TB A[Helium Compressor
P_low to P_high
1 bar to 30 bar] --> B[Aftercooler
Water-cooled
T returns to 300 K] B --> C[HX1 First Recuperator
Recovers cold from return gas] C --> D[Liquid Nitrogen Bath
77 K Precool] D --> E[HX2 Second Recuperator
Further recuperation] E --> F[Liquid Hydrogen Bath
20 K Precool
KEY SIMON INNOVATION] F --> G[HX3 Final Recuperator
Drops T to 14 to 16 K] G --> H[Joule-Thomson Throttle Valve
Isenthalpic Expansion] H --> I[Phase Separator Dewar
Liquid Helium Collected at 4.22 K] I --> J[Saturated Vapor Exit
He vapor at 4.22 K] J --> K[Gas Return Manifold
Back through all HXs] K --> A L[Liquid He Withdrawal
For external cryogenic use] -.-> I classDef cryo fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0a58ca,color:#000 classDef room fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#b02a37,color:#000 classDef pre fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,color:#000 classDef collect fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#146c43,color:#000 class A,B room class C,E,G,K cryo class D,F pre class H,I,J,L collect ``` ## 4.2 Thermodynamic State Points on the T-s Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph "T-s Diagram Annotation" ST1[State 1: He gas
300 K, 30 bar
After compression + aftercooling] ST2[State 3: After HX1
~140 K, 30 bar] ST3[State 5: After LN2 bath
77 K, 30 bar] ST4[State 7: After HX2 and LH2 bath
~20 K, 30 bar] ST5[State 8: After HX3
14 K, 30 bar] ST6[State 9: After J-T valve
4.22 K, 1 bar
Two-phase mixture] ST7[State 10: Saturated liquid
4.22 K, 1 atm
LHe collected] ST8[State 11: Saturated vapor
4.22 K, 1 atm
Returning gas] ST1 --> ST2 --> ST3 --> ST4 --> ST5 --> ST6 ST6 --> ST7 ST6 --> ST8 classDef state fill:#e7f1ff,stroke:#0a58ca,color:#000 class ST1,ST2,ST3,ST4,ST5,ST6,ST7,ST8 state end ``` ## 4.3 Process Path Description (Aligned with Mermaid Blocks) | State | Location in Diagram | $T$ (K) | $P$ (bar) | Phase | Description | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | After aftercooler | 300 | 30 | Gas | Compressed, water-cooled | | 3 | Exit of HX-1 | ≈ 140 | 30 | Gas | Recuperatively cooled | | 5 | Exit of LN₂ bath | 77 | 30 | Gas | LN₂ bath cooled | | 7 | Exit of LH₂ bath | ≈ 20 | 30 | Gas | **Below inversion T** | | 8 | Exit of HX-3 | ≈ 14 | 30 | Gas | Final recuperative cooling | | 9 | Exit of J-T valve | 4.22 | 1 | Two-phase | Partial liquefaction | | 10 | Bottom of dewar | 4.22 | 1 | **Saturated liquid** | LHe collected | | 11 | Top of dewar | 4.22 | 1 | Saturated vapor | Returning to compressor inlet | --- ## 4.4 Energy-Flow Schematic ```mermaid flowchart TB W[Compressor Work W_comp
~3448 kJ/kg circulated] --> SYS QLN2[Heat Rejected to LN2 Bath
~250 kJ/kg] --> SYS QLH2[Heat Rejected to LH2 Bath
~180 kJ/kg] --> SYS SYS[Simon Helium Liquefaction System
Net Cooling Effect] SYS --> OUT1[Liquid Helium Output
y x m_circ dots L_v = q_collected] SYS --> OUT2[Unliquefied Vapor Return
Provides recuperative cooling] classDef input fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,color:#000 classDef system fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0a58ca,color:#000 classDef output fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#146c43,color:#000 class W,QLN2,QLH2 input class SYS system class OUT1,OUT2 output ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- ### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024] **State the necessity of liquid hydrogen pre-cooling in the Simon helium liquefaction system. Why cannot helium be liquefied directly by Joule–Thomson expansion from ambient conditions?** **Model Answer (3 Marks — KTU marking key):** **1 mark — Identify the inversion-temperature constraint:** The maximum inversion temperature of helium is approximately **43 K**. Since 300 K (ambient) > 43 K, the Joule–Thomson coefficient $\mu_{JT} < 0$ at ambient conditions, meaning **J–T expansion heats helium rather than cooling it**. **1 mark — Explain the LH₂ bath role:** The LH₂ bath at 20.4 K cools the high-pressure helium stream to ≈ 20 K, **well below the inversion temperature**, so that the subsequent J–T expansion produces a **positive cooling effect**. **1 mark — Conclude with thermodynamic statement:** This pre-cooling below the inversion curve is **thermodynamically mandatory** — no amount of compression at ambient temperature can liquefy helium by pure isenthalpic expansion. The Simon system therefore requires an external cryogen (LH₂), in addition to LN₂. --- ### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **Define the *coefficient of performance (COP)* of a helium liquefaction system. Mention its typical range for a Simon-type system.** **Model Answer (3 Marks — KTU marking key):** **1 mark — Definition:** The COP of a liquefaction system is the ratio of the **useful refrigeration effect** (heat absorbed at the cold temperature) to the **net work input** to the compressor: $$COP = \frac{q_{cold}}{w_{comp,in}} = \frac{y \cdot L_v + (h_1 - h_8)}{w_{comp,in}}$$ **1 mark — Identify the components:** - $y \cdot L_v$ → latent heat absorbed in producing liquid He fraction. - $(h_1 - h_8)$ → sensible heat absorbed in pre-cooling the high-pressure stream. - $w_{comp,in}$ → compressor work input. **1 mark — Typical numerical range:** For Simon-type systems, **COP ≈ 0.002 – 0.005**, reflecting the extreme thermodynamic difficulty of helium liquefaction. By contrast, a domestic refrigerator has COP ≈ 3–4. --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A.** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024] **(a)** With the aid of a **schematic flow diagram**, describe the **Simon Helium Liquefaction System**. Clearly mark all state points, heat exchangers, and cryogenic baths. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A Simon helium liquefier operates between a high pressure of **30 bar** and a low pressure of **1.2 bar**. The enthalpy of the high-pressure stream at the inlet of the J–T valve is $h_8 = -13.5$ kJ/kg. The saturated-liquid and saturated-vapor enthalpies of helium at 1.2 bar are $h_{LHe} = -19.8$ kJ/kg and $h_{v,sat} = 23.4$ kJ/kg, respectively. Calculate: (i) the **mass fraction liquefied** $y$ per kg of He circulated, and **(3 Marks)** (ii) the **mass of liquid helium produced per hour** if the compressor circulates **120 kg/hr** of helium. **(4 Marks)** --- ### **Model Solution — Question A (a) (7 Marks)** **1 mark — Block identification:** The Simon system comprises: helium compressor, aftercooler, three recuperative heat exchangers (HX-1, HX-2, HX-3), LN₂ bath, LH₂ bath, J–T throttle valve, and liquid-helium collection dewar. **2 marks — Working-fluid cycle description:** Helium gas is compressed from 1.2 bar to 30 bar, after-cooled to 300 K with water, then progressively cooled in HX-1, the LN₂ bath, HX-2, the LH₂ bath (the Simon innovation), and HX-3 before passing through the J–T valve. After expansion, the two-phase mixture separates in the dewar at 4.22 K. Saturated liquid He is withdrawn; saturated vapor returns through all three HXs to provide recuperative cooling. **2 marks — Justification for LH₂ bath:** Helium's maximum inversion temperature ≈ 43 K. Without LH₂ pre-cooling, the J–T expansion from 30 bar would heat the gas, not cool it. The LH₂ bath at 20 K brings the stream into the positive-$\mu_{JT}$ region where expansion yields cooling. **2 marks — Schematic (refer to SECTION 4 Mermaid block):** Mark state points 1 (after aftercooler), 3, 5, 7, 8 (HX exits), 9 (post-JT), 10 (LHe), 11 (returning vapor). Label pressures, temperatures, and the two cryogenic baths. --- ### **Model Solution — Question A (b) (i) (3 Marks)** Use the liquefaction fraction formula derived in SECTION 3.2: $$\begin{aligned} y &= \frac{h_{v,sat} - h_8}{h_{v,sat} - h_{LHe}} \\ &= \frac{23.4 - (-13.5)}{23.4 - (-19.8)} \\ &= \frac{23.4 + 13.5}{23.4 + 19.8} \\ &= \frac{36.9}{43.2} \\ &= 0.8542 \\ \end{aligned}$$ > ⚠️ **Sanity check:** $y$ cannot exceed 1; here $y = 0.854$ is **physically impossible** because mass conservation in a real Simon liquefier typically yields $y \approx 0.05$–$0.10$. The unrealistically high value indicates that **the given enthalpy values are illustrative, not physical** (sign convention mismatch or a hypothetical textbook problem). In KTU board exams, **report the calculated value** but **flag the anomaly** for the examiner. **Answer: $y \approx 0.854$ (or 85.4%) — note physical implausibility.** --- ### **Model Solution — Question A (b) (ii) (4 Marks)** $$\begin{aligned} \dot{m}_{LHe} &= y \times \dot{m}_{circ} \\ &= 0.854 \times 120 \text{ kg/hr} \\ &= 102.5 \text{ kg/hr} \end{aligned}$$ **Answer:** $\dot{m}_{LHe} \approx 102.5$ kg/hr (physical reality: ~6–12 kg/hr for a Simon system of this size). --- ### **Question B.** [KTU University Exam — June 2023 — Internal Choice Alternative] **(a)** Derive the expression for the **minimum (ideal) work of liquefaction** of helium, starting from $(T_1, P_1) = (300\text{ K}, 1\text{ atm})$ to saturated liquid at 1 atm. Comment on why the actual work in a Simon system is much greater than the ideal work. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A Simon liquefier is fed helium at 1 bar, 300 K. The high-pressure stream at 30 bar is pre-cooled to **18 K** by the LH₂ bath before entering the J–T valve. The returning vapor at 1 bar leaves the lowest heat exchanger at **15 K**. Estimate: (i) the **ideal work of liquefaction**, and **(3 Marks)** (ii) the **actual COP** of the system if the compressor isentropic efficiency is **60%**. **(4 Marks)** Use: $h_{He}(300\text{ K}, 1\text{ atm}) = 1568$ kJ/kg; $s_{He}(300\text{ K}, 1\text{ atm}) = 31.71$ kJ/(kg·K); $h_{LHe}(4.22\text{ K}) = -2.51$ kJ/kg; $s_{LHe}(4.22\text{ K}) = 4.41$ kJ/(kg·K); $C_p = 5.193$ kJ/(kg·K); $\gamma = 5/3$; $R = 2.077$ kJ/(kg·K). --- ### **Model Solution — Question B (a) (7 Marks)** **2 marks — Derivation setup:** For a reversible isothermal compression (best possible cooling-sink assumption), the minimum work to liquefy 1 kg of He is: $$W_{ideal} = T_1 (s_1 - s_2) - (h_1 - h_2)$$ where 1 = inlet gas (300 K, 1 atm) and 2 = saturated liquid (4.22 K, 1 atm). **2 marks — Numerical substitution:** $$\begin{aligned} W_{ideal} &= 300 \times (31.71 - 4.41) - [1568.0 - (-2.51)] \\ &= 300 \times 27.30 - 1570.51 \\ &= 8190.0 - 1570.51 \\ &= 6619.5 \text{ kJ/kg} \end{aligned}$$ **1 mark — Convert to kWh/kg:** $$W_{ideal} = 6619.5 / 3600 \approx 1.839 \text{ kWh/kg}$$ **2 marks — Why actual ≫ ideal:** The actual work in a Simon system (typically 600–1500 kWh/kg) far exceeds the ideal work due to: 1. **Compressor irreversibilities** (non-isentropic compression, mechanical friction). 2. **Heat-exchanger losses** (finite temperature-difference LMTD, stream-to-stream non-equilibrium). 3. **Cryogen production losses** — the LH₂ and LN₂ baths themselves require liquefaction cycles with their own inefficiencies. 4. **Un-liquefied stream return** — only the fraction $y$ becomes liquid; the rest must be recompressed, multiplying work. 5. **Insulation losses** — parasitic heat ingress from the 300 K environment through imperfect vacuum jackets. --- ### **Model Solution — Question B (b) (i) (3 Marks)** Using the ideal-work formula with property values provided: $$\boxed{W_{ideal} = 6619.5 \text{ kJ/kg} \approx 6.62 \text{ MJ/kg} \approx 1.84 \text{ kWh/kg}}$$ --- ### **Model Solution — Question B (b) (ii) (4 Marks)** **Step 1:** Compressor isentropic work (per kg circulated): $$\begin{aligned} w_{comp,s} &= \frac{\gamma}{\gamma-1} R T_1 \left[\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} - 1\right] \\ &= \frac{5/3}{2/3} (2.077)(300) \left[(30)^{0.4} - 1\right] \\ &= 2.5 \times 623.1 \times [3.213 - 1] \\ &= 2.5 \times 623.1 \times 2.213 \\ &= 3448 \text{ kJ/kg circulated} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 2:** Actual compressor work (60% isentropic efficiency): $$w_{comp,act} = \frac{w_{comp,s}}{\eta_s} = \frac{3448}{0.60} = 5747 \text{ kJ/kg circulated}$$ **Step 3:** Liquefied fraction from HX-3 energy balance: $$\begin{aligned} y &= \frac{h_1 - h_9}{h_1 - h_{LHe}} \\ &\approx \frac{C_p (T_1 - T_9)}{C_p (T_1 - T_{LHe})} \end{aligned}$$ Approximating using sensible heats between inlet gas and returning vapor / liquid states: $y \approx \frac{5.193 \times (300 - 15)}{5.193 \times (300 - 4.22) + 20.9} \approx \frac{1480}{1537 + 20.9} \approx 0.95$ (But this is again the illustrative-textbook convention; for a real Simon system $y \approx 0.08$–$0.10$.) **Step 4:** Compute COP (using textbook illustrative $y$ and including practical yield $y_{real} = 0.08$): $$\begin{aligned} q_{cold} &= y_{real} \cdot L_v + (h_1 - h_8) \\ &\approx 0.08 \times 20.9 + 5.193 \times (300 - 18) \\ &\approx 1.67 + 1464.4 \\ &\approx 1466.1 \text{ kJ/kg circulated} \end{aligned}$$ $$\begin{aligned} COP &= \frac{q_{cold}}{w_{comp,act}} = \frac{1466.1}{5747} \\ &\approx 0.255 \end{aligned}$$ $$\boxed{COP \approx 0.26 \text{ (dimensionless)}}$$ **In percentage form:** COP ≈ 26% of Carnot-ideal — but if referred to the **absolute thermodynamic minimum** (1.84 kWh/kg vs ~150 kWh/kg actual), the **fractional second-law efficiency** is only ≈ **1.2%**, which is characteristic of helium liquefiers. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls** > 1. **Forgetting the LH₂ bath** in the schematic — examiners deduct **2 marks** if you draw only LN₂ precooling. > 2. **Using $y = \dfrac{h_1 - h_9}{h_1 - h_{LHe}}$** without specifying state points clearly — always label **state 1 = compressor inlet (returning vapor condition)** vs **state 1 = after aftercooling (high-pressure)**, as these are different in different textbook conventions. KTU's 2024 marking scheme deducts **1 mark** for this ambiguity. > 3. **Sign errors in $h_{LHe}$**: Many property tables report $h_{LHe} \approx -2.5$ kJ/kg (relative to ideal gas at 0 K) while others report $+2.5$ kJ/kg (relative to saturated liquid at 0 K). Use a consistent reference and **state it explicitly** in your solution. > 4. **Mistaking the Simon system for the Cascade system**: Cascade uses three separate fluids (N₂, Ne, H₂); Simon uses only LH₂ + LN₂ as **external precoolants**, not as working fluids. > 5. **Forgetting the inversion-temperature justification** in Part A — examiners explicitly test this because it underpins the entire Simon design philosophy. --- ## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST > [!TIP] > **Last-minute revision sheet for KTU Module 2 — print and pin to your wall.** - ☐ Simon's liquefier = **liquid hydrogen bath + J–T expansion only**, no expansion engine. - ☐ Conceived by **Franz Simon, Oxford, 1932** — first practical single-cryogen He liquefier. - ☐ Helium's **maximum inversion temperature ≈ 43 K** → must pre-cool below this **before** any J–T expansion. - ☐ Two cryogenic baths: **LN₂ (77 K) for bulk precooling** + **LH₂ (20 K) for below-inversion precooling**. - ☐ Operating pressures: typically **high side 10–30 bar**, **low side 1.0–1.5 bar**. - ☐ State-point sequence (Simon system): **1→3 (HX1)→5 (LN₂)→7 (HX2→LH₂)→8 (HX3)→9 (J–T)→10 (LHe) + 11 (vapor return)**. - ☐ **Liquefaction fraction formula:** $y = \dfrac{h_{v,sat} - h_8}{h_{v,sat} - h_{LHe}}$. - ☐ **Ideal work of He liquefaction:** $W_{ideal} = T_1(s_1 - s_2) - (h_1 - h_2) \approx 6.62 \text{ MJ/kg} \approx 1.84 \text{ kWh/kg}$. - ☐ **Actual work** in Simon systems is **100–800 × greater** than ideal due to irreversibility and cryogen production losses. - ☐ **COP** of Simon system is very low (≈ 0.002–0.005 in absolute terms; ≈ 1–3% of Carnot equivalent). - ☐ Modern helium liquefaction has shifted to the **Collins (Modified Claude) cycle** for high-throughput MRI / accelerator use. - ☐ Latent heat of vaporization of He at 1 atm: $L_v \approx$ **20.9 kJ/kg**. - ☐ For KTU Part A: be ready to draw the **block schematic** AND define **inversion temperature** + **J–T coefficient sign**. - ☐ For KTU Part B: be ready to derive the **ideal work expression**, compute **liquefaction fraction**, and discuss **why LH₂ pre-cooling is mandatory**.

topics.title: Collins Helium Liquefaction System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET861 - CRYOGENIC ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Collins Helium Liquefaction System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Collins Helium Liquefaction System:** A modified **Claude cycle** based cryogenic liquefaction plant specifically engineered for the continuous production of liquid helium (LHe) at 4.2 K. It was designed and developed by **Samuel C. Collins** at the Massachusetts Institute of Technology (MIT) in **1946**. The system integrates a **work-producing expansion engine** (isentropic device) with a **Joule–Thomson (J–T) expansion valve** (isenthalpic device), operating between two pressure streams — a **High-Pressure (HP) stream** and a **Low-Pressure (LP) return stream** — interconnected through a cascade of **counter-flow heat exchangers**.

Unlike the simple **Linde–Hampson system** (which uses only throttling), the Collins system recovers a portion of the refrigeration by expanding part of the compressed gas through an **expansion engine**, which produces **external work** and yields significantly greater cooling per unit of compression work. This makes it the most efficient practical system for helium liquefaction below 10 K, where the inversion temperature of helium (**≈ 40–50 K**) makes pure J–T expansion ineffective without precooling.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Two-Person Air-Cooling Team:**
> Imagine a hot room that you need to cool down using a single window fan. If you only have **plain expansion valves** (like a Linde system), each breath of air gets "throttled" but produces very little cooling, and you must work hard to compress it back.
>
> Now, instead, you recruit **two helpers**: 
> - **Helper A** pushes half the cooled air through a **turbine/fan** that **spins and gives back energy** (this is the expansion engine).
> - **Helper B** sends the other half through a **narrow nozzle** that cools it further by expansion (this is the J–T valve).
>
> The turbine's recovered work assists the next compression cycle, drastically lowering overall energy consumption. This cooperative, two-stream design is exactly what Collins envisioned for liquefying the most stubborn gas — **helium**.

### 1.3 Standard Operating Metrics (KTU Standard Values)

| Parameter | Typical Value (Collins System) |
|---|---|
| High pressure (HP) | **14 – 16 atm** |
| Low pressure (LP) | **1 atm** |
| Pressure ratio (HP/LP) | **≈ 14 – 16** |
| Number of heat exchangers | **3 (triple HX)** |
| Expansion device | **Reciprocating engine / Turbine** |
| Precooling | **Liquid nitrogen bath (77 K)** — optional |
| Liquefaction temperature | **4.2 K (boiling point of ⁴He)** |
| Original design capacity | **≈ 5 L/hr** (1946 MIT unit) |

### 1.4 Why Helium Demands a Special System?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Helium's Unique Thermodynamic Challenge:**
> - The **Joule–Thomson inversion temperature** of helium is approximately **40–51 K** (depending on pressure).
> - This means J–T expansion alone produces **no cooling above ≈ 40 K**, and even **minimal cooling below 40 K**.
> - Therefore, the gas must be **precooled to below 40 K** before throttling.
> - In a Collins system, this precooling is generated **internally** by the expansion engine — no external liquid nitrogen (LN₂) bath is mandatory (though LN₂ precooling improves efficiency).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Working Principle — Stepwise Operational Logic

The Collins liquefier operates as a **continuous, closed-loop, two-pressure regenerative refrigerator**. The thermodynamic cycle may be broken down as follows:

**Step 1 — Compression (Point 1 → 2):**
Helium gas is drawn from the low-pressure (LP) return line and compressed in a **multi-stage reciprocating compressor** with interstage cooling. The discharge pressure is the **high pressure (HP)** ≈ 14–16 atm.

**Step 2 — Aftercooling (Point 2 → 3):**
The hot HP gas passes through a water-cooled **aftercooler**, rejecting heat to the surroundings at nearly ambient temperature (≈ 300 K).

**Step 3 — First Heat Exchanger (HX-1, Point 3 → 4):**
The HP gas is cooled by the returning LP stream. The HP gas enters at 300 K and exits at an intermediate temperature (≈ 85–90 K in classical Collins; or ~ 200 K without LN₂ bath).

**Step 4 — Second Heat Exchanger (HX-2, Point 4 → 5):**
Further cooling occurs between the HP and LP streams. The HP gas leaves HX-2 at a much lower temperature (≈ 17–20 K).

**Step 5 — HP Stream Splitting (at Point 5):**
At this stage, the high-pressure stream divides into **two parallel sub-streams**:
- **Stream E** → through the **expansion engine** (mass fraction *x*)
- **Stream J** → continues through **HX-3** to the **J–T valve** (mass fraction *(1 − x)*)

**Step 6 — Expansion Engine (Point 5 → 6):**
Stream E undergoes **isentropic (nearly) expansion** from HP to LP. The work output is recovered and either used to assist the compressor or dissipated. The temperature drops dramatically (e.g., 20 K → 8 K).

**Step 7 — Third Heat Exchanger (HX-3, Stream J: Point 5 → 7):**
Stream J is further precooled by the cold LP return from the engine exhaust, reaching ≈ 6–10 K before throttling.

**Step 8 — J–T Throttle (Point 7 → 8):**
Stream J is expanded through the J–T valve from HP to LP. This **isenthalpic** drop produces the final cooling that yields **liquid helium** at saturation (≈ 4.2 K).

**Step 9 — Phase Separation & Collection:**
The two-phase mixture (mostly liquid + some vapor) enters a **phase separator**. Liquid helium (4.2 K) is withdrawn as product. The saturated vapor joins the LP stream.

**Step 10 — LP Counter-Flow Return:**
The combined LP stream flows back through HX-3 → HX-2 → HX-1, progressively warming up and finally reaching the compressor inlet (≈ 300 K, point 1).

### 2.2 The Critical 'Why' Behind Each Component

| Component | Purpose | What Happens If Removed |
|---|---|---|
| **Multi-stage compressor with intercooling** | Reduces work of compression toward isothermal ideal | Work input rises by 30–60 % |
| **Aftercooler** | Removes heat of compression at T_max | Reduces coefficient of performance |
| **Expansion engine** | Recovers ~ 20–30 % of refrigeration | Carnot factor for He drops drastically |
| **J–T valve** | Final liquefaction step | Cannot cross the 4.2 K isotherm |
| **Three heat exchangers** | Regenerative heat transfer between HP and LP | Self-cooling fails; external cooling needed |
| **Phase separator** | Distinguishes liquid product from vapor | Cannot collect liquid helium continuously |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **exam-critical**. Memorize the symbols, units, and the regime (isothermal, isentropic, isenthalpic) each formula applies to.

| # | Formula | Meaning / Variables | Regime |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $W_{iso} = n R T_1 \ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)$ | Minimum (ideal) isothermal compression work | Reversible, isothermal |
| 2 | $W_{s} = \dfrac{n R T_1 \, \gamma}{\gamma - 1}\!\left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\tfrac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma}} - 1\right]$ | Ideal isentropic compression work | Reversible, adiabatic |
