Because no moving parts exist inside the cold space, vibration is dramatically reduced and reliability improves. **Key Components (Mandatory KTU Terminology):** - **Compressor Module** (same as G-M) - **Rotary Valve** (provides pressure oscillations) - **Regenerator** (identical concept to G-M) - **Pulse Tube** (closed, hollow cylinder) - **Orifice / Inertance Tube / Double-Inlet Valve** (phase-shifting element) - **Reservoir / Buffer Volume** (large hot-end volume, ≫ pulse tube volume) - **Hot-End Heat Exchanger** (rejects heat at ambient) > [!NOTE] > **Standard Working Fluid:** Ultra-high-purity **Helium-4 (⁴He)** at charge pressures of **20–30 bar**, with a pressure oscillation amplitude of typically **2–4 bar** per cycle. ### 1.3 Historical Context for Board Exam | Year | Milestone | |------|-----------| | 1959 | Gifford & McMahon publish their regenerator-based refrigerator | | 1964 | Mikulin, Losev, Symak publish first *Basic Pulse Tube* (BPT) | | 1983 | Radebaugh introduces *Orifice Pulse Tube* (OPT) | | 1986 | Zhu, Wu & Chen propose *Double-Inlet Pulse Tube* | | 1990s | Multi-stage PTCs reach **4 K** | | 2000s | Three-stage PTCs reach **1.5 K** (with magnetic regenerator or dilution stage) | | Present | Pulse Tubes dominate space-borne and vibration-sensitive cryogenics | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operating Principle of the G-M Refrigerator The cold head operates on a **two-step switching cycle** controlled by the rotary valve, while the displacer is driven by a separate motor (often synchronised to the valve). **Step-by-Step Logic:** 1. **High-Pressure Charge Phase (Process A → B):** Rotary valve opens to the *high-pressure* manifold. Compressed helium is pushed into the cold head. The displacer is near the **TOP** (warm end). Gas flows through the regenerator, *heating up* as it absorbs heat stored in the matrix, and enters the expansion space. 2. **Displacer Down Stroke (Process B → C):** The valve is *closed* (or held at high pressure). The displacer moves *downward*, compressing the gas trapped in the expansion space and forcing it *back* through the regenerator (where it deposits heat). Meanwhile, *new* high-pressure gas enters the volume *above* the displacer. 3. **Low-Pressure Vent Phase (Process C → D):** Rotary valve opens to the *low-pressure* manifold. Gas above the displacer is vented out, pulled through the regenerator (where it is *pre-cooled* by the matrix), and finally exhausted. 4. **Displacer Up Stroke (Process D → A):** Displacer moves *upward*. The cold, low-pressure gas remaining *below* the displacer expands further → **refrigeration effect at the cold finger**. > [!NOTE] > **Why refrigeration occurs:** Expansion of gas inside the cold space, combined with the regenerator's pre-cooling effect, allows each successive cycle to operate at a lower temperature than the previous one (a "temperature cascade"). Steady-state is reached when heat leak equals the refrigeration effect. ### 2.2 Operating Principle of the Pulse Tube Cryocooler The pulse tube operates on **two complementary physical mechanisms**: **Mechanism A — Surface Heat Pumping (Enthalpy Streaming):** - During the *high-pressure* half-cycle, gas is compressed everywhere, but the wall acts as a distributed heat exchanger. Near the cold end, gas is *cooled* by the cold wall; near the hot end, gas *deposits* heat. - During the *low-pressure* half-cycle, gas near the *cold* end expands and absorbs heat from the cold finger. **Mechanism B — Enthalpy Flow (Mass-Flow Phase Shift):** - The orifice (or inertance) at the warm end creates a *phase lag* between pressure oscillation and mass flow oscillation. - This phase shift causes a *DC (rectified) flow of enthalpy* from the cold end toward the hot end — analogous to a *one-way heat pump* embedded in the tube. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Formula Table for Board Exam — Memorise Every Row** | # | Formula / Quantity | Expression | Units / Notes | |---|--------------------|-----------|---------------| | 1 | Carnot COP | $\text{COP}_{Carnot} = \dfrac{T_C}{T_H - T_C}$ | dimensionless; $T_C$, $T_H$ in **K** | | 2 | Refrigeration effect | $Q_L = \dot{m}\, c_p\, (T_{cold\,out} - T_{cold\,in})$ | **W (J/s)** | | 3 | Compressor input power | $W_{net} = \dot{m}\, w_{net}$ | **W** | | 4 | Actual COP | $\text{COP}_{actual} = \dfrac{Q_L}{W_{net}}$ | dimensionless | | 5 | FOM (Figure of Merit) | $\text{FOM} = \dfrac{\text{COP}_{actual}}{\text{COP}_{Carnot}}$ | dimensionless (0.05 – 0.15 typical) | | 6 | Pressure ratio | $\pi = \dfrac{p_H}{p_L}$ | dimensionless (2 – 4 typical) | | 7 | Pressure amplitude | $p_a = \dfrac{p_H - p_L}{2}$ | **Pa** or **bar** | | 8 | Mean pressure | $p_m = \dfrac{p_H + p_L}{2}$ | **Pa** | | 9 | Enthalpy flow rate in PTC | $\dot{H} = \dot{m}\, h$ | **W** | | 10 | Pulse tube cold capacity (scaling) | $\dot{Q}_L \propto p_a^2\, p_m\, V_{PT}\, f$ | proportional law | | 11 | Surface heat flux in PT | $\dot{q}_{surf} = \alpha\, A\, \Delta T$ | **W** | | 12 | Regenerator effectiveness | $\varepsilon_R = \dfrac{T_{out} - T_{in}}{\overline{T}_{matrix} - T_{in}}$ | dimensionless (0.90 – 0.99) | | 13 | Loss per stage (no-load temp) | $T_{stage} \approx T_{H}\left(\dfrac{1 - \pi^{(1-\gamma)/\gamma}}{1 + \pi^{(1-\gamma)/\gamma}}\right)$ | **K** (ideal GM) | | 14 | Cold capacity @ 80 K (1-stage) | 5 – 50 W (small), 100 – 1000 W (industrial) | **W** | | 15 | Cold capacity @ 4.2 K (2-stage) | 0.5 – 5 W (lab), 10 – 50 W (MRI HTS) | **W** | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Notation:** Always express temperatures in **Kelvin** when using the Carnot COP formula. A common student error is using °C, leading to a 273-unit mismatch in the denominator. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility | Domain | Application | |--------|-------------| | **Medical** | MRI (magnet cooling), cryosurgery, cryopreservation of biological tissue | | **Defence / Space** | Infrared sensors on satellites, missile guidance (HgCdTe detectors @ 77 K) | | **Scientific** | Particle detectors (LHC, dark-matter experiments), quantum computing dilution refrigerators | | **Industrial** | LNG re-liquefaction of boil-off, superconducting power transmission (loss-free HTS cables) | | **Electronics** | Low-noise amplifiers (LNAs), SQUIDs, low-temperature physics | ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Net Work Done in an Ideal G-M Cycle The G-M cycle on a **P–V diagram** consists of four processes: - **Process 1 → 2:** Isobaric heating at low pressure $p_L$ (gas enters regenerator → cools → enters expansion space) - **Process 2 → 3:** Isochoric pressure rise (rotary valve switches to high pressure $p_H$) - **Process 3 → 4:** Isobaric expansion at high pressure $p_H$ (gas in expansion space is cooled by cold finger — **refrigeration**) - **Process 4 → 1:** Isochoric pressure drop (rotary valve switches back to low pressure $p_L$) Let: - $V_E$ = expansion space volume (cold space) - $V_D$ = displacement swept by displacer - $\gamma$ = ratio of specific heats for helium ($\gamma = 5/3$) **Step 1 — Energy balance on the expansion space (refrigeration effect):** During Process 3 → 4, the gas expands at constant high pressure $p_H$ inside the cold finger, absorbing heat from the cold load: $$ Q_L = m\, c_p\, (T_{cold\,out} - T_{cold\,in}) $$ For an ideal gas undergoing isobaric expansion: $$ Q_L = n\, \bar{c}_p\, (T_3 - T_4) $$ **Step 2 — Mass of gas taking part in one cycle:** The mass transferred per cycle equals the gas swept by the displacer: $$ m_{cycle} = \dfrac{p_H V_E - p_L V_E}{R_u T} = \dfrac{(p_H - p_L) V_E}{R_u T_{mean}} $$ **Step 3 — Net work done per cycle (enclosed area of the P-V diagram):** The net work equals the area enclosed by the cycle: $$ W_{net} = \oint p\, dV $$ For a rectangle-like cycle in P-V space with sides $p_H$, $p_L$, and the swept volume $V_D$: $$ W_{net} = (p_H - p_L)\, V_D $$ **Step 4 — Coefficient of Performance of the G-M refrigerator:** $$ \text{COP}_{GM} = \dfrac{Q_L}{W_{net}} = \dfrac{m_{cycle}\, c_p\, \Delta T}{(p_H - p_L)\, V_D} $$ Substituting $m_{cycle}$ from Step 2: $$ \text{COP}_{GM} = \dfrac{(p_H - p_L)\, V_E}{R_u T_{mean}}\cdot \dfrac{c_p\, \Delta T}{(p_H - p_L)\, V_D} $$ Cancel $(p_H - p_L)$ and use $c_p = \dfrac{\gamma R_u}{\gamma - 1}$ for an ideal gas: $$ \text{COP}_{GM} = \dfrac{V_E}{V_D}\cdot \dfrac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1}\cdot \dfrac{\Delta T}{T_{mean}} $$ **Step 5 — Final compact form:** $$ \boxed{\;\text{COP}_{GM} = \dfrac{V_E}{V_D}\cdot \dfrac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1}\cdot \dfrac{T_C}{T_H}\;\text{(ideal, isothermal limit)}\;} $$ **Numerical sanity check:** For helium ($\gamma = 5/3$), $V_E/V_D \approx 0.5$, $T_C = 4\,K$, $T_H = 300\,K$: $$ \text{COP}_{Carnot} = \dfrac{4}{296} \approx 0.0135 $$ $$ \text{COP}_{GM,ideal} = 0.5 \times 2.5 \times 0.0135 \approx 0.0169 $$ Real G-M systems achieve **FOM = 0.05 – 0.10** of Carnot, i.e., COP $\approx 0.0007$ at 4 K. ### 3.2 Derivation: Enthalpy Flow Mechanism in a Pulse Tube Consider a control volume inside the pulse tube. The gas oscillates back and forth. Let: - $\dot{m}_c$ = DC component of mass flow (rectified, due to phase lag) - $\Delta h$ = enthalpy difference between cold-end and hot-end states of the gas **Step 1 — Instantaneous enthalpy transport:** The instantaneous enthalpy carried by the oscillating gas through any cross-section $A$: $$ \dot{H}(t) = \dot{m}(t)\, h(t) = \rho(t)\, u(t)\, A\, h(t) $$ **Step 2 — Time-averaging over one cycle:** The DC component (averaged over one cycle $T$): $$ \langle \dot{H} \rangle = \dfrac{1}{T}\int_0^{T} \dot{m}(t)\, h(t)\, dt $$ For sinusoidal pressure and mass-flow oscillations with phase lag $\phi$: $$ p(t) = p_m + p_a \cos(\omega t) $$ $$ \dot{m}(t) = \dot{m}_a \cos(\omega t + \phi) $$ **Step 3 — Resolving the time average:** Using the trigonometric identity $\cos(\omega t)\cos(\omega t + \phi) = \tfrac{1}{2}[\cos\phi + \cos(2\omega t + \phi)]$: $$ \langle \dot{H} \rangle = \tfrac{1}{2}\dot{m}_a\, \Delta h\, \cos\phi $$ **Step 4 — Refrigeration power:** The rectified enthalpy flow from cold to hot equals the cooling power: $$ \boxed{\;\dot{Q}_L = \tfrac{1}{2}\, \dot{m}_a\, \Delta h\, \cos\phi\;} $$ This is the **fundamental pulse-tube refrigeration equation** demanded by KTU examiners. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Physical Interpretation:** > - The factor $\tfrac{1}{2}$ arises from time-averaging. > - $\cos\phi$ represents the **phase shift** introduced by the orifice/inertance. **Maximum cooling occurs at $\phi = 0°$**, i.e., when mass flow is in phase with the **velocity** rather than the pressure. > - The optimum phase shift is typically **20° – 40°** in real systems (beyond which flow losses dominate). ### 3.3 Derivation: Cold Capacity Scaling Law for Pulse Tubes The mass-flow amplitude is proportional to pressure amplitude and pulse tube geometry: $$ \dot{m}_a \propto p_a\, A_{PT} $$ The enthalpy difference is proportional to temperature lift: $$ \Delta h = c_p\, \Delta T_{lift} $$ Substituting into the refrigeration equation: $$ \dot{Q}_L \propto p_a\, c_p\, \Delta T_{lift}\, \cos\phi $$ But the *no-load temperature* (lowest achievable) scales with $p_a^2$ (because gas-column inertia dominates), so for a fixed geometry: $$ \boxed{\;\dot{Q}_L \propto p_a^2\, p_m\, V_{PT}\, f\;} $$ This is the **standard empirical scaling law** for pulse-tube cold capacity, frequently quoted in KTU board questions. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram: Gifford-McMahon Refrigerator (Functional Architecture Flow) ```mermaid flowchart LR A1[Compressor Module
Oil free He compressor] --> A2[Rotary Switching Valve
60 rpm typical] A2 -->|High p line| A3[Inlet Manifold
to cold head] A2 -->|Low p line| A4[Outlet Manifold
from cold head] A3 --> A5[Regenerator
HoCu2 Er3Ni matrix] A5 --> A6[Displacer Top
gas filled gap] A6 --> A7[Expansion Space
cold finger] A7 --> A8[Cold End HX
load mount 4 to 80 K] A8 --> A9[Return through
regenerator warm end] A9 --> A4 A2 --> A10[Drive Motor
synchro timing] A10 -.-> A6 A11[Hot End HX
water cooled 300 K] --> A1 A12[Charge Port
He 99.999 percent] --> A1 ``` **Reading Guide for Students:** - Solid arrows = gas flow path during one half-cycle. - Dashed arrow = mechanical linkage (motor → displacer). - Note the *split* between mechanical (compressor) and fluid (cold head) sub-systems. ### 4.2 Block Diagram: Pulse Tube Cryocooler (Functional Architecture Flow) ```mermaid flowchart TB B1[Compressor Module
He 20 to 30 bar] --> B2[Rotary Valve
pressure oscillator] B2 -->|Hot manifold| B3[Regenerator
2 stage cold head] B3 --> B4[Cold End HX
4 to 80 K stage 1] B3 --> B5[Pulse Tube Stage 1
laminar flow inside] B4 --> B5 B5 --> B6[Orifice Valve
phase shift element] B6 --> B7[Reservoir
large buffer volume] B7 --> B8[Hot End HX
water cooled] B8 -->|Return line| B2 B3 --> B9[Second Stage
Regenerator] B9 --> B10[Cold Tip HX
4 K load mount] B9 --> B11[Pulse Tube Stage 2
interstage coupling] B11 --> B6 B12[Inertance Tube
alternative to orifice] -.-> B6 B13[Double Inlet Valve
improved phase control] -.-> B6 ``` **Reading Guide for Students:** - Notice that **no moving parts exist inside the cold space** — a key differentiator from G-M. - The orifice/inertance/double-inlet valve can be interchanged — each represents a *generation* of PTC design evolution. ### 4.3 Comparative Block Diagram: G-M vs Pulse Tube ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph GM[GM Refrigerator Architecture] C1[Compressor] --> C2[Rotary Valve] C2 --> C3[Regenerator] C3 --> C4[Displacer MOVING] C4 --> C5[Cold Tip] end subgraph PTC[Pulse Tube Architecture] D1[Compressor] --> D2[Rotary Valve] D2 --> D3[Regenerator] D3 --> D4[Pulse Tube NO MOVING PARTS] D4 --> D5[Orifice] D5 --> D6[Reservoir] D4 --> D7[Cold Tip] end ``` > [!NOTE] > **Comparison Helper:** The displaced MOVING element in G-M is *replaced* by a *stationary* pulse tube in PTC. The function of the displacer is taken over by the *orifice + reservoir* combination, which creates the necessary phase shift externally (at warm end). ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **A.1 [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** *State the principle of operation of a Gifford-McMahon refrigerator. List its major components.* **Model Answer (10 lines):** A Gifford-McMahon (G-M) refrigerator is a *closed-cycle regenerative cryocooler* in which a room-temperature reciprocating compressor supplies high- and low-pressure helium pulses to a cold head through a rotary switching valve. Inside the cold head, a displacer shuttles the gas between a regenerator and an expansion space (cold finger). During each cycle, gas is alternately compressed, cooled by the regenerator, expanded in the cold space (producing refrigeration), and exhausted. **Major components:** (1) Compressor, (2) Rotary (switching) valve, (3) Inlet/outlet manifolds, (4) **Regenerator** (e.g., HoCu₂, Er₃Ni), (5) **Displacer**, (6) **Expansion space (cold finger)**, (7) Hot-end heat exchanger, (8) Cold-end heat exchanger. --- **A.2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *Differentiate between a Basic Pulse Tube (BPT) and an Orifice Pulse Tube (OPT) cryocooler.* **Model Answer (8–10 lines):** | Feature | Basic Pulse Tube (BPT) | Orifice Pulse Tube (OPT) | |---------|------------------------|--------------------------| | Phase-shift element | **None** (open hot end) | **Orifice** between pulse tube and reservoir | | DC flow path | Absent — only oscillating flow | Present — rectified mass flow | | Refrigeration mechanism | Surface heat pumping only | Surface heat pumping **+ enthalpy flow** | | Cold capacity | Very low | ~5–10× higher than BPT | | Practical use | Obsolete (laboratory only) | Widely commercialised | The **OPT** is the *minimum* viable commercial PTC; without the orifice, the BPT cannot sustain meaningful cooling at cryogenic temperatures. --- ### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Provided) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Gifford-McMahon Focus **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3 Internal Choice Q1]** **(a)** Explain the construction and working of a Gifford-McMahon refrigerator with the help of a neat schematic diagram. Describe the four processes of the G-M cycle on a P-V diagram. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A two-stage G-M refrigerator operates with high pressure $p_H = 25$ bar and low pressure $p_L = 7$ bar. The expansion space volume is $V_E = 0.05\,L$ and the displacer swept volume is $V_D = 0.10\,L$. The cold temperature is $T_C = 4.2\,K$ and the hot temperature is $T_H = 300\,K$. The working gas is helium ($\gamma = 5/3$, $R_u = 8.314\,J/mol\cdot K$, $c_p = 5.19\,kJ/kg\cdot K$). Calculate: (i) The mean pressure $p_m$ and pressure ratio $\pi$. (ii) The mass of gas transferred per cycle. (iii) The net work done per cycle. (iv) The ideal Carnot COP and the ideal G-M COP. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution:** **(a) Construction & Working:** The G-M refrigerator consists of two physically separated units — a **compressor module** at room temperature and a **cold head** that is thermally insulated and connected to the compressor via gas-transfer lines. A **rotary switching valve** at the compressor outlet alternates the supply between a high-pressure line and a low-pressure exhaust line. The cold head contains a cylindrical housing fitted with (i) a **regenerator matrix** made of porous high-heat-capacity material such as HoCu₂ or Er₃Ni, (ii) a low-mass **displacer** that slides axially inside the housing with a small clearance gap, (iii) an **expansion space** (cold finger) at the bottom, and (iv) heat exchangers at both ends. **Working Cycle (refer to Section 2.1 for the four-step description):** 1. **High-pressure charge** (rotary valve open to $p_H$ line) 2. **Displacer down-stroke** (gas forced back through regenerator) 3. **Low-pressure vent** (rotary valve open to $p_L$ line) 4. **Displacer up-stroke** (gas in expansion space expands → refrigeration) **P–V Diagram:** The cycle traces a rectangle-like closed loop on the P-V plane, with two isobaric processes (expansion at $p_H$, compression at $p_L$) and two isochoric processes (pressure change at constant volume). The enclosed area equals the net work input. --- **(b) Numerical Solution:** **Given:** $p_H = 25\,bar = 25 \times 10^5\,Pa$ $p_L = 7\,bar = 7 \times 10^5\,Pa$ $V_E = 0.05\,L = 5 \times 10^{-5}\,m^3$ $V_D = 0.10\,L = 1 \times 10^{-4}\,m^3$ $T_C = 4.2\,K$ $T_H = 300\,K$ $\gamma = 5/3$ $c_p = 5190\,J/kg\cdot K$ **(i) Mean pressure & pressure ratio:** $$ p_m = \dfrac{p_H + p_L}{2} = \dfrac{25 + 7}{2} = \mathbf{16\,bar} $$ $$ \pi = \dfrac{p_H}{p_L} = \dfrac{25}{7} \approx \mathbf{3.57} $$ **(ii) Mass per cycle:** Using ideal gas law with mean pressure and approximate mean temperature $T_{mean} = (T_C + T_H)/2 = 152.1\,K$ (or more accurately, using cold temperature since the expansion occurs there): $$ m = \dfrac{(p_H - p_L)\, V_E}{R_u\, T_C / M_{He}} $$ For helium $M_{He} = 4 \times 10^{-3}\,kg/mol$, so $R_{specific} = R_u/M = 2077\,J/kg\cdot K$: $$ m = \dfrac{(25 - 7)\times 10^5 \times 5 \times 10^{-5}}{2077 \times 4.2} $$ $$ m = \dfrac{18 \times 10^5 \times 5 \times 10^{-5}}{8723.4} = \dfrac{90}{8723.4} $$ $$ \boxed{\;m \approx 0.01032\,kg = 10.32\,g\;} $$ **(iii) Net work per cycle:** $$ W_{net} = (p_H - p_L)\, V_D = (25 - 7) \times 10^5 \times 1 \times 10^{-4} $$ $$ W_{net} = 18 \times 10^5 \times 10^{-4} = \mathbf{180\,J} $$ **(iv) Carnot COP and ideal G-M COP:** $$ \text{COP}_{Carnot} = \dfrac{T_C}{T_H - T_C} = \dfrac{4.2}{300 - 4.2} = \dfrac{4.2}{295.8} $$ $$ \text{COP}_{Carnot} \approx \mathbf{0.0142} $$ For ideal G-M (using the derived formula in Section 3.1): $$ \text{COP}_{GM,ideal} = \dfrac{V_E}{V_D}\cdot \dfrac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1}\cdot \dfrac{T_C}{T_H} $$ $$ = \dfrac{0.05}{0.10}\cdot \dfrac{5/3}{2/3}\cdot \dfrac{4.2}{300} $$ $$ = 0.5 \times 2.5 \times 0.014 $$ $$ \boxed{\;\text{COP}_{GM,ideal} \approx 0.0175\;} $$ --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Pulse Tube Focus **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3 Internal Choice Q2]** **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working of a **Double-Inlet Pulse Tube Cryocooler**. Discuss how it improves upon the basic orifice pulse tube. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A pulse tube cryocooler operates with pressure amplitude $p_a = 1.5\,bar$, mean pressure $p_m = 10\,bar$, pulse tube volume $V_{PT} = 50\,cm^3$, and operating frequency $f = 1.5\,Hz$. The phase shift introduced by the orifice is $\phi = 30°$ and the enthalpy difference of gas between cold and hot ends is $\Delta h = 800\,J/kg$. The mass-flow amplitude is $\dot{m}_a = 0.012\,kg/s$. Calculate: (i) The refrigeration power using the enthalpy-flow equation. (ii) The expected cold capacity using the empirical scaling law, given $K_{cal} = 0.85\,W\cdot s/(m^3\cdot bar^2)$. (iii) The Carnot COP at $T_C = 80\,K$, $T_H = 300\,K$ and the FOM if actual measured COP is 0.0008. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution:** **(a) Construction & Working of Double-Inlet Pulse Tube Cryocooler:** A **double-inlet pulse tube (DIPT)** modifies the standard OPT by adding a *secondary* inlet valve between the pulse tube and the reservoir, located near the **cold end** of the pulse tube (or at the warm end, depending on the design). This valve allows a fraction of the oscillating gas to *bypass* the regenerator and pulse tube, returning directly from the cold end to the reservoir during one half of the cycle. **Block Diagram Layout:** Compressor → Rotary Valve → Regenerator → **Cold-end HX** → Pulse Tube ↔ **Double-Inlet Valve** ↔ Reservoir → Hot-end HX → Return line. **Improvement over basic OPT:** - The double-inlet valve introduces an *additional* controlled phase shift between the cold end and the warm end. - This reduces the *impedance mismatch* and allows **lower no-load temperatures** (down to ~25 K with single stage, instead of 40–50 K for plain OPT). - Mass flow through the regenerator is *reduced* → lower regenerator loss → higher FOM. - Typical improvement: **20–40 % more cooling capacity** at the same input power, or alternatively, **lower achievable temperature** at the same capacity. --- **(b) Numerical Solution:** **Given:** $p_a = 1.5\,bar$ $p_m = 10\,bar$ $V_{PT} = 50\,cm^3 = 50 \times 10^{-6}\,m^3$ $f = 1.5\,Hz$ $\phi = 30°$ $\Delta h = 800\,J/kg$ $\dot{m}_a = 0.012\,kg/s$ **(i) Refrigeration power (enthalpy-flow equation):** $$ \dot{Q}_L = \tfrac{1}{2}\dot{m}_a\, \Delta h\, \cos\phi $$ $$ \cos(30°) = \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \approx 0.866 $$ $$ \dot{Q}_L = 0.5 \times 0.012 \times 800 \times 0.866 $$ $$ \dot{Q}_L = 0.006 \times 800 \times 0.866 = 4.8 \times 0.866 $$ $$ \boxed{\;\dot{Q}_L \approx 4.16\,W\;} $$ **(ii) Cold capacity using the scaling law:** $$ \dot{Q}_L = K_{cal}\, p_a^2\, p_m\, V_{PT}\, f $$ $$ = 0.85 \times (1.5)^2 \times 10 \times (50 \times 10^{-6}) \times 1.5 $$ $$ = 0.85 \times 2.25 \times 10 \times 75 \times 10^{-6} $$ $$ = 0.85 \times 2.25 \times 750 \times 10^{-6} $$ $$ = 0.85 \times 1687.5 \times 10^{-6} = 1434.4 \times 10^{-6}\,W $$ $$ \boxed{\;\dot{Q}_L \approx 1.43\,mW\;} $$ *(Note: This very small value reflects the scaling-law coefficient's dependence on geometry and the chosen operating regime; in real designs, $K_{cal}$ is empirical and tuned to the actual hardware.)* **(iii) Carnot COP and FOM:** $$ \text{COP}_{Carnot} = \dfrac{T_C}{T_H - T_C} = \dfrac{80}{300 - 80} = \dfrac{80}{220} $$ $$ \text{COP}_{Carnot} \approx 0.3636 $$ $$ \text{FOM} = \dfrac{\text{COP}_{actual}}{\text{COP}_{Carnot}} = \dfrac{0.0008}{0.3636} $$ $$ \boxed{\;\text{FOM} \approx 0.0022\;(0.22\%)\;} $$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Unit consistency is critical.** Pressure must be in **Pa** (not bar) when used with SI volume in m³. A common mistake is computing work as $(p_H - p_L)\,V$ in mixed units, giving an answer off by a factor of $10^5$. > 2. **Direction of COP inequality.** Students often write $\text{COP}_{actual} > \text{COP}_{Carnot}$ in their summary, claiming the device "beats Carnot". This is impossible — Carnot COP is the upper bound. KTU examiners deduct **2 full marks** for this error. > 3. **Confusing GM and PTC cycles.** In GM, the rotary valve *switches* between high and low pressure lines (so $p$ is a step function). In PTC, the *compressor itself* provides the pressure oscillation through the rotary valve — but some PTCs use an *inline* valve instead. Do not interchange the two mechanisms in your answer. > 4. **Phase shift interpretation.** In the enthalpy-flow equation, $\phi$ is between *pressure* and *mass flow*, not between pressure and velocity. This nuance is frequently missed. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!NOTE] > **Mandatory High-Density Summary for KTU 2024 Board Revision** - ✅ **G-M Refrigerator:** Cold head + room-temperature compressor connected via a *rotary valve*. Working fluid: Helium. Cycle = two isobaric + two isochoric processes on P-V diagram. - ✅ **Pulse Tube Cryocooler:** No moving parts in the cold space. Refrigeration arises from *phase-shifted* enthalpy flow + *surface heat pumping*. - ✅ **Carnot COP formula** must use **Kelvin** temperatures. - ✅ **FOM** = $\text{COP}_{actual} / \text{COP}_{Carnot}$; typical real values 0.05–0.15 for GM, 0.02–0.10 for PTC. - ✅ **Pressure ratio** $\pi = p_H / p_L$ typically between **2 and 4**. - ✅ **Enthalpy-flow refrigeration power** $\dot{Q}_L = \tfrac{1}{2}\dot{m}_a\, \Delta h\, \cos\phi$ — **memorise this equation** for any 7-mark derivation. - ✅ **Cold-capacity scaling** $\dot{Q}_L \propto p_a^2\, p_m\, V_{PT}\, f$ — used when no detailed model is asked. - ✅ **Three PTC variants** (must be aware for KTU short answers): **Basic Pulse Tube (BPT)**, **Orifice Pulse Tube (OPT)**, **Double-Inlet Pulse Tube (DIPT)**. Inertance tube is a *modern alternative* to orifice. - ✅ **Regenerator materials** depend on temperature stage: Pb or Gd₂O₃ for 30–80 K, Er₃Ni for 4–20 K, HoCu₂ for 4–10 K. - ✅ **Typical temperature ranges:** 1-stage GM → 30–80 K; 2-stage GM → 4–30 K; 3-stage PTC with magnetic regenerator → 1.5–4 K. - ✅ **Applications:** MRI (medical), satellite IR sensors (defence/space), LHC detectors (research), HTS cables (power). - ✅ **Key advantage of PTC over GM:** *No moving parts* at the cold end → *lower vibration*, *higher reliability*, *longer maintenance intervals*. - ✅ **Key advantage of GM over PTC:** *Higher cold capacity* per unit mass of hardware, mature technology, lower initial cost. - ✅ **Always label diagrams** with: compressor, rotary valve, regenerator, displacer/Pulse Tube, cold end HX, hot end HX, reservoir (PTC only), orifice/inertance (PTC only). - ✅ **Final formula to remember for board:** $\text{COP}_{GM,ideal} = \dfrac{V_E}{V_D}\cdot \dfrac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1}\cdot \dfrac{T_C}{T_H}$. > **END OF MODULE 3 NOTES — Gifford McMahon Refrigerators and Pulse Tube Cryocoolers**

topics.title: Refrigerators Using Solids as Working Media - Magnetic Refrigerators - Thermodynamics of Magnetic Refrigerators and Dilution Refrigerators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET861 - CRYOGENIC ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Refrigerators Using Solids as Working Media - Magnetic Refrigerators - Thermodynamics of Magnetic Refrigerators and Dilution Refrigerators

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Refrigerators Using Solids as Working Media — Introduction

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board Examiner Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme)**
> Solid-state cryogenic refrigerators are systems that achieve very low temperatures (typically **below 1 K**) by exploiting the **thermal and magnetic properties of solid working media**, rather than the compression–expansion of gases used in conventional refrigerators. The two principal categories covered in this module are **Magnetic Refrigerators (Adiabatic Demagnetization Refrigerators, ADR)** and **Dilution Refrigerators** using liquid ³He–⁴He mixtures.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Use Solids?** Below ~0.3 K, all gases have condensed to liquids or solids, and the vapor-pressure method of cooling becomes impractically slow. Solids offer an entirely different thermodynamic pathway — manipulating the **internal degrees of freedom** (spin systems, quantum fluids) — to extract heat at ultra-low temperatures.

---

### 1.2 Magnetic Refrigerator (Adiabatic Demagnetization Refrigerator)

**Formal Definition:** A magnetic refrigerator is a cryogenic device that uses a **paramagnetic solid** (working substance) and exploits the **magnetocaloric effect** — the reversible temperature change of a magnetic material when subjected to a changing magnetic field under adiabatic or isothermal conditions — to achieve temperatures in the **millikelvin (mK) to microkelvin (μK)** range.

**Working Substance (Typical Paramagnetic Salts):**
- **CMN** — Cerium Magnesium Nitrate [Ce₂Mg₃(NO₃)₁₂·24H₂O]
- **CPA** — Cerium Phosphate [CePO₄]
- **GGG** — Gadolinium Gallium Garnet [Gd₃Ga₅O₁₂]
- **Dysprosium compounds** — for higher temperature ranges

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The "Sponge of Spins"**
> Imagine the paramagnetic salt as a *sponge full of tiny magnetic compass needles (atomic spins)*. When you switch on an external magnet, all the needles snap into alignment — they become **ordered**. Re-arranging them releases a tiny puff of energy (heat) which is expelled to the surroundings. Now, when you **suddenly remove** the magnet while thermally insulating the sponge, the needles have to "find their own way" — they become disordered, and to do this they must **steal heat energy from the surrounding lattice**, cooling the solid dramatically. This is precisely the principle of adiabatic demagnetization.

---

### 1.3 Dilution Refrigerator

**Formal Definition:** A dilution refrigerator is a continuous-cycle cryogenic device that exploits the **phase separation of ³He from ⁴He** below the tricritical temperature of **~0.87 K** and the quantum-mechanical property that ³He atoms have a lower effective energy when dissolved in superfluid ⁴He than when present as a concentrated (nearly pure) liquid. The "evaporation" of ³He atoms from a concentrated phase into a dilute phase absorbs latent heat, producing continuous cooling down to **~2 mK**.

**Working Substances:**
- **³He (Helium-3)** — light, fermionic isotope of helium
- **⁴He (Helium-4)** — common bosonic isotope of helium (acts as solvent)

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The "Endless Faucet of Cold"**
> Picture two immiscible liquids (³He-rich on top, ³He-poor on bottom) locked in a closed thermos. Tiny ³He atoms constantly "leak" from the upper layer to the lower layer, just as water evaporates from a pond. Every time a ³He atom "evaporates" downward, it must absorb a small quantum of latent heat from its surroundings — producing cooling. Unlike a pond that dries up, a pump continuously extracts ³He from the lower layer, recondenses it, and returns it to the top. The result is a **perpetual, steady-state cold bath** — the dilution refrigerator's hallmark.

---

### 1.4 Comparison Snapshot

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Distinction for KTU Examinations**
> - **Magnetic Refrigerator (ADR):** Operates in **batch mode** (single-shot), reaching **microkelvin** range, requires re-magnetization cycle.
> - **Dilution Refrigerator:** Operates in **continuous mode**, typically reaches **2–10 mK**, requires continuous pumping.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Framework — Magnetic Refrigerators

#### 2.1.1 The Magnetocaloric Effect

When a paramagnetic material is placed in a magnetic field **H**, the magnetic dipoles (spins) partially align with the field, **reducing the magnetic (spin) entropy** of the system. Two complementary processes exploit this:

| Process | Condition | Field Change | Entropy | Temperature |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Isothermal Magnetization | T = constant | H increases | S decreases | — |
| Adiabatic Demagnetization | S = constant | H decreases | — | T decreases |

#### 2.1.2 First Law for a Magnetic System

For a magnetic system with internal energy **U(T, H)**:

$$dU = TdS + \mu_0 H dM$$

where:
- $T$ = absolute temperature (K)
- $S$ = total entropy (J/K)
- $\mu_0$ = permeability of free space = **4π × 10⁻⁷ H/m**
- $H$ = applied magnetic field (A/m)
- $M$ = magnetization (A/m)

#### 2.1.3 Maxwell Relation from Helmholtz Free Energy

Defining $F = U - TS$, with $dF = -SdT + \mu_0 H dM$, the cross-differentiation yields:

$$\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial H}\right)_T = \mu_0 \left(\frac{\partial M}{\partial T}\right)_H$$

#### 2.1.4 Curie Law and Curie–Weiss Law

For an ideal paramagnet with no interaction between spins:

$$M = \frac{C \cdot H}{T}$$

where $C$ = **Curie Constant** = $\dfrac{N \mu_B^2 g^2 J(J+1)}{3 k_B}$

For interacting spins (above ordering temperature):

$$M = \frac{C \cdot H}{T - \theta}$$

where $\theta$ = **Weiss constant** (Curie temperature).

#### 2.1.5 Magnetocaloric Effect Equation (Board Favorite)

Combining the Maxwell relation with cyclic identity yields:

$$\left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial H}\right)_S = -\frac{\mu_0 T}{C_H} \left(\frac{\partial M}{\partial T}\right)_H$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** This equation tells us that the temperature change of an adiabatic process is governed by **how rapidly magnetization changes with temperature**, weighted by the heat capacity at constant field $C_H$. Strongly temperature-dependent magnetization (large $\partial M/\partial T$) yields large magnetocaloric effects — which is why **rare-earth salts** with high Curie constants are preferred.

---

### 2.2 Theoretical Framework — Dilution Refrigerators

#### 2.2.1 ³He–⁴He Phase Diagram (Below 0.87 K)

Below the **tricritical temperature (0.87 K)**, the ³He–⁴He mixture spontaneously separates into two liquid phases:

- **Concentrated phase (³He-rich):** ~100% ³He at T → 0 K; floats on top (lower density).
- **Dilute phase (³He-poor):** ³He dissolved in superfluid ⁴He; ³He mole fraction $x_d \to 0.064$ as $T \to 0$ K.

#### 2.2.2 Why Phase Separation Persists Down to Absolute Zero

A ³He atom dissolved in superfluid ⁴He gains **zero-point kinetic energy** because it must localize itself between ⁴He atoms. Its energy **E₀ ≈ 0.56 K** above the energy in pure ³He phase. Therefore, even at T = 0 K, the concentrated phase (pure ³He) has lower energy than dilute ³He. Equilibrium demands that ³He "evaporates" from the dilute to the concentrated phase — exactly the **opposite** of a normal liquid mixture.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** This quantum-mechanical effect is unique to ³He–⁴He. It is the *only known liquid mixture that phase-separates down to absolute zero*, making the dilution refrigerator the **only continuous-cycle refrigerator** capable of reaching millikelvin temperatures.

#### 2.2.3 Cooling Mechanism

³He atoms continuously "evaporate" from the **concentrated phase** into the **dilute phase** through the phase boundary in the **mixing chamber**. Each ³He atom crossing the boundary absorbs **latent heat of dilution** $Q_d \approx 95 T^2$ J/mol, cooling the surrounding liquid.

#### 2.2.4 Steady-State Cooling Power Equation

$$\dot{Q}_{\text{mixing}} = \dot{n}_3 \left[ h_d(T) - h_c(T) \right] \approx 95 \, T^2 \, \dot{n}_3 \quad \text{(W)}$$

where:
- $\dot{n}_3$ = molar flow rate of ³He (mol/s)
- $T$ = mixing chamber temperature (K)
- $h_d, h_c$ = partial molar enthalpies of ³He in dilute and concentrated phases

#### 2.2.5 ³He Concentration in Dilute Phase (Solved Quantitatively)

$$x_d(T) \approx 0.85 \, T^{3/2} \, e^{-0.560/T}$$

This decays **exponentially** with temperature — explaining why the cooling power diminishes rapidly at very low T.

---

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $dU = TdS + \mu_0 H dM$ | First law for magnetic system | J, K, J/K, T |
| 2 | $\left(\dfrac{\partial S}{\partial H}\right)_T = \mu_0 \left(\dfrac{\partial M}{\partial T}\right)_H$ | Maxwell relation | — |
| 3 | $\left(\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial H}\right)_S = -\dfrac{\mu_0 T}{C_H} \left(\dfrac{\partial M}{\partial T}\right)_H$ | Magnetocaloric equation | K/T |
| 4 | $M = \dfrac{CH}{T}$ | Curie's Law (ideal paramagnet) | A/m |
| 5 | $M = \dfrac{CH}{T-\theta}$ | Curie–Weiss Law | A/m |
| 6 | $T_f = T_i \cdot \dfrac{H_f}{H_i}$ | Ideal adiabatic demagnetization | K |
| 7 | $S(T,H) = S_0 - \dfrac{CH^2}{2T^2}$ | Spin entropy (low H/T limit) | J/K |
| 8 | $C_{\text{spin}} = \dfrac{CH^2}{T^2}$ | Magnetic heat capacity at const H | J/K |
| 9 | $x_d(T) \approx 0.85\, T^{3/2} \exp(-0.560/T)$ | ³He solubility in dilute phase | mole fraction |
| 10 | $\dot{Q} \approx 95\, T^2\, \dot{n}_3$ | Dilution refrigerator cooling power | W |
| 11 | $T_c^{\text{tri}} = 0.87$ K | ³He–⁴He phase separation temperature | K |
| 12 | $\eta_{\text{Carnot}} = \dfrac{T_C}{T_H - T_C}$ | Carnot efficiency limit | — |

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Magnetic Refrigerators** → Ultra-low temperature physics research, quantum computing dilution pre-cooling, fundamental constant measurements.
- **Dilution Refrigerators** → Industry standard for **quantum computer cooling** (IBM, Google, Rigetti), ultra-sensitive SQUID magnetometry, dark matter detection experiments (CDMS), CMB telescope bolometers.

## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I — Magnetocaloric Effect Equation (Board Critical)

**Starting Point:** Magnetic first law
$$dU = TdS + \mu_0 H dM$$

Define the Helmholtz free energy $F = U - TS$, so:
$$dF = -S dT + \mu_0 H dM$$

**Step 1 — Extract Partial Derivatives of F:**
By definition of total differential:
$$\left(\frac{\partial F}{\partial T}\right)_M = -S \quad ; \quad \left(\frac{\partial F}{\partial M}\right)_T = \mu_0 H$$

**Step 2 — Apply Equality of Mixed Second Derivatives:**
$$\left(\frac{\partial^2 F}{\partial M \, \partial T}\right) = \left(\frac{\partial^2 F}{\partial T \, \partial M}\right)$$

Substituting:
$$-\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial M}\right)_T = \mu_0 \left(\frac{\partial H}{\partial T}\right)_M$$

Equivalently, switching the independent variable from M to H (using M = M(T,H)):
$$\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial H}\right)_T = \mu_0 \left(\frac{\partial M}{\partial T}\right)_H$$

**Step 3 — Form the Adiabatic (S = const) Temperature Derivative:**
Use the cyclic relation for $S(T, H)$:
$$\left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial H}\right)_S \left(\frac{\partial H}{\partial S}\right)_T \left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial T}\right)_H = -1$$

Solving for $(\partial T/\partial H)_S$:
$$\left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial H}\right)_S = -\frac{(\partial S/\partial H)_T}{(\partial S/\partial T)_H}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute Maxwell Relation and Heat Capacity Definition:**
With $(\partial S/\partial H)_T = \mu_0 (\partial M/\partial T)_H$ and $C_H = T(\partial S/\partial T)_H$:

$$\left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial H}\right)_S = -\frac{\mu_0 T}{C_H} \left(\frac{\partial M}{\partial T}\right)_H \quad \blacksquare$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation II — Final Temperature in Ideal Adiabatic Demagnetization

**Assumption:** Ideal paramagnet obeying Curie's Law $M = CH/T$. Internal energy depends only on T (lattice), and the magnetic entropy dominates.

**Step 1 — Write Spin Entropy in Low H/T Limit:**
For a system of N spins each of spin J:
$$S(T,H) = N k_B \ln(2J+1) - \frac{N \mu_B^2 g^2 J(J+1)}{6 k_B} \cdot \frac{H^2}{T^2}$$

Compact form:
$$S(T,H) = S_0 - \frac{C H^2}{2 T^2}$$

where $S_0 = N k_B \ln(2J+1)$ is the high-temperature (random) entropy.

**Step 2 — Apply Adiabatic Constraint $S(T, H) = \text{const}$:**
$$S_0 - \frac{C H^2}{2 T^2} = \text{const}$$

This reduces to:
$$\frac{H^2}{T^2} = \text{const} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{H}{T} = \text{const}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for Final Temperature:**
$$\frac{H_i}{T_i} = \frac{H_f}{T_f} \quad \Rightarrow \quad T_f = T_i \cdot \frac{H_f}{H_i} \quad \blacksquare$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the famous ADR result**, frequently asked in KTU examinations.

**Numerical Illustration:**
A paramagnetic salt is magnetized isothermally at 4.2 K in a field of 2.0 T, then adiabatically demagnetized to 0.05 T. Final temperature:
$$T_f = 4.2 \times \frac{0.05}{2.0} = 0.105 \text{ K}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation III — Cooling Power of Dilution Refrigerator

**Starting Point:** Steady-flow energy balance on the mixing chamber.

Let:
- $\dot{n}_3$ = molar flow rate of ³He (mol/s) entering as concentrated phase
- $h_c(T_m)$ = partial molar enthalpy of ³He in concentrated phase at mixing chamber temperature $T_m$
- $h_d(T_m)$ = partial molar enthalpy of ³He in dilute phase at mixing chamber temperature $T_m$

**Step 1 — Energy Conservation at Mixing Chamber:**
The ³He arrives with enthalpy $h_c$ and leaves (after dilution) with enthalpy $h_d$. The enthalpy absorbed as cooling equals this difference:

$$\dot{Q}_{\text{cool}} = \dot{n}_3 \left[ h_d(T_m) - h_c(T_m) \right]$$

**Step 2 — Compute Partial Molar Enthalpies at Low T:**

For **pure liquid ³He** (concentrated phase), treated as a degenerate Fermi liquid with $T_F \approx 0.38$ K:
$$h_c(T) = \frac{5}{2} RT - \frac{\pi^2}{4} R \frac{T^2}{T_F} \approx 12\, T^2 \quad \text{J/mol}$$

For **³He dissolved in superfluid ⁴He** (dilute phase), behaving as a quasi-ideal Fermi gas with effective mass $m^* \approx 2.4\, m_3$:
$$h_d(T) \approx 0 \quad \text{(in the millikelvin range, as } T \ll T_F^{\text{dilute}}\text{)}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute and Simplify:**
$$\dot{Q}_{\text{cool}} \approx \dot{n}_3 \left[ 0 - (-12\, T_m^2) \right] = 12\, T_m^2\, \dot{n}_3$$

However, experimentally (Pobell, Lounasmaa), the more accurate result including entropy-of-mixing terms is:

$$\boxed{\dot{Q}_{\text{cool}} \approx 95\, T_m^2\, \dot{n}_3 \quad \text{W}}$$

**Numerical Illustration:**
A dilution refrigerator circulates ³He at $\dot{n}_3 = 200\ \mu\text{mol/s}$ and operates at $T_m = 0.05$ K:
$$\dot{Q}_{\text{cool}} = 95 \times (0.05)^2 \times (200 \times 10^{-6})$$
$$\dot{Q}_{\text{cool}} = 95 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 2 \times 10^{-4}$$
$$\dot{Q}_{\text{cool}} = 4.75 \times 10^{-5} \text{ W} = 47.5\ \mu\text{W}$$

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Performance

| Parameter | Magnetic Refrigerator (ADR) | Dilution Refrigerator |
|---|---|---|
| Working medium | Paramagnetic salt (solid) | ³He–⁴He liquid mixture |
| Lowest temperature | ~1 μK (with nuclear ADR) | ~2 mK (practical); ~1.5 mK (lab record) |
| Operating mode | **Single-shot (batch)** | **Continuous (steady-state)** |
| Cycle reset required | Yes | No |
| Physical principle | Magnetocaloric effect | ³He "evaporation" into ⁴He |
| Governing equation | $T_f = T_i \cdot H_f/H_i$ | $\dot{Q} = 95\, T^2\, \dot{n}_3$ |
| Practical applications | Pre-cooling for dilution, fundamental research | Quantum computers, SQUIDs, CMB bolometers |
| Pre-cooling required | Liquid helium (~4 K) | Liquid helium (~4 K) |

## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Flow — Magnetic Refrigeration Cycle (Carnot Analog)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1["STATE 1: T_H, H_LOW, S_HIGH"] -->|"Isothermal Magnetization\nH increases, Q rejected"| A2
    A2["STATE 2: T_H, H_HIGH, S_LOW"] -->|"Adiabatic Demagnetization\nS constant, T drops"| A3
    A3["STATE 3: T_C, H_LOW, S_LOW"] -->|"Isothermal Demagnetization\nQ absorbed from cold load"| A4
    A4["STATE 4: T_C, H_HIGH, S_HIGH"] -->|"Adiabatic Magnetization\nS constant, T rises"| A1
    A3 -->|"Cooling effect delivered"| A5["COLD LOAD AT T_C"]
```

**Process interpretation in T–S plane:** Two isotherms (T_H and T_C) and two isentropes (S_low and S_high), forming a closed rectangular cycle — the **magnetic Carnot cycle**.

---

### 4.2 Functional Block Flow — Dilution Refrigerator Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    B1["³He GAS\nT_amb P_atm"] -->|"Room T compressor\nand purifier"| B2
    B2["³He INLET\nT_4K, P_high"] -->|"Counter-flow\nheat exchanger\nHX1"| B3
    B3["³He PRE-COOLED\nT_still, P_high"] -->|"Still condenser\nheat exchanger\nHX2"| B4
    B4["³He LIQUEFIES\nT_still"] -->|"Drip into\nconcentrated phase"| B5
    B5["MIXING CHAMBER\nTop concentrated phase\nT_mix, x=1"] -->|"³He evaporates\nacross phase boundary\nLATENT HEAT ABSORBED\nQ_cool = 95 T² ṅ₃"| B6
    B6["DILUTE PHASE\nBottom phase\nT_mix, x_d small"] -->|"Driven by osmotic\npressure difference\nthrough superflow"| B7
    B7["STILL\nT_still approx 0.6K\n³He pumps preferentially"| B8
    B8["³He GAS to pump\nT_still, P_low"] -->|"Rotary and\ndiffusion pumps"| B1
    B5 -->|"COLD STAGE\nQ_cool output"| B9["COLD LOAD\nT_mix approx mK"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **circulation loop** is closed: ³He pumped from the still is compressed, purified, recondensed, and re-injected into the concentrated phase. The cooling is delivered continuously at the mixing chamber.

---

### 4.3 Functional Architecture — Thermodynamic Cycle Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    C1["PARAMAGNETIC SALT\nIN HIGH FIELD H_HI\nT_HI"] -->|"ADIABATIC\nDEMAGNETIZATION"| C2
    C2["PARAMAGNETIC SALT\nIN LOW FIELD H_LO\nT_LO COLD"] -->|"Heat absorbed\nfrom cold load"| C3
    C3["PARAMAGNETIC SALT\nAT T_LO H_LO\nwarmed slightly"] -->|"RE-MAGNETIZE\nto H_HI"| C1
    C2 -->|"Heat leaks in\nfrom surroundings"| C4["SALT GRADUALLY\nWARMS BACK TO T_HI"]
    C4 -->|"Cycle restart\nrequired"| C1
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This illustrates the **batch nature of ADR**: after each demagnetization, the salt must be re-magnetized to begin a new cycle. This is the **key disadvantage** compared to dilution refrigerators.

---

### 4.4 Phase-Separation Logic — ³He–⁴He Below 0.87 K

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    D1["³He–⁴He MIXTURE\nT greater than 0.87K"] -->|"Cool below\ntricritical point"| D2
    D2["T less than 0.87K\nTwo-phase region"] -->|"Gravity separates"| D3
    D3["CONCENTRATED PHASE\nTop layer\nx_3 approx 1\ndensity low"] -->|"Phase boundary\nmass transfer\nof ³He"| D4
    D4["DILUTE PHASE\nBottom layer\nx_d approx 0.064 at T→0\ndensity high"] -->|"³He pumped out\nvia still"| D5
    D5["³He VAPOR\nstill at 0.6K\nrecondensed"] -->|"Re-injected\nto concentrated phase"| D3
```
