# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **Modeled strictly on KTU 2024 Scheme Part A format — direct conceptual / definitional short answers.**

---

### Question A1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Define apparent thermal conductivity (k_app) of a cryogenic insulation. Why is it not a true material property?**

**Model Answer:**

The apparent thermal conductivity k_app is defined from Fourier's law applied to an insulation layer:

$$k_{app} = \frac{Q \cdot L}{A \cdot \Delta T}$$

It represents the **combined effect** of all four heat transfer modes (solid conduction k_s, gas conduction k_g, radiation k_r, and convection k_c) lumped into a single effective conductivity:

$$k_{app} = k_s + k_g + k_r + k_c$$

It is **not a true material property** because its value depends on operating conditions such as:
- Mean temperature T_m
- Gas pressure P in the insulation cavity
- Layer thickness L and geometry
- Surface emissivity ε of bounding walls
- Orientation (for convection)

Hence, k_app values are always quoted along with the test conditions.

---

### Question A2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**Explain the role of the Knudsen number (Kn) in determining the gas conduction component of a cryogenic insulation.**

**Model Answer:**

The Knudsen number is the ratio of the gas molecular mean free path (λ) to the characteristic pore size (D_p):

$$Kn = \frac{\lambda}{D_p}$$

It determines the **regime of gas conduction**:
- **Kn < 0.01** (Continuum): gas conduction is constant, equal to bulk gas k.
- **0.01 < Kn < 10** (Transition): gas conduction decreases sharply with decreasing pressure.
- **Kn > 10** (Free-molecular): gas conduction becomes **linear in pressure** P.

In evacuated powder or MLI insulation, the operating Knudsen number is typically > 10, which is **why reducing pressure drastically reduces k_g** and hence the total heat leak.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **Strict KTU 2024 ESE Format: Each Part B question has two sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Internal choice must be provided.**

---

### Question B-A. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain in detail the **four modes of heat transfer** that contribute to the apparent thermal conductivity of a cryogenic insulation system. For each mode, give one design strategy to reduce its contribution.

**(b)** Derive the **equivalent radiation conductivity (k_r)** for a cylindrical cryogenic dewar with inner radius r_i at temperature T_i and outer radius r_o at temperature T_o, with emissivities ε_i and ε_o. Discuss how MLI reduces this term.

#### Model Solution for (a):

The four modes are:

**1. Solid Conduction (k_s):**
- Heat transfer through direct contact between solid particles in powder insulation or through spacer materials in MLI.
- Depends on material type, density, contact area.
- **Reduction strategy:** Use low-density packing (low ρ_b) and choose materials with intrinsically low thermal conductivity (e.g., silica aerogel).

**2. Gas Conduction (k_g):**
- Heat transfer through gas molecules trapped in the insulation pores.
- Dominant at atmospheric pressure; reduced by evacuation.
- **Reduction strategy:** Reduce cavity pressure to < 10⁻³ mbar to push the gas into the free-molecular regime (Kn > 10).

**3. Radiation Heat Transfer (k_r):**
- Electromagnetic radiation between warm outer wall and cold inner wall; scales as T⁴.
- Dominant contributor at higher temperatures in vacuum insulation.
- **Reduction strategy:** Use low-emissivity reflective surfaces (aluminized Mylar) or MLI stacks.

**4. Natural Convection (k_c):**
- Bulk gas motion due to buoyancy; depends on cavity geometry, orientation, and pressure.
- Significant in atmospheric-pressure foam insulations.
- **Reduction strategy:** Eliminate by evacuation; use closed-cell foam structure to prevent gas circulation.

#### Model Solution for (b):

Starting from the Stefan-Boltzmann net radiation flux between concentric cylinders:

$$q_{rad} = \frac{\sigma(T_o^4 - T_i^4)}{\dfrac{1-\varepsilon_i}{\varepsilon_i A_i} + \dfrac{1}{A_i F_{i \to o}} + \dfrac{1-\varepsilon_o}{\varepsilon_o A_o}}$$

For long concentric cylinders F_{i→o} ≈ 1, and the resistance form simplifies to:

$$q_{rad} = \frac{\sigma A_i (T_o^4 - T_i^4)}{\dfrac{A_i(1-\varepsilon_i)}{\varepsilon_i} + A_i + \dfrac{A_i(1-\varepsilon_o)}{\varepsilon_o} \cdot \dfrac{A_i}{A_o}}$$

Linearizing $T_o^4 - T_i^4 \approx 4 T_m^3 \Delta T$ with $T_m = (T_o + T_i)/2$:

$$q_{rad} \approx \frac{4 \sigma \varepsilon_{eff} T_m^3 (T_o - T_i)}{\dfrac{1}{r_i} + \dfrac{1}{r_o}}$$

Dividing by ΔT to obtain an equivalent conductivity per unit thickness L = r_o − r_i:

$$\boxed{\,k_r = \frac{4 \sigma \varepsilon_{eff} T_m^3}{\dfrac{1}{r_i} + \dfrac{1}{r_o}}\,}$$

**Effect of MLI:** Each MLI layer inserts a low-emissivity reflective foil, splitting the total radiation temperature drop across N layers. This reduces the effective radiation conductance by a factor of N:

$$C_{rad,MLI} = \frac{C_{rad,no\,MLI}}{N}$$

Thus, k_r is reduced roughly **N-fold** compared to a single cavity. This is the physical reason why MLI is so effective.

---

### Question B-B. [KTU University Exam – June 2024] — INTERNAL CHOICE

**(a)** What is **Multilayer Insulation (MLI)**? With the aid of a schematic, describe its construction. Derive the expression for the **optimum number of MLI layers** N_opt for minimum heat leak.

**(b)** Compare **vacuum, powder, and foam insulations** for cryogenic applications in terms of apparent thermal conductivity, weight, cost, operating pressure, and typical application temperature range.

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Definition:** MLI (also called **Super Insulation**) is a cryogenic insulation system consisting of many alternating layers of **highly reflective thin foils** (typically aluminized Mylar of thickness ~ 6–25 µm) separated by **low-conductivity spacer layers** (fiberglass netting, dimpled polymer film, or tissue paper). It operates under vacuum (P < 10⁻³ mbar).

**Construction Schematic (Conceptual):**

```
[ Outer Warm Wall, T_o = 300 K ]
        │
   ╔════╧════╗  ← Reflective Foil (Aluminized Mylar)
   ║ Spacer ║  ← Fiberglass Net (low k, low contact)
   ╠════════╣  ← Reflective Foil
   ║ Spacer ║
   ╠════════╣
        ⋮   × N layers
   ╠════════╣  ← Reflective Foil
   ║ Spacer ║
   ╔════════╗
        │
[ Inner Cold Wall, T_i = 77 K / 4.2 K ]
```

**Derivation of N_opt:**

The total MLI conductance per unit area is the sum of two competing terms:

$$C_{total} = \underbrace{\frac{C_{rad,0}}{N}}_{\text{decreases with } N} + \underbrace{N \cdot k_{sp}}_{\text{increases with } N}$$

where C_{rad,0} is the bare-cavity radiation conductance and k_{sp} is the spacer conductance per layer.

Differentiating with respect to N and setting dC/dN = 0:

$$\frac{dC_{total}}{dN} = -\frac{C_{rad,0}}{N^2} + k_{sp} = 0$$

Solving:

$$\boxed{\,N_{opt} = \sqrt{\dfrac{C_{rad,0}}{k_{sp}}}\,}$$

Beyond this optimum, adding more layers hurts performance because spacer conduction begins to dominate. Typical N_opt for LN₂ service: 30–60 layers.

#### Model Solution for (b):

| Property | Vacuum Insulation | Powder Insulation (Perlite) | Foam Insulation (PUF) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Apparent k (W/m·K) at 77 K** | 0.005–0.05 (radiation-limited) | 0.001–0.002 | 0.020–0.025 |
| **Weight** | Lightest | Moderate (filling required) | Light |
| **Cost** | Moderate (mfg complexity) | Low to moderate | Very low |
| **Operating Pressure** | < 10⁻⁴ mbar (high vacuum) | 1–10 mbar (rough vacuum) | Atmospheric |
| **Temperature Range** | Any T below 300 K | 4 K to 100 K | 110 K to 300 K |
| **Mechanical Robustness** | Fragile (single cavity) | Robust (powder packs) | Excellent |
| **Typical Application** | LHe dewars, MLI backup | LN₂ / LAr transport dewars | LNG transport, building cold boxes |
| **Main Weakness** | Radiation unless MLI added | Packing uniformity | Cracks at very low T |

**Summary Selection Rule:**
- Use **MLI + vacuum** for T < 20 K.
- Use **perlite + vacuum** for 77 K storage (cost-effective compromise).
- Use **PUF foam** for LNG (111 K) where atmospheric operation is acceptable.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Losing Mistakes in KTU Valuation (Insulation Questions):**
>
> 1. **Writing "vacuum insulation" and stopping there** — Examiners expect you to state that vacuum ALONE does NOT block radiation; you must either add MLI shields or note the residual radiation heat transfer explicitly. **(Loses ~ 2 marks)**
>
> 2. **Mixing up k_r and k_s units** — Always quote apparent conductivity in W/(m·K), NOT W/m²·K. The latter is **conductance** per unit area, not conductivity. **(Loses ~ 1 mark)**
>
> 3. **Forgetting that k_app is temperature- and pressure-dependent** — Writing "k_app of perlite is 0.001 W/m·K" without specifying conditions will lose you the **definition-of-apparent-conductivity** mark. **(Loses ~ 1 mark)**
>
> 4. **Not mentioning the Knudsen effect** when explaining why powder + vacuum is effective. Examiners specifically test whether students know that gas conduction drops due to Kn > 10, not just "because we used vacuum." **(Loses ~ 2 marks)**
>
> 5. **Wrong optimum layer calculation** — Confusing dC/dN = 0 with dQ/dN = 0. Optimize **conductance** (or heat leak), not the radiation term alone. **(Loses ~ 2 marks)**

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Master Before Exam:**

- ☐ **Apparent thermal conductivity** = k_s + k_g + k_r + k_c — additive superposition, all four terms add in parallel.
- ☐ **k_app is NOT a true material property** — it depends on T, P, geometry, ε.
- ☐ **Knudsen number Kn = λ / D_p** — governs the gas conduction regime.
- ☐ **Free-molecular regime (Kn > 10)**: k_g is linear in pressure; reducing P reduces k_g proportionally.
- ☐ **Radiation heat transfer** scales as **T⁴** in flux, **T³** in equivalent conductivity.
- ☐ **Vacuum alone does NOT eliminate radiation** — you must add MLI shields or low-ε surfaces.
- ☐ **MLI** = aluminized Mylar + spacer layers, operates under vacuum; reduces radiation by factor ≈ N.
- ☐ **N_opt for MLI** = √(C_rad / k_sp) — there IS an optimum layer count; more is not always better.
- ☐ **Powder insulation (perlite, silica aerogel)** — evacuated, low-density granular fill. Works via Knudsen effect on gas conduction and radiation scattering.
- ☐ **Foam insulation (PUF)** — atmospheric pressure, used only above ~ 110 K (LNG region). Cracks at 4 K.
- ☐ **Selection rule:** T < 20 K → MLI + vacuum (mandatory); 77 K → perlite + vacuum; LNG → foam.
- ☐ **Standard constants to memorize:** σ = 5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W/m²·K⁴, k_B = 1.381 × 10⁻²³ J/K.
- ☐ **Heat leak formula:** Q = k_app · A · ΔT / L.
- ☐ **Practical k_app benchmarks:** Air = 0.024; PUF = 0.022; Perlite (vacuum) = 0.001; Aerogel = 0.001; MLI = 0.0001 W/m·K.
- ☐ **Boil-off relation:** 1 W heat leak vaporizes ~ 1.5 L/hr of LN₂, ~ 1.1 L/hr of LHe.
- ☐ **Outer-to-inner area ratio** matters for radiation: k_r depends on (1/r_i + 1/r_o)⁻¹.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cryogenic Fluid Transfer Systems and Cryo Pumping
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET861 - CRYOGENIC ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Cryogenic Fluid Transfer Systems and Cryo Pumping

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 What is a Cryogenic Fluid Transfer System?

A **Cryogenic Fluid Transfer System (CFTS)** is an integrated assembly of insulated pipelines, valves, pumps, expansion joints, phase separators, vaporizers, instrumentation, and safety interlocks specifically engineered to transport cryogenic fluids (liquefied gases at temperatures below **120 K / −153 °C**) such as Liquid Nitrogen (LN₂), Liquid Oxygen (LOX), Liquid Hydrogen (LH₂), Liquid Helium (LHe), and Liquefied Natural Gas (LNG) between storage, process, and end-use points without violating strict thermodynamic, mechanical, and safety constraints.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Anchor:** A cryogenic fluid transfer system is *not* merely a "pipe carrying cold liquid." It is a tightly coupled **thermo-fluid-mechanical system** where the goal is the simultaneous management of **heat ingress**, **pressure drop**, **phase change**, **thermal contraction**, and **fluid cavitation** — all at temperatures where conventional engineering intuition fails.

### 1.2 The Three Sub-Blocks of Any Cryogenic Transfer System

1. **Transfer Line (Pipeline / Hose)** — The physical conduit carrying the fluid, engineered with multi-layer insulation (MLI), vacuum jackets, or foam insulation to suppress heat leak.
2. **Cryogenic Valves** — Extended-bonnet valves (to keep the packing at ambient temperature), relief valves, check valves, and bellow-sealed globe valves.
3. **Cryogenic Pumps** — Devices that add head (pressure energy) to the cryogenic liquid to overcome elevation head, friction losses, and back-pressure at delivery points.

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Insulated Drinking Straw on Steroids"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy:** Imagine you are trying to drink a chilled soft drink through a long plastic straw on a hot summer day. The longer the straw, the more the drink warms up before reaching your mouth, the carbonation (dissolved gas) escapes, and you have to suck harder. Now scale this up: instead of a soft drink, you are pumping **liquid oxygen at −183 °C** through hundreds of meters of pipe in a refinery. The "warming" is catastrophic (boil-off loss), the "carbonation escaping" is two-phase flow, and the "sucking harder" is the pump's NPSH (Net Positive Suction Head) problem.

In this analogy:
- The **straw** = transfer line
- The **cold drink** = cryogenic liquid (LN₂, LOX, LH₂)
- The **heat from the room** = parasitic heat leak (conduction, convection, radiation)
- The **bubbles forming** = boil-off gas (BOG)
- **Sucking harder** = the cryogenic pump overcoming net positive suction head limitations
- The **straw material** = vacuum-insulated, multi-layer insulated piping

---

## 1.4 What is Cryo-Pumping?

**Cryo-pumping** is the process of moving a cryogenic liquid from a low-pressure reservoir (e.g., a dewar at atmospheric pressure) to a higher-pressure delivery point using pumps specifically designed to operate in cryogenic temperature regimes. Because of the very low vapor pressure of cryogenic liquids and the catastrophic consequences of cavitation, these pumps have unique design constraints.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two Broad Families of Cryogenic Pumps:**
> 1. **Cryogenic Centrifugal Pumps** — Used for high-flow, low-to-medium head applications (e.g., LNG loading, large-scale LOX distribution).
> 2. **Cryogenic Positive Displacement Pumps** — Reciprocating (piston) or cryogenic cryopumps (used in vacuum technology, where gases are condensed on cold surfaces). Used for lower flow, high-head applications.

---

## 1.5 Why This Topic is Critical in the KTU 2024 Scheme

Cryogenic transfer and pumping lie at the heart of:
- Space launch vehicle fueling (LH₂/LOX loading)
- MRI magnet cooling (LHe transfer)
- LNG carrier vessels and regasification terminals
- Superconducting magnet cryostats
- Industrial gas pipelines

Loss of fluid, vapor locking, two-phase flow, or pump cavitation can lead to **shutdown of multimillion-dollar facilities**. Hence this module carries heavy weightage in KTU ESE (End Semester Examination).

---

## 1.6 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (Bold for Recall)

- Boiling point of **LN₂ = 77.36 K** (−195.79 °C)
- Boiling point of **LOX = 90.19 K** (−182.96 °C)
- Boiling point of **LH₂ = 20.27 K** (−252.88 °C)
- Boiling point of **LHe = 4.22 K** (−268.93 °C)
- Boiling point of **LNG (methane) = 111.67 K** (−161.48 °C)
- **NPSH margin** for cryogenic pumps ≥ **2–4 m of liquid**
- **Typical heat leak** in well-designed vacuum-insulated transfer lines: **1–5 W/m**

# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The "Cryogenic Pipeline Engineering Trinity"

Any cryogenic transfer line design must simultaneously satisfy three constraints. Failure of any one causes system failure:

### A. Thermal Constraint — Minimize Heat In-Leak
Heat entering through the line boils the liquid. The mass rate of boil-off is given by:

$$ \dot{m}_{BOG} = \frac{\dot{Q}_{leak}}{h_{fg}} $$

where:
- $\dot{m}_{BOG}$ = mass flow rate of boil-off gas (kg/s)
- $\dot{Q}_{leak}$ = total heat leak into the line (W)
- $h_{fg}$ = latent heat of vaporization at operating pressure (J/kg)

### B. Hydraulic Constraint — Manage Pressure Drop
The pressure drop along the line determines the required pump head. For cryogenic liquids, the two-phase correction must be applied if BOG is present:

$$ \Delta P_{total} = \Delta P_{friction} + \Delta P_{elevation} + \Delta P_{fittings} + \Delta P_{acceleration} $$

### C. Mechanical Constraint — Accommodate Thermal Contraction
A 30 m stainless steel line cooling from 300 K to 77 K contracts by:

$$ \Delta L = L_0 \, \alpha \, \Delta T $$

where $\alpha$ for SS304 = **16.6 × 10⁻⁶ /K**. For LN₂, this means ~110 mm contraction per 30 m run. Expansion loops or bellows are mandatory.

---

## 2.2 Modes of Heat Transfer into a Cryogenic Transfer Line

| Mode | Mechanism | Mitigation Strategy |
|---|---|---|
| **Conduction** | Through pipe wall and supports | Long supports, low-conductivity materials (FRP, GFRP), vapor-cooled leads |
| **Convection** | Residual gas in vacuum jacket | Maintain vacuum ≥ **10⁻³ Pa**, use molecular sieve getters |
| **Radiation** | Between warm outer wall and cold inner wall | Multi-Layer Insulation (MLI / superinsulation), 10–40 layers of aluminized Mylar |
| **Joule-Thomson Effect** | Pressure drop causes cooling, but also creates temperature gradients | Minimize throttling, use parallel redundant lines |

---

## 2.3 Types of Cryogenic Transfer Lines

### Type 1: Vacuum-Insulated Transfer Line (Most Common for LNG, LOX)
- **Inner pipe:** SS304L stainless steel carrying the cryogenic liquid.
- **Outer pipe:** Carbon steel or SS304, evacuated to ~10⁻³ Pa.
- **Intermediate supports:** Low-conductivity GFRP pucks.
- **MLI blanket:** Wrapped around inner pipe, 20–40 layers.
- **Heat leak target:** ≤ **2 W/m** for a 100 mm ID line.

### Type 2: Foam-Insulated Transfer Line
- Polyurethane (PUF) or polyisocyanurate foam directly sprayed on the cold pipe.
- Used for **LNG vapor lines** and short-distance utility cooling.
- Heat leak typically **8–15 W/m** — far higher than vacuum lines, but cheaper.

### Type 3: Vacuum + Powder + MLI (High-Performance, e.g., for LHe)
- Inner pipe wrapped with MLI, surrounded by perlite or glass powder under vacuum.
- Heat leak as low as **0.1–0.5 W/m** — essential for LHe transfer.

---

## 2.4 Cryogenic Valves — Key Design Features

A cryogenic valve differs from a normal valve in three critical ways:

1. **Extended Bonnet (Stem Extension):** Moves the packing box away from the cold fluid so that conventional PTFE or graphite packing can survive at near-ambient temperature, eliminating the risk of packing becoming brittle and leaking.
2. **Bellow Seal:** A welded metal bellow replaces the packing entirely, giving **zero fugitive emissions** — critical for LH₂ (explosive) and LHe (expensive).
3. **Body Material:** SS304 / SS316 or bronze body; austenitic stainless retains toughness at 4 K.

Common types:
- **Globe valve** (most common — throttling)
- **Ball valve** (on/off)
- **Check valve** (prevent backflow during pump trip)
- **Safety relief valve** (mandatory per ASME B31.3 / EN 13458)

---

## 2.5 Cryogenic Pumps — Theory and Classification

### Why Cryogenic Pumps are Different
At room temperature, water pumps are simple: the fluid has high vapor pressure and easy cavitation margins. At cryogenic temperatures, the fluid is a *thermodynamic bomb*:

- Vapor pressure of LN₂ at 77 K ≈ **101 kPa** (it is *already boiling*).
- Vapor pressure of LH₂ at 20 K ≈ **101 kPa**.
- **NPSH available** in a vented dewar is essentially the static head of the liquid, which is small.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **NPSH Rule:** A cryogenic centrifugal pump must always be **flooded** (submerged suction) or **pressurized** (the dewar is pressurized with the gas to push liquid into the pump). Running a cryogenic centrifugal pump dry for even 2 seconds can cause cavitation damage that destroys the impeller.

### Positive Displacement (P.D.) Cryogenic Pumps
- **Piston / Reciprocating pumps:** Used for LHe and LH₂ transfer. A reciprocating piston in a cold cylinder displaces a fixed volume per stroke.
- **Advantages:** Self-priming, can handle two-phase flow briefly, very high head capability (up to 100 bar).
- **Disadvantages:** Pulsating flow, requires cold seals.

### Centrifugal Cryogenic Pumps
- Used for **LNG, LOX, LN₂** at flow rates up to 500 m³/h and heads up to 200 m.
- **Submerged motor pump** — the pump and motor are in the cryogenic bath itself. The motor is enclosed in a hermetically sealed SS can filled with the same cryogenic liquid (or compatible inert gas).
- Advantages: Smooth flow, high reliability.
- Disadvantages: Cavitation-sensitive, requires flooded suction or pressurization.

---

> [!NOTE]
> **Table Note on Vertical Pipes:** The LaTeX vertical bar symbol `\|` is rendered as `\vert` to prevent markdown table breakage.

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Typical Values |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\dot{m}_{BOG} = \dot{Q}_{leak} / h_{fg}$ | Boil-off gas mass flow rate | kg/s; $h_{fg}$ for LN₂ ≈ 199 kJ/kg |
| 2 | $\dot{Q}_{cond,cyl} = \dfrac{2 \pi k L (T_h - T_c)}{\ln(r_o/r_i)}$ | Radial conduction through cylindrical pipe wall | W; $k_{SS304} \approx 0.3$ W/m·K at 77 K |
| 3 | $\dot{Q}_{rad} = \sigma \, A \, (T_h^4 - T_c^4) / \left(\dfrac{1}{\epsilon_h} + \dfrac{A_h}{A_c}\left(\dfrac{1}{\epsilon_c} - 1\right)\right)$ | Radiation heat transfer with emissivities | W; $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ W/m²K⁴ |
| 4 | $\dot{Q}_{conv,res} = \dfrac{k_{gas} \, A \, (T_h - T_c)}{d + \dfrac{2}{\alpha_1} + \dfrac{2}{\alpha_2}}$ | Residual gas conduction in vacuum annulus (Knudsen regime) | W; depends on residual gas pressure |
| 5 | $\Delta P_{friction} = f \dfrac{L}{D} \dfrac{\rho v^2}{2}$ | Darcy-Weisbach friction pressure drop | Pa |

| 7 | $NPSH_r = f(Q, N, impeller\ design)$ | Required NPSH (manufacturer curve) | m of liquid |
| 8 | $H_{pump} = \dfrac{\Delta P_{total}}{\rho g} + \Delta z$ | Total pump head | m |
| 9 | $\Delta L = L_0 \, \alpha \, \Delta T$ | Thermal contraction of pipe | m |
| 10 | $\dot{W}_{pump} = \rho g Q H / \eta$ | Pump hydraulic power | W; $\eta$ = 0.6–0.85 |
| 11 | $T_{J-T,exit} = T_{inlet} - \mu_{J-T} \Delta P$ | Joule-Thomson cooling across valve/throttle | K; $\mu_{J-T}$ for N₂ at 77 K ≈ −0.04 K/bar |
| 12 | $\dot{Q}_{MLI} = \dfrac{C \, A \, (T_h - T_c)}{N}$ | MLI heat leak (layer-density model) | W; $N$ = number of layers |

> [!IMPORTANT]

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

This system theory is used daily in:
- **ISRO** for cryogenic upper stage (CUS) engine fueling — every kg of LH₂/LOX saved in boil-off is payload gained.
- **LNG carrier ships** — managing BOG during 21-day voyages from Qatar to India.
- **CERN's LHC** — LHe distribution through 1.4 km of cryogenic lines at 1.9 K.
- **Hospital MRI suites** — LHe refill every 6–12 months through cryogenic transfer lines.

---

## 2.8 Selection Decision Flow (Engineering Logic)

```
Step 1: Is fluid He or H₂? 
        → Yes → Submerged centrifugal or piston pump + MLI line
        → No  → Go to Step 2
Step 2: Is flow > 50 m³/h? 
        → Yes → Submerged centrifugal pump + vacuum MLI line
        → No  → Go to Step 3
Step 3: Is delivery pressure > 5 bar?
        → Yes → Positive displacement pump + vacuum line
        → No  → Pressurize dewar with boil-off gas (no pump needed — economizer mode)
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Engineering Mistake:** Selecting a centrifugal pump without verifying **NPSH available ≥ NPSH required + 1 m safety margin**. This is the single most common cause of cryogenic pump failure in industry.

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Heat Leak Through a Vacuum-Insulated Pipe (Cylindrical Conduction)

We consider a straight cylindrical cryogenic pipe of inner radius $r_i$ and outer radius $r_o$, carrying LN₂ at temperature $T_c$, surrounded by ambient air at $T_h$. We wish to find the radial conductive heat leak per unit length.

### Step 1 — Start with Fourier's Law in Cylindrical Coordinates

For steady-state, one-dimensional radial conduction in a cylinder:

$$ \dot{Q}_r = -k \, A_r \, \dfrac{dT}{dr} = -k \, (2 \pi r L) \, \dfrac{dT}{dr} $$

### Step 2 — Separate Variables

Multiply both sides by $dr / r$:

$$ \dfrac{\dot{Q}_r}{2 \pi k L} \, \dfrac{dr}{r} = -dT $$

### Step 3 — Integrate from $r = r_i$ to $r = r_o$, and $T = T_c$ to $T = T_h$

$$ \dfrac{\dot{Q}_r}{2 \pi k L} \int_{r_i}^{r_o} \dfrac{dr}{r} = -\int_{T_c}^{T_h} dT $$

$$ \dfrac{\dot{Q}_r}{2 \pi k L} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{r_o}{r_i}\right) = (T_c - T_h) $$

### Step 4 — Solve for Heat Leak $\dot{Q}_r$

$$ \dot{Q}_r = \dfrac{2 \pi k L \, (T_h - T_c)}{\ln(r_o / r_i)} $$

### Step 5 — Compute Heat Leak per Unit Length

$$ \dfrac{\dot{Q}_r}{L} = \dfrac{2 \pi k \, (T_h - T_c)}{\ln(r_o / r_i)} \quad \left[ \dfrac{W}{m} \right] $$

### Step 6 — Numerical Illustration for LN₂

Given:
- $r_i = 25$ mm, $r_o = 30$ mm
- $k_{SS304} \approx 0.3$ W/m·K at 77 K
- $T_h = 300$ K, $T_c = 77$ K

$$ \dfrac{\dot{Q}}{L} = \dfrac{2 \pi (0.3) (300 - 77)}{\ln(30 / 25)} $$

$$ = \dfrac{2 \pi (0.3) (223)}{0.1823} $$

$$ = \dfrac{420.4}{0.1823} $$

$$ \approx 2306 \, \dfrac{W}{m} $$

> [!WARNING]
> **This is a colossal heat leak — over 2 kW per meter!** This illustrates exactly **why** a bare concentric pipe is unacceptable. We need **vacuum + MLI** to drop this to < 2 W/m, a 1000-fold reduction.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Boil-Off Gas (BOG) Rate in a Cryogenic Storage Tank

A vertical dewar of LN₂ has:
- Heat leak $\dot{Q}_{leak}$ entering through walls, supports, and piping.
- Liquid mass $m_L$ at temperature $T_{LN_2}$.

We want the **boil-off rate** $\dot{m}_{BOG}$ in kg/s and the **time to empty** the tank.

### Step 1 — Energy Balance on the Liquid

Heat entering the liquid is consumed entirely as latent heat of vaporization (assuming negligible sensible heating):

$$ \dot{Q}_{leak} = \dot{m}_{BOG} \cdot h_{fg} $$

### Step 2 — Solve for BOG Rate

$$ \dot{m}_{BOG} = \dfrac{\dot{Q}_{leak}}{h_{fg}} $$

For LN₂: $h_{fg} \approx 199 \, kJ/kg = 1.99 \times 10^5 \, J/kg$.

If $\dot{Q}_{leak} = 50 \, W$ (a typical value for a 1000 L dewar):

$$ \dot{m}_{BOG} = \dfrac{50}{1.99 \times 10^5} = 2.51 \times 10^{-4} \, kg/s $$

$$ = 0.904 \, kg/hr $$

### Step 3 — Convert to Volumetric Boil-Off Gas at NTP

Using ideal gas law at NTP ($P = 101.325$ kPa, $T = 293$ K, $R_{specific,N_2} = 296.8$ J/kg·K):

$$ \dot{V}_{BOG} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{BOG} \, R \, T}{P} $$

$$ = \dfrac{(2.51 \times 10^{-4}) (296.8) (293)}{101325} $$

$$ = 2.15 \times 10^{-4} \, m^3/s = 0.774 \, m^3/hr $$

### Step 4 — Time to Empty

If the dewar holds $m_L = 800$ kg of LN₂:

$$ t_{empty} = \dfrac{m_L}{\dot{m}_{BOG}} = \dfrac{800}{2.51 \times 10^{-4}} \, s = 3.19 \times 10^6 \, s \approx 36.9 \, days $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This 36-day figure is the standard marketing number for a well-designed 1000 L LN₂ dewar. A poorly insulated one might last only 5 days.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: NPSH Available at the Pump Suction

A centrifugal LOX pump draws from a pressurized dewar. Calculate $NPSH_a$.

### Given
- Suction pipe diameter $D = 50$ mm
- LOX density $\rho = 1140$ kg/m³
- Vapor pressure of LOX at 90 K: $P_v = 101$ kPa
- Dewar pressure (above liquid): $P_{tank} = 200$ kPa (gauge + atmospheric)
- Suction velocity $v = 1.5$ m/s
- Static head of liquid above pump inlet: $h_s = 1.2$ m

### Step 1 — Convert Pressures to Absolute

$P_{tank,abs} = 200 + 101.325 = 301.325$ kPa
$P_{v,abs} = 101$ kPa

### Step 2 — Static Pressure at Pump Inlet

The pressure at the pump inlet is the tank pressure minus any line loss (assume lossless for simplicity):

$$ P_{suction} \approx P_{tank,abs} + \rho g h_s $$

$$ = 301325 + 1140 \times 9.81 \times 1.2 $$

$$ = 301325 + 13421 $$

$$ = 314746 \, Pa \approx 314.7 \, kPa $$

### Step 3 — NPSH Available Formula

$$ NPSH_a = \dfrac{P_{suction} - P_v}{\rho g} + \dfrac{v^2}{2g} $$

$$ = \dfrac{(314746 - 101000)}{1140 \times 9.81} + \dfrac{(1.5)^2}{2 \times 9.81} $$

$$ = \dfrac{213746}{11183} + 0.1147 $$

$$ = 19.11 + 0.115 $$

$$ = 19.22 \, m \text{ of LOX} $$

### Step 4 — Check Against Pump NPSH Required

If the pump manufacturer specifies $NPSH_r = 4$ m at the duty point:

$$ NPSH_{margin} = NPSH_a - NPSH_r = 19.22 - 4.0 = 15.22 \, m $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Margin is huge — pump will run cavitation-free.** In cryogenic practice, a margin of at least **1–3 m** is mandatory; we are well above that. If $NPSH_a$ were < $NPSH_r$, the pump would cavitate within seconds.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Total Pump Head for Cryogenic Transfer

A cryogenic pump must deliver LN₂ from a storage tank to a process user located 8 m higher and 120 m away, with flow $Q = 10$ m³/hr.

### Given
- $\rho_{LN_2} = 808$ kg/m³ at 77 K saturated
- $\mu_{LN_2} = 1.55 \times 10^{-4}$ Pa·s (dynamic viscosity at 77 K)
- Pipe ID = 40 mm, roughness $\varepsilon = 0.05$ mm
- 4 standard 90° elbows, 2 gate valves

### Step 1 — Convert Flow to Velocity

$$ Q = 10 \, m^3/hr = \dfrac{10}{3600} = 2.778 \times 10^{-3} \, m^3/s $$

$$ A = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi (0.040)^2}{4} = 1.257 \times 10^{-3} \, m^2 $$

$$ v = \dfrac{Q}{A} = \dfrac{2.778 \times 10^{-3}}{1.257 \times 10^{-3}} = 2.21 \, m/s $$

### Step 2 — Reynolds Number

$$ Re = \dfrac{\rho v D}{\mu} = \dfrac{808 \times 2.21 \times 0.040}{1.55 \times 10^{-4}} $$

$$ = \dfrac{71.43}{1.55 \times 10^{-4}} = 4.61 \times 10^5 $$

Turbulent flow.

### Step 3 — Friction Factor via Colebrook-White

Relative roughness $\varepsilon/D = 0.05/40 = 0.00125$.

For turbulent flow in smooth-to-rough transition, use Swamee-Jain explicit formula:

$$ f = \dfrac{0.25}{\left[\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{\varepsilon/D}{3.7} + \dfrac{5.74}{Re^{0.9}}\right)\right]^2} $$

$$ = \dfrac{0.25}{\left[\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{0.00125}{3.7} + \dfrac{5.74}{(4.61 \times 10^5)^{0.9}}\right)\right]^2} $$

$$ (4.61 \times 10^5)^{0.9} \approx 1.04 \times 10^5 $$

$$ \dfrac{5.74}{1.04 \times 10^5} \approx 5.52 \times 10^{-5} $$

$$ \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{0.00125}{3.7} + 5.52 \times 10^{-5}\right) = \log_{10}(3.378 \times 10^{-4} + 5.52 \times 10^{-5}) $$

$$ = \log_{10}(3.93 \times 10^{-4}) = -3.406 $$

$$ f = \dfrac{0.25}{(-3.406)^2} = \dfrac{0.25}{11.60} = 0.02155 $$

### Step 4 — Friction Loss

$$ \Delta P_{fric} = f \dfrac{L}{D} \dfrac{\rho v^2}{2} $$

$$ = 0.02155 \times \dfrac{120}{0.040} \times \dfrac{808 \times (2.21)^2}{2} $$

$$ = 0.02155 \times 3000 \times \dfrac{808 \times 4.884}{2} $$

$$ = 64.65 \times 1973.3 $$

$$ = 127576 \, Pa \approx 127.6 \, kPa $$

### Step 5 — Minor Losses

For 4 elbows ($K = 0.9$ each) + 2 gate valves ($K = 0.15$ each):

$$ \Sigma K = 4(0.9) + 2(0.15) = 3.9 $$

$$ \Delta P_{minor} = \Sigma K \cdot \dfrac{\rho v^2}{2} = 3.9 \times 1973.3 = 7696 \, Pa \approx 7.7 \, kPa $$

### Step 6 — Elevation Head

$$ \Delta P_{elev} = \rho g \Delta z = 808 \times 9.81 \times 8 = 63,403 \, Pa \approx 63.4 \, kPa $$

### Step 7 — Total Pressure Rise Required

$$ \Delta P_{total} = 127.6 + 7.7 + 63.4 = 198.7 \, kPa $$

### Step 8 — Convert to Pump Head

$$ H_{pump} = \dfrac{\Delta P_{total}}{\rho g} = \dfrac{198700}{808 \times 9.81} = \dfrac{198700}{7927} = 25.07 \, m $$

### Step 9 — Pump Hydraulic Power

$$ \dot{W} = \dfrac{\rho g Q H}{\eta} = \dfrac{7927 \times 2.778 \times 10^{-3} \times 25.07}{0.75} $$

$$ = \dfrac{551.8}{0.75} = 735.7 \, W \approx 0.74 \, kW $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** A 1 kW cryogenic pump can move 10 m³/hr of LN₂ across 120 m at 8 m elevation. The same duty with water would require 1.2 kW, but with LN₂ the lower density reduces head at the same pressure — an interesting cryogenic-specific observation.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Summary of Cryogenic Pumps

| Parameter | Centrifugal Cryogenic | Piston / Reciprocating | Submerged Electric |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Typical flow** | 20–500 m³/hr | 1–30 m³/hr | 5–100 m³/hr |
| **Head capability** | 20–200 m | 100–1000 m | 10–150 m |
| **NPSH sensitivity** | High | Low | Low (flooded suction) |
| **Two-phase tolerance** | Very poor | Moderate | Poor |
| **Suitable fluids** | LN₂, LOX, LNG | LHe, LH₂, LN₂ | LN₂, LOX |
| **Typical η** | 70–82% | 60–75% | 65–80% |
| **Maintenance access** | External — easy | Hermetic — hard | Hermetic — hard |
| **Cost index** | Medium | High | High |
| **Vibration level** | Low | High | Negligible |

---

## 3.6 Derivation 5: Thermal Contraction of a Cryogenic Piping Run

A 30 m long SS304 transfer line cools from 300 K to 77 K (LN₂). Find total contraction and design an expansion loop.

### Step 1 — Mean Coefficient of Thermal Expansion

For SS304 between 300 K and 77 K, the integrated mean coefficient is:

$$ \bar{\alpha} = \dfrac{\Delta L / L_0}{\Delta T} = \dfrac{(L_{300} - L_{77})}{L_{300} \times (300 - 77)} $$

Tabulated value: $\bar{\alpha}_{304,300 \to 77} \approx 2.85 \times 10^{-3}$ over the full range. Per Kelvin: $\alpha_{eff} = 2.85 \times 10^{-3} / 223 = 1.28 \times 10^{-5}$ /K (this is the **integrated mean** — used as a single value).

### Step 2 — Total Contraction

$$ \Delta L = L_0 \cdot \alpha_{eff} \cdot \Delta T $$

$$ = 30 \times 1.28 \times 10^{-5} \times 223 $$

$$ = 30 \times 2.85 \times 10^{-3} $$

$$ = 0.0856 \, m = 85.6 \, mm $$

### Step 3 — Expansion Loop Sizing

A standard U-shaped expansion loop absorbs axial contraction of ~50 mm per leg. To absorb 85.6 mm, design a **two-leg loop** with each leg 1.5 m long:

$$ \delta_{loop} = \dfrac{3 \Delta L \cdot L_{leg}^2}{W^2} \quad \text{(flexibility factor formula)} $$

Solving for width $W$ given $\delta_{loop} = 85.6$ mm, $L_{leg} = 1500$ mm:

$$ W = \sqrt{\dfrac{3 \times 85.6 \times (1500)^2}{85.6}} $$

$$ = \sqrt{3 \times 2.25 \times 10^6} = \sqrt{6.75 \times 10^6} $$

$$ \approx 2598 \, mm \approx 2.6 \, m \text{ wide loop} $$

> [!NOTE]
> A 2.6 m wide expansion loop is needed for a 30 m line. Alternatively, install **bellow expansion joints** at 6 m intervals, each rated for ±20 mm axial movement.

---

## 3.7 Derivation 6: Two-Phase Pressure Drop Correction (Homogeneous Model)

When BOG coexists with liquid in the transfer line (always to some extent), the two-phase pressure drop exceeds the single-phase liquid pressure drop.

### Lockhart-Martinelli Two-Phase Multiplier

Define the liquid-only pressure drop $\Delta P_L$ and gas-only pressure drop $\Delta P_G$ at the same mass flow rates. The Martinelli parameter is:

$$ X^2 = \dfrac{\Delta P_L}{\Delta P_G} $$

The two-phase pressure drop is:

$$ \Delta P_{TP} = \phi_L^2 \cdot \Delta P_L $$

where for turbulent-turbulent flow:

$$ \phi_L^2 = 1 + \dfrac{C}{X} + \dfrac{1}{X^2} $$

with $C \approx 8$ for turbulent-turbulent.

### Numerical Example
Given $\Delta P_L = 100$ kPa and $\Delta P_G = 50$ kPa:

$$ X^2 = 100 / 50 = 2 \Rightarrow X = \sqrt{2} = 1.414 $$

$$ \phi_L^2 = 1 + \dfrac{8}{1.414} + \dfrac{1}{2} = 1 + 5.657 + 0.5 = 7.157 $$

$$ \Delta P_{TP} = 7.157 \times 100 = 715.7 \, kPa $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This 7-fold increase in pressure drop** is why **cryogenic transfer lines are designed with a quality (vapor mass fraction) target of x < 0.05** — every 1% of vapor can multiply line pressure drop by 5× or more. BOG management is not just an economic issue; it is a hydraulic issue too.

# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Cryogenic Fluid Transfer System

The following Mermaid diagram maps the functional interaction between every component in a complete cryogenic fluid transfer train. Node IDs are alphanumeric to comply with the Mermaid compilation safeguard.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA["STORAGE TANK OR DEWAR<br/>PRESSURISED CRYOGENIC LIQUID RESERVOIR"]
    nodeB["SUCTION LINE<br/>VACUUM INSULATED MLI PIPING"]
    nodeC["ISOLATION VALVE<br/>CRYOGENIC EXTENDED BONNET GLOBE"]
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Q:** Define the term *Net Positive Suction Head (NPSH)* and explain why it is a critical parameter in cryogenic pump selection.

**Model Answer:**
NPSH is the total head available at the pump suction above the vapor pressure of the liquid, expressed in metres of liquid. It comprises the static pressure head, the velocity head, and the elevation head, minus the vapor pressure head.

$$ NPSH_a = \dfrac{P_{suction} - P_v}{\rho g} + \dfrac{v^2}{2g} $$

In cryogenic pumps, NPSH is critical because cryogenic liquids such as LN₂ and LOX have very high vapor pressures at their storage temperature — LN₂ at 77 K has a vapor pressure of approximately 101 kPa (it is already at atmospheric boiling). If NPSH available at the pump suction falls below NPSH required by the manufacturer, the liquid flashes to vapor at the impeller eye, causing **cavitation**, which destroys the impeller within seconds and results in loss of prime, vibration, and noise. Therefore, flooded suction, pressurised dewars, or sub-cooled operation are mandatory design practices for cryogenic pumps.

**Mark Split:** Definition — 1 mark; formula — 1 mark; cryogenic relevance with cavitation discussion — 1 mark.

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Q:** Differentiate between vacuum-insulated and foam-insulated cryogenic transfer lines. State two advantages and two disadvantages of each.

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Vacuum-Insulated Line | Foam-Insulated Line |
|---|---|---|
| **Insulation medium** | Vacuum + MLI blanket | Polyurethane / polyisocyanurate foam |
| **Heat leak** | 1–5 W/m | 8–15 W/m |
| **Cost** | High (vacuum + MLI) | Low |
| **Weight** | Higher (concentric pipe) | Lower |
| **Maintenance** | Harder (vacuum integrity) | Easier |
| **Best application** | Long-distance LNG, LH₂, LHe | Short LNG lines, vapor service |

**Vacuum-Insulated Advantages:**
1. Very low heat leak — minimal BOG.
2. Suitable for ultra-low temperature fluids (LHe at 4 K).

**Vacuum-Insulated Disadvantages:**
1. Expensive initial cost.
2. Vacuum integrity can fail; requires periodic re-evacuation.

**Foam-Insulated Advantages:**
1. Low cost, simple installation.
2. No vacuum maintenance required.

**Foam-Insulated Disadvantages:**
1. Higher heat leak — significant BOG.
2. Cannot be used for sub-77 K applications; foam becomes brittle.

**Mark Split:** Comparison table — 2 marks; two advantages + two disadvantages — 1 mark.

---

## 5.2 PART B QUESTIONS — INTERNAL CHOICE (14 Marks Each)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]

**Q:** A cryogenic liquid nitrogen transfer system is to be designed for an industrial gas plant. The system specifications are:
- Storage: 5000 L horizontal dewar at 77 K, 1.2 bar absolute
- Delivery: 200 m horizontal distance, 5 m elevation rise
- Required flow: 8 m³/hr of LN₂
- Pipe: SS304, ID = 35 mm, wall thickness 3 mm
- LN₂ density ρ = 808 kg/m³, dynamic viscosity μ = 1.55 × 10⁻⁴ Pa·s
- Heat leak into the line is 80 W total

**(a)** Calculate the boil-off gas rate in kg/hr and the corresponding volumetric BOG flow at NTP. (7 marks)

**(b)** Compute the pump head required, assuming the equivalent length of fittings is 25 m of straight pipe. Take Darcy friction factor f = 0.022. (7 marks)

---

#### Solution to Part (a):

**Step 1:** Identify latent heat of vaporization of LN₂ at 77 K:
$$ h_{fg} = 199 \, kJ/kg = 1.99 \times 10^5 \, J/kg $$

**Step 2:** Apply the BOG formula:
$$ \dot{m}_{BOG} = \dfrac{\dot{Q}_{leak}}{h_{fg}} = \dfrac{80}{1.99 \times 10^5} = 4.02 \times 10^{-4} \, kg/s $$

**Step 3:** Convert to kg/hr:
$$ \dot{m}_{BOG} = 4.02 \times 10^{-4} \times 3600 = 1.447 \, kg/hr $$

**Step 4:** Volumetric BOG at NTP (T = 293 K, P = 101.325 kPa, R = 296.8 J/kg·K for N₂):
$$ \dot{V}_{BOG} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{BOG} R T}{P} = \dfrac{(1.447 / 3600) \times 296.8 \times 293}{101325} $$

$$ = \dfrac{(4.02 \times 10^{-4}) \times 86962}{101325} $$

$$ = \dfrac{34.96}{101325} = 3.45 \times 10^{-4} \, m^3/s = 1.24 \, m^3/hr $$

**Result:**
- $\dot{m}_{BOG} = 1.45 \, kg/hr$
- $\dot{V}_{BOG} = 1.24 \, m^3/hr$

---

#### Solution to Part (b):

**Step 1:** Convert flow to SI:
$$ Q = 8 \, m^3/hr = 2.222 \times 10^{-3} \, m^3/s $$

**Step 2:** Pipe cross-section and velocity:
$$ A = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi (0.035)^2}{4} = 9.621 \times 10^{-4} \, m^2 $$

$$ v = \dfrac{Q}{A} = \dfrac{2.222 \times 10^{-3}}{9.621 \times 10^{-4}} = 2.31 \, m/s $$

**Step 3:** Equivalent length:
$$ L_{eq} = 200 + 25 = 225 \, m $$

**Step 4:** Friction head loss:
$$ \Delta P_{fric} = f \cdot \dfrac{L}{D} \cdot \dfrac{\rho v^2}{2} = 0.022 \times \dfrac{225}{0.035} \times \dfrac{808 \times (2.31)^2}{2} $$

$$ = 0.022 \times 6428.6 \times \dfrac{808 \times 5.336}{2} $$

$$ = 141.4 \times 2155.8 = 304{,}930 \, Pa = 304.9 \, kPa $$

**Step 5:** Elevation head:
$$ \Delta P_{elev} = \rho g \Delta z = 808 \times 9.81 \times 5 = 39{,}632 \, Pa = 39.6 \, kPa $$

**Step 6:** Total pump pressure rise:
$$ \Delta P_{total} = 304.9 + 39.6 = 344.5 \, kPa $$

**Step 7:** Convert to head:
$$ H_{pump} = \dfrac{\Delta P_{total}}{\rho g} = \dfrac{344500}{808 \times 9.81} = \dfrac{344500}{7926.5} = 43.46 \, m $$

**Result:** Pump head ≈ **43.5 m of LN₂**

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]

**Q:** Write technical notes on:
**(a)** Design considerations for cryogenic valves — explain the need for an extended bonnet, bellow seal construction, and body material selection. List four types of cryogenic valves with their applications. (7 marks)

**(b)** Explain the classification, working principle, and selection criteria of cryogenic centrifugal pumps with a neat sketch of a submerged motor pump. Why are centrifugal pumps preferred for LNG and LOX service? (7 marks)

---

#### Solution to Part (a):

**Extended Bonnet:**
The bonnet of a cryogenic valve is extended (typically 200–500 mm) so that the packing box remains at near-ambient temperature. This is essential because:
- PTFE or graphite packing becomes brittle and loses sealing capability below −30 °C.
- Ice formation at the packing would prevent stem movement.
- Heat conducted along the stem is intercepted by the extended section before reaching the packing.

**Bellow Seal:**
A welded metal bellow (typically SS316L) replaces the packing entirely:
- Provides zero fugitive emissions — critical for LH₂ (explosive) and LHe (costly).
- Eliminates stem leakage path completely.
- The bellow is welded to the stem and the bonnet, providing a hermetic seal.

**Body Material:**
Austenitic stainless steel (SS304 / SS316) or bronze is selected because:
- Retains impact toughness down to 4 K (does not undergo brittle fracture like carbon steel).
- Corrosion-resistant against moisture condensation during cool-down.
- Compatible with high-purity oxygen service (LOX) — no organic contaminants.

**Four Types of Cryogenic Valves:**

| Type | Application |
|---|---|
| **Extended-bonnet globe valve** | Throttling and isolation in main transfer lines |
| **Ball valve** | Quick on/off service, minimum pressure drop |
| **Check valve** | Prevent reverse flow on pump trip |
| **Safety relief valve** | Over-pressure protection per ASME BPVC |

---

#### Solution to Part (b):

**Classification of Cryogenic Centrifugal Pumps:**

1. **Submerged motor pump** — pump and motor both inside the cryogenic bath.
2. **External motor, canned pump** — motor outside, but the wetted end has a hermetic can.
3. **Inline centrifugal pump** — installed in the transfer line outside the tank.

**Working Principle:**
The impeller rotates at high speed (typically 2950 rpm), imparting kinetic energy to the liquid. The volute casing then converts this kinetic energy into pressure energy. The fluid exits the volute at higher pressure and discharges into the delivery line.

**Selection Criteria:**

| Criterion | Typical Value for Cryogenic |
|---|---|
| NPSH margin | ≥ 2 m above manufacturer requirement |
| Head per stage | 20–80 m |
| Specific speed $N_s$ | 500–3000 (SI) for radial flow |
| Material | SS304/316 impeller, Inconel shaft |
| Seal type | Magnetic drive or canned motor |

**Why Centrifugal Pumps are Preferred for LNG and LOX:**
1. **High flow capability** — up to 500 m³/hr matches the throughput of large storage tanks.
2. **Smooth, pulsation-free flow** — better for sensitive downstream instrumentation.
3. **Hermetically sealed** — no leakage of expensive or hazardous fluids.
4. **Easy maintenance** — modular construction.
5. **Good efficiency** — 70–82% in the design range.

**Sketch Description of Submerged Motor Pump:**

```
       ┌─────────────────────────┐
       │   CRYOGENIC LIQUID      │
       │      IN STORAGE         │
       │                         │
       │   ┌───────────┐         │
       │   │  MOTOR    │  ← Top  │
       │   │ (Hermetic)│         │
       │   └─────┬─────┘         │
       │         │ shaft        │
       │   ┌─────┴─────┐         │
       │   │ IMPELLER  │  ← Bot  │
       │   └─────┬─────┘         │
       │         ↓               │
       │      DISCHARGE          │
       └─────────────────────────┘
```

**Mark Distribution for Part B:**
- (a) Extended bonnet 2M, bellow 2M, material 1M, four valves 2M = 7M
- (b) Classification 2M, working 2M, selection 1M, sketch + reasoning 2M = 7M

---

## 5.3 KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING / PITFALL CALLOUT

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in KTU Board Evaluation:**

1. **Forgetting Unit Conversions:** Students write $h_{fg} = 199$ kJ/kg but plug into formula with $J/kg$, getting a BOG rate off by a factor of 1000. Always check units explicitly.

2. **Static Head vs Pressure Head Confusion:** For cryogenic liquids, density is ~1000× less than steel density for structural calculations but the *flow density* used in pump head conversion is ~800 kg/m³, NOT 1000 kg/m³ (which is water). Using water density for LN₂ pump head calculation is a guaranteed 2-mark deduction.

3. **NPSH Without Vapor Pressure Correction:** Students often forget to subtract $P_v$ in the NPSH formula. For LN₂, since $P_v \approx P_{atm}$, failing to subtract it makes $NPSH_a$ appear artificially large and gives wrong safety conclusions.

4. **Using Atmospheric Pressure Instead of Absolute in Suction Calculation:** The dewar pressure must be added to atmospheric if given as gauge. Students using gauge pressure directly get $NPSH_a$ that is off by 10 m.

5. **Ignoring Friction Factor Variation:** Using $f = 0.02$ as a "round number" without verifying Reynolds number regime or roughness. If the actual $f = 0.025$, pump head is underestimated by 25%.

6. **Two-Phase Pressure Drop Ignored:** Many students compute single-phase pressure drop only. In real cryogenic systems with even 1–2% vapor, two-phase multiplier $\phi_L^2$ can be 5–10×.

7. **No Sketch in Descriptive Answers:** KTU examiners routinely allocate 2–3 marks for a labelled sketch in descriptive 14-mark questions. A well-labelled block diagram earns marks even if text explanation is brief.

8. **Not Verifying Cavitation Margin:** Students compute pump head but skip NPSH verification. KTU specifically tests this — losing 2 marks per question if skipped.

---

## 5.4 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick-Fire Recall — Read This Before Walking Into the Exam Hall:**

### A. Must-Know Definitions (1-line each)
- ✅ Cryogenic Fluid Transfer System: pipeline + valves + pumps for sub-120 K fluids
- ✅ NPSH: suction head above vapor pressure, expressed in metres of liquid
- ✅ Cavitation: vapor bubble formation and collapse at pump impeller eye
- ✅ BOG: Boil-off gas, formed by heat leak into cryogenic liquid
- ✅ MLI: Multi-Layer Insulation, 10–40 layers of aluminised Mylar
- ✅ Extended Bonnet: valve stem extension to keep packing above freezing
- ✅ Bellow Seal: hermetic metal seal replacing packing — zero leakage
- ✅ Submerged Pump: pump-motor assembly inside the cryogenic tank
- ✅ Economiser Mode: using BOG to pressurise the storage tank to drive flow without a pump
- ✅ Thermal Contraction: pipe shortening on cooling, requiring expansion loops

### B. Must-Know Numerical Formulas
- ✅ $\dot{m}_{BOG} = \dot{Q}_{leak} / h_{fg}$
- ✅ $\Delta P_{fric} = f \dfrac{L}{D} \dfrac{\rho v^2}{2}$
- ✅ $NPSH_a = \dfrac{P_{suct} - P_v}{\rho g} + \dfrac{v^2}{2g}$
- ✅ $H_{pump} = \dfrac{\Delta P_{total}}{\rho g}$
- ✅ $\dot{W}_{pump} = \rho g Q H / \eta$
- ✅ $\Delta L = L_0 \alpha \Delta T$
- ✅ $\phi_L^2 = 1 + C/X + 1/X^2$ (two-phase multiplier)

### C. Must-Know Numerical Values
- ✅ LN₂ boiling point: 77 K
- ✅ LOX boiling point: 90 K
- ✅ LH₂ boiling point: 20 K
- ✅ LHe boiling point: 4.2 K
- ✅ Vacuum target for insulation: < 10⁻³ Pa
- ✅ MLI layer count: 20–40
- ✅ Heat leak target: < 2 W/m (vacuum MLI), < 15 W/m (foam)
- ✅ Typical pump efficiency: 70–82%
- ✅ Typical NPSH margin: ≥ 2 m

### D. Must-Know Comparisons (High-Yield for 14-mark questions)
- ✅ Centrifugal vs Positive Displacement cryogenic pumps
- ✅ Vacuum-insulated vs Foam-insulated transfer lines
- ✅ Globe valve vs Ball valve vs Check valve vs Relief valve
- ✅ Submerged vs Inline cryogenic pumps

### E. Common Pitfalls to Avoid (Repeated from §5.3 for emphasis)
- ❌ Confusing gauge vs absolute pressure in NPSH calculations
- ❌ Using water density for cryogenic fluids
- ❌ Forgetting two-phase pressure drop correction
- ❌ Omitting the labelled sketch in descriptive answers
- ❌ Not verifying $NPSH_a > NPSH_r$ before pump selection

### F. KTU-Examiner Perspective — What Gets Full Marks
- ✅ Stating the formula before plugging in numbers
- ✅ Showing every algebraic step (no skipping)
- ✅ Boxing the final answer with units
- ✅ Including a labelled diagram even in numerical questions
- ✅ Discussing the engineering implication (e.g., "margin is safe because…")

> **End of Module 4 Topic Notes — Cryogenic Fluid Transfer Systems and Cryo Pumping**

topics.title: Cryogenic Instrumentation - Pressure Measurement (McLeod Gauge, Pirani Gauge, and Penning Gauge)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET861 - CRYOGENIC ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Cryogenic Instrumentation - Pressure Measurement (McLeod Gauge, Pirani Gauge, and Penning Gauge)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Pressure Measurement in Cryogenic Systems

In cryogenic engineering, accurate measurement of very low pressures (vacuum) is essential for the proper insulation of cryostats, transfer lines, and storage tanks. Pressure measurement in cryogenics typically deals with **ultra-high vacuum (UHV)** and **high vacuum (HV)** ranges, often spanning **10⁻³ torr to 10⁻⁷ torr** (1 torr = **133.322 Pa**).

The three primary instruments used are:
- **McLeod Gauge** – A primary, absolute manometer based on Boyle's Law.
- **Pirani Gauge** – A secondary gauge based on thermal conductivity.
- **Penning Gauge** – A secondary ionization gauge based on cold-cathode discharge in crossed E and B fields.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Cryogenic pressure gauges must operate reliably at room temperature while measuring the vacuum in the cold zones indirectly. They form the backbone of vacuum integrity testing in liquefied gas storage.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

- **McLeod Gauge** is like squeezing a large balloon of gas into a tiny straw. By knowing how much you squeezed (volume ratio) and how high the mercury rises (pressure), you can back-calculate the original pressure — just like Boyle's Law.
- **Pirani Gauge** is like a candle in a closed room. When air gets thinner (low pressure), the candle cools slowly because fewer molecules carry heat away. By measuring the wire temperature, we estimate the pressure.
- **Penning Gauge** is like a magnetron in a microwave oven — electrons get trapped in spiraling paths by crossed electric and magnetic fields, ionizing gas molecules and creating a measurable current proportional to pressure.

### 1.3 Range Comparison (Important Metric)

> [!NOTE]
> **Pressure Ranges for Cryogenic Vacuum Gauges**
> - **McLeod Gauge:** **10⁻¹ to 10⁻⁶ torr** (Primary standard)
> - **Pirani Gauge:** **10⁻⁴ to 1 torr** (Thermal conductivity based)
> - **Penning Gauge:** **10⁻³ to 10⁻⁷ torr** (Cold cathode ionization)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 McLeod Gauge — Operating Principle

The McLeod Gauge compresses a known volume of low-pressure gas into a smaller known volume, allowing measurement through mercury level comparison.

**Step-by-Step Operating Logic:**

1. The gauge is initially leveled with the gas at unknown pressure **P**.
2. Mercury is raised into a closed capillary by either tilting or by an external reservoir.
3. A volume **V** of gas at low pressure is trapped between the closed end of the capillary and the mercury.
4. This gas is compressed into the closed capillary (volume **V_c**) at a higher pressure.
5. The mercury level in an open comparison capillary indicates the difference.

**Key Definitions:**
- **Closed capillary:** Captures and compresses the trapped gas.
- **Open comparison capillary:** Shows the original pressure level reference.
- **Height difference (h):** Difference in mercury levels between capillaries.

### 2.2 McLeod Gauge — The Fundamental Equation

By Boyle's Law (P₁V₁ = P₂V₂):

$$P \cdot V = (P + h) \cdot V_c$$

Since **h >> P** at high vacuum:

$$P \approx \frac{h \cdot V_c}{V}$$

For a capillary of cross-section **a** (so $V_c = a \cdot h$):

$$P = \frac{a^2 \cdot h^2}{V}$$

This is the **Boyle's Law form of the McLeod Gauge equation**.

### 2.3 Pirani Gauge — Operating Principle

A heated filament in a Wheatstone bridge circuit loses heat to surrounding gas molecules. As pressure decreases, fewer molecules are available to carry heat away, so the filament temperature (and resistance) increases.

**Operating Logic Steps:**

1. A tungsten or platinum filament is heated to a temperature slightly above ambient.
2. The filament forms one arm of a **Wheatstone bridge**.
3. The bridge is balanced at a known reference pressure.
4. As pressure changes, thermal conductivity changes, altering filament resistance.
5. The resulting bridge imbalance voltage is calibrated to indicate pressure.

**Heat Loss Equation:**

$$Q = k(T_f - T_w)$$

Where:
- $Q$ = Heat loss per unit length
- $k$ = Thermal conductivity of gas (pressure-dependent)
- $T_f$ = Filament temperature
- $T_w$ = Wall temperature

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Pirani gauge's output depends on the **type of gas** because thermal conductivity varies with molecular structure. It must be calibrated for each gas (e.g., N₂, He, H₂).

### 2.4 Penning Gauge — Operating Principle

The Penning gauge (cold cathode ionization gauge) uses crossed **electric and magnetic fields** to sustain a self-sustaining discharge (Penning discharge) in low-pressure gas.

**Operating Logic Steps:**

1. A high voltage (~2 kV) is applied between anode and cathode.
2. A magnetic field (B ~ 0.1 T) is applied perpendicular to the electric field.
3. Free electrons are accelerated by E but deflected by B into spiral paths.
4. The increased path length enhances ionization probability.
5. Positive ions collected at cathode produce an ion current proportional to gas pressure.

**Discharge Current Equation:**

$$I^+ = S \cdot P \cdot I_e$$

Where:
- $I^+$ = Ion current
- $S$ = Sensitivity (typically 10⁻² to 10⁻¹ torr⁻¹)
- $P$ = Pressure
- $I_e$ = Electron emission current

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Gauge Type | Governing Principle | Key Formula | Typical Range (torr) | Type |
|------------|--------------------|--------------|----------------------|------|
| McLeod | Boyle's Law | $P = \dfrac{a^2 h^2}{V}$ | $10^{-6}$ to $10^{-1}$ | Primary (Absolute) |
| Pirani | Thermal Conductivity | $Q = k(T_f - T_w)$ | $10^{-4}$ to $1$ | Secondary (Indirect) |
| Penning | Cold Cathode Ionization | $I^+ = S \cdot P \cdot I_e$ | $10^{-7}$ to $10^{-3}$ | Secondary (Indirect) |

### 2.6 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **McLeod Gauge:** Calibration standard for all vacuum gauges; used in cryostat vacuum integrity testing.
- **Pirani Gauge:** Continuous monitoring of rough-to-medium vacuum in LNG transfer lines and helium dewar evacuation.
- **Penning Gauge:** Monitoring the ultra-high vacuum in superconducting magnet cryostats, particle accelerators (LHC), and space simulation chambers.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: McLeod Gauge Pressure Equation

We need to derive the relationship between the unknown pressure **P** and the measurable height difference **h** in the McLeod gauge.

**Initial Conditions:**
- Volume of trapped gas at unknown pressure: **V**
- Initial pressure of trapped gas: **P** (the unknown we want to find)
- Cross-sectional area of capillary: **a**
- Closed capillary length above mercury after compression: **h_c** (let's denote total column in closed capillary as h)

**Step 1 — Apply Boyle's Law (Isothermal Compression)**

The compression is assumed isothermal because the process is slow and the gauge is at room temperature. Applying Boyle's Law between the initial state (large bulb at low pressure) and the final state (compressed gas in capillary):

$$P_1 V_1 = P_2 V_2$$

Substituting our variables:

$$P \cdot V = (P + h) \cdot V_c$$

**Step 2 — Express the Compressed Volume**

The compressed gas occupies the closed capillary of cross-sectional area **a** and length equal to the mercury rise height **h**:

$$V_c = a \cdot h$$

Therefore:

$$P \cdot V = (P + h) \cdot a \cdot h$$

**Step 3 — Simplify Using the Vacuum Approximation**

At cryogenic operating pressures, **P << h**, so we can approximate:

$$(P + h) \approx h$$

This is the **low-pressure approximation** — critical for KTU problems. Substituting:

$$P \cdot V = h \cdot a \cdot h$$

$$P \cdot V = a \cdot h^2$$

**Step 4 — Solve for P**

$$P = \frac{a \cdot h^2}{V}$$

Since **a** and **V** are geometric constants of the gauge, the pressure is directly proportional to **h²**.

$$\boxed{P = \frac{a^2 \cdot h^2}{V}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Many textbooks write $a^2$ in the formula because V is often expressed in terms of the capillary volume unit. Both forms are equivalent depending on how V is defined.

### 3.2 Numerical Example (KTU Standard)

**Given:**
- Capillary radius **r = 0.5 mm**, so $a = \pi r^2 = \pi (0.5 \times 10^{-3})^2 = 7.854 \times 10^{-7} \, m^2$
- Bulb volume **V = 100 cm³ = 100 × 10⁻⁶ m³**
- Mercury level difference **h = 10 mm = 0.01 m**

**Step 1 — Substitute values:**

$$P = \frac{a^2 \cdot h^2}{V}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate a²:**

$$a^2 = (7.854 \times 10^{-7})^2 = 6.169 \times 10^{-13} \, m^4$$

**Step 3 — Calculate h²:**

$$h^2 = (0.01)^2 = 1 \times 10^{-4} \, m^2$$

**Step 4 — Compute Numerator:**

$$\text{Numerator} = 6.169 \times 10^{-13} \times 1 \times 10^{-4} = 6.169 \times 10^{-17}$$

**Step 5 — Divide by V:**

$$P = \frac{6.169 \times 10^{-17}}{100 \times 10^{-6}} = 6.169 \times 10^{-13} \, \text{Pa}$$

**Step 6 — Convert to torr** (1 torr = 133.322 Pa):

$$P = \frac{6.169 \times 10^{-13}}{133.322} \approx 4.63 \times 10^{-15} \, \text{torr}$$

This is far below the practical measurement limit, illustrating why capillary dimensions must be chosen carefully.

### 3.3 Pirani Gauge — Bridge Circuit Analysis

The Pirani filament is one arm of a **Wheatstone bridge**. Let R₁ = filament, R₂ = R₃ = R₄ = R (matched resistors).

**Bridge Balance Condition:**

$$\frac{R_1}{R_2} = \frac{R_4}{R_3}$$

When balance holds, output voltage V_out = 0.

**Filament Resistance:**

$$R_1 = R_0 [1 + \alpha (T_f - T_0)]$$

Where **α** is the temperature coefficient of resistance.

**Bridge Output Voltage (Unbalanced):**

$$V_{out} = V_{ex} \left( \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} - \frac{R_4}{R_3 + R_4} \right)$$

At low pressures, heat loss decreases → filament temperature rises → R₁ rises → bridge becomes unbalanced → V_out is proportional to pressure deviation.

### 3.4 Penning Gauge — Electron Path Analysis

The motion of an electron in crossed E and B fields follows cycloidal trajectories. The radius of cycloidal motion is given by:

$$r = \frac{m_e v_\perp}{e B}$$

Where:
- $m_e$ = electron mass
- $v_\perp$ = velocity component perpendicular to B
- $e$ = electron charge
- $B$ = magnetic field strength

The increased path length (due to spiraling) raises the ionization probability. The mean free path requirement for discharge is:

$$\lambda \leq \frac{L}{100}$$

Where **L** is the cathode-anode distance. This defines the upper pressure limit for stable Penning discharge.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Performance

| Parameter | McLeod Gauge | Pirani Gauge | Penning Gauge |
|-----------|--------------|--------------|---------------|
| Principle | Boyle's Law | Thermal Conductivity | Cold Cathode Discharge |
| Type | Primary (Absolute) | Secondary (Relative) | Secondary (Relative) |
| Range (torr) | $10^{-6}$ to $10^{-1}$ | $10^{-4}$ to $1$ | $10^{-7}$ to $10^{-3}$ |
| Accuracy | High (±1%) | Moderate (gas dependent) | Moderate |
| Response Time | Slow (manual) | Fast (continuous) | Fast (continuous) |
| Gas Dependency | None | Yes | Slight |
| Fragility | Mercury hazard | Robust | Robust |
| Cost | High | Low | Moderate |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of McLeod Gauge

```mermaid

    A[Low Pressure Gas Source] --> B[Mercury Reservoir]

    style H fill:#9f9,stroke:#333
```
