# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**
**Q: List any three cryogenic liquid level measurement techniques and state one limitation of each.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Hydrostatic Gauge:** Based on hydrostatic head pressure P = ρgh. *Limitation:* Requires knowledge of saturated liquid density, which varies slightly with pressure; also unsuitable in microgravity.
2. **Capacitance Gauge:** Based on dielectric constant difference between liquid and vapor. *Limitation:* Sensitive to calibration drift and stray capacitance; difficult near the meniscus due to dielectric stratification.
3. **Thermodynamic Gauge:** Based on heat transfer contrast. *Limitation:* Heat input from the heater disturbs the liquid and may cause local boiling; not suitable for ultra-sensitive calorimetry dewars.

### Question 2
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**
**Q: Why is the vapor column contribution (ρ_v g h_v) usually neglected in the hydrostatic level gauge equation for cryogenic liquids?**

**Model Answer:**
For a typical cryogenic fluid such as LNG, the saturated vapor density ρ_v is approximately **1.5 to 3 kg/m³**, while the saturated liquid density ρ_L is approximately **450 kg/m³**. The ratio ρ_v / ρ_L is therefore of the order of **0.005** (i.e., 0.5 %). When this small ratio is multiplied by the height ratio (h_v / h_L), the resulting vapor contribution to the total hydrostatic head is well below **1 %**, which is within the accuracy tolerance of the differential pressure transducer. Hence, the term ρ_v g h_v is dropped, giving the simplified equation:

$$\Delta P \approx \rho_L \, g \, h_L$$

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1)

**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

**(a) [7 Marks] With a neat sketch, explain the working principle of a hydrostatic liquid level gauge used in cryogenic storage tanks. Derive the working equation relating the measured differential pressure to the liquid level height.**

**Model Solution:**

**Working Principle:**
A regulated supply of inert gas (typically helium) is fed through a thin tube (called a *bubbler* or *dip tube*) that extends to near the bottom of the cryogenic tank. The gas bubbles out from the bottom of the tube into the liquid. The back-pressure in the tube at the inlet is the sum of:
1. The pressure at the liquid surface (≈ vapor pressure of the fluid), plus
2. The hydrostatic head of the liquid column above the bubble release point.

This back-pressure is measured by a high-precision differential pressure transducer. The differential pressure is proportional to the liquid column height.

**Schematic (described in text since drawing will be done on paper):**
- Bubbler tube enters the dewar from the top.
- Tube end is at the bottom of the tank.
- Inert gas supply with a regulator connects to the tube.
- Pressure transducer measures the gas pressure inside the tube.
- Readout is calibrated in units of liquid level (m) or volume (m³).

**Derivation:**
Consider a vertical column of cryogenic liquid of height h_L, density ρ_L, and vapor above it of density ρ_v. Pressure at the gas-liquid interface is the saturation pressure P_sat. Pressure at depth h_L (bottom of dip tube) is:

$$P_{bottom} = P_{sat} + \rho_L \, g \, h_L + \rho_v \, g \, h_v$$

The differential pressure between the bottom sensor and a top sensor (located in the vapor space) is:

$$\Delta P = P_{bottom} - P_{top} = (P_{sat} + \rho_L g h_L + \rho_v g h_v) - (P_{sat} + \rho_v g h_v)$$

$$\Delta P = \rho_L \, g \, h_L \quad \text{(since } \rho_v \ll \rho_L\text{)}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{h_L = \frac{\Delta P}{\rho_L \, g}}$$

This is the working equation used for direct digital readout of liquid level.

---

**(b) [7 Marks] A cryogenic tank contains liquid nitrogen (LN₂). The bubbler tube pressure transducer reads a differential pressure of 6 kPa. Given ρ_L = 804 kg/m³ and g = 9.81 m/s², calculate (i) the liquid height in metres, and (ii) the percentage error introduced if the vapor contribution is mistakenly included with ρ_v = 4.6 kg/m³ and h_v = 1.0 m.**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Liquid height (correcting only for liquid):**

$$h_L = \frac{\Delta P}{\rho_L g} = \frac{6000}{804 \times 9.81}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
h_L &= \frac{6000}{7887.24} \\
h_L &= 0.7608 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}$$

**(ii) True pressure including vapor contribution:**
The vapor contribution that was *mistakenly* added by the student is:

$$\Delta P_v = \rho_v \, g \, h_v = 4.6 \times 9.81 \times 1.0 = 45.13 \text{ Pa}$$

This is the error in pressure. As a percentage of total pressure:

$$\% \text{ error} = \frac{45.13}{6000} \times 100 = 0.752 \%$$

So the student's reported height would be:

$$h_{reported} = \frac{6000 - 45.13}{804 \times 9.81} = 0.7551 \text{ m}$$

Percentage error in level = (45.13 / 6000) × 100 ≈ **0.75 %**. This is typically within the tolerance of the gauge, justifying the simplification.

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Internal Choice Alternative)

**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

**(a) [7 Marks] Describe the construction and working of a capacitance-type cryogenic liquid level gauge. Derive the relation between the change in capacitance and the immersion depth for a coaxial cylindrical probe.**

**Model Solution:**

**Construction:**
A coaxial cylindrical capacitor is fabricated as the level probe. It consists of:
- An inner solid rod of radius *a* (typically stainless steel or copper).
- An outer cylindrical tube of inner radius *b*, concentric with the inner rod.
- Insulating spacers (Teflon or ceramic) at top and bottom to maintain concentricity.
- The two electrodes form a two-terminal capacitor. A coaxial cable carries the AC excitation signal out of the cryostat.

**Working:**
An AC excitation at a fixed frequency (typically 1 kHz to 100 kHz) is applied between the two electrodes. The dielectric medium between them is partly liquid (ε_L) and partly vapor (ε_v). Because ε_L > ε_v, the capacitance increases linearly with the length of probe immersed in liquid. The capacitance change is read by a precision LCR meter and converted to a liquid level reading.

**Derivation (already detailed in Section 3.2):**

$$\Delta C = \frac{2 \pi \varepsilon_0 (\varepsilon_L - \varepsilon_v) \, \ell}{\ln(b/a)}$$

Solving for ℓ:

$$\ell = \frac{\Delta C \, \ln(b/a)}{2 \pi \varepsilon_0 (\varepsilon_L - \varepsilon_v)}$$

---

**(b) [7 Marks] A coaxial capacitance probe has inner radius a = 1.5 mm, outer radius b = 5.0 mm, and total length L = 1.0 m. It is used to measure liquid hydrogen level (ε_L = 1.23, ε_v = 1.00). The measured capacitance is 245 pF. Determine the immersion depth of liquid hydrogen.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Capacitance per unit length in pure vapor (reference):**

$$C_v^{\prime} = \frac{2 \pi \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_v}{\ln(b/a)} = \frac{2 \pi (8.854 \times 10^{-12})(1.0)}{\ln(5/1.5)}$$

$$\ln(5/1.5) = \ln(3.333) = 1.2040$$

$$C_v^{\prime} = \frac{5.564 \times 10^{-11}}{1.2040} = 4.621 \times 10^{-11} \text{ F/m} = 46.21 \text{ pF/m}$$

**Step 2 — Capacitance per unit length in pure liquid:**

$$C_L^{\prime} = \frac{2 \pi (8.854 \times 10^{-12})(1.23)}{1.2040} = 5.684 \times 10^{-11} \text{ F/m} = 56.84 \text{ pF/m}$$

**Step 3 — Total capacitance as a function of immersion ℓ:**

$$C_{total} = C_v^{\prime} (L - \ell) + C_L^{\prime} \, \ell = C_v^{\prime} L + (C_L^{\prime} - C_v^{\prime}) \, \ell$$

**Step 4 — Solve for ℓ given C_total = 245 pF and L = 1.0 m:**

$$C_v^{\prime} L = 46.21 \times 1.0 = 46.21 \text{ pF}$$

$$\ell = \frac{C_{total} - C_v^{\prime} L}{C_L^{\prime} - C_v^{\prime}} = \frac{245 - 46.21}{56.84 - 46.21}$$

$$\ell = \frac{198.79}{10.63} = 18.70 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5 — Sanity check:** Since L = 1.0 m, an immersion depth of 18.70 m is physically impossible. This means the assumption that the gauge is **partially immersed** is wrong — the probe is fully immersed, and we must re-evaluate.

For the fully immersed case:

$$C_{full} = C_L^{\prime} \times L = 56.84 \times 1.0 = 56.84 \text{ pF}$$

But the measured value 245 pF is much larger than 56.84 pF, which suggests the probe is likely a **multi-section** or the stated geometry does not produce 245 pF. Re-checking with a hypothetical multi-section or considering cable capacitance in parallel would normally be required. For KTU board purposes, the partial-immersion formula yields:

$$\boxed{\ell \approx 18.7 \text{ m (theoretical; indicates probe fully submerged)}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the natural logarithm ln(b/a)**, or computing ln(a/b) (which gives a negative number) — common 2-mark deduction.
> 2. **Mixing up** ε_L and ε_v in the subtraction (ε_L − ε_v). A negative difference yields nonsensical negative level.
> 3. **Ignoring stray capacitance** of the connecting coaxial cable, which often exceeds the probe capacitance at LN₂ temperatures.
> 4. **Confusing partial immersion** with full immersion (as in this numerical example). Always check if ℓ ≤ L.
> 5. In hydrostatic problems, students frequently **omit the units conversion** of pressure from kPa to Pa — leading to height off by a factor of 1000.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4: Cryogenic Liquid Level Gauges**

### Core Definitions
- ☐ **Hydrostatic Gauge:** Measures ΔP at the bottom of liquid column → h = ΔP / (ρ_L g). Vapor contribution negligible.
- ☐ **Resistance Gauge:** Array of resistors scanned by multiplexer; resistance drops sharply in liquid. *Discrete output.*
- ☐ **Capacitance Gauge:** Coaxial capacitor; ε_L > ε_v gives ΔC proportional to immersion depth ℓ. *Continuous output.*
- ☐ **Thermodynamic Gauge:** Heated probe; superheat ΔT = Q̇/(hA). ΔT_L ≪ ΔT_v due to h_L ≫ h_v.

### Essential Formulas (must memorize)
- ☐ $h_L = \dfrac{\Delta P}{\rho_L g}$
- ☐ $\Delta C = \dfrac{2\pi \varepsilon_0 (\varepsilon_L - \varepsilon_v) \ell}{\ln(b/a)}$
- ☐ $\Delta T = \dfrac{\dot{Q}}{h \, A}$
- ☐ $R(T) = R_0 [1 + \alpha(T - T_0)]$

### Key Approximations
- ☐ ρ_v / ρ_L ≈ 0.005 (so vapor hydrostatic term dropped).
- ☐ Radiation heat loss negligible compared to convection at T < 100 K.
- ☐ AC excitation frequency for capacitance: 1 kHz to 100 kHz.

### Selection Criteria (memorize for viva)
- ☐ **Microgravity** → Thermodynamic or Resistance.
- ☐ **Highest accuracy** → Hydrostatic.
- ☐ **No heat input required** → Hydrostatic or Capacitance.
- ☐ **Continuous + low cost** → Capacitance.
- ☐ **Space applications** → Thermodynamic.

### Common Mistakes to Avoid in KTU Exam
- ☐ Do not confuse *continuous* (analog) gauges with *discrete* (stepped) gauges.
- ☐ Do not use ρ_v instead of ρ_L in hydrostatic head equation.
- ☐ Do not forget the factor of g (= 9.81 m/s²) when only ΔP is given.
- ☐ Always specify units of h in **metres**, ΔP in **Pa**, ρ in **kg/m³**.
- ☐ In capacitance derivations, ensure that *b > a* so that ln(b/a) > 0.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Temperature Measurements - ITS-90, Thermocouple, Magnetic Thermometers, and Vapor Pressure Thermometers
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET861 - CRYOGENIC ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Temperature Measurements - ITS-90, Thermocouple, Magnetic Thermometers, and Vapor Pressure Thermometers

# Module 4: Temperature Measurements in Cryogenic Engineering

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 The International Temperature Scale of 1990 (ITS-90)

**Formal Definition:** ITS-90 is an internationally adopted practical temperature scale established by the **Bureau International des Poids et Mesures (BIPM)** in 1989, that defines temperatures through a set of fixed points, reproducible equilibrium states, and specified measuring instruments calibrated against those fixed points. It is the practical successor to IPTS-68 and covers a range from **0.65 K to the highest temperatures practically measurable** using Planck's radiation law.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> ITS-90 is **not** a thermodynamic scale, but a defined scale that closely approximates the thermodynamic temperature scale. It provides the *only* legal, internationally recognized way of reporting temperature measurements in metrology, instrumentation, and cryogenics.

**Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine the world had no rulers, and every carpenter made their own. Lengths would be inconsistent. ITS-90 is like a "master ruler" for temperature — it says: *"this much voltage from this thermocouple at this fixed point equals exactly this many Kelvin."* Without it, two cryogenic labs in Kerala and Canada might disagree on whether a sample is at 77.3 K or 77.5 K, even with identical sensors.

**Fixed Points of ITS-90 (Selection):**

| Fixed Point | Temperature (K) | State |
|---|---|---|
| Triple Point of Helium-3 | **0.65 K** | Phase equilibrium |
| Triple Point of Neon | 24.5561 K | Phase equilibrium |
| Triple Point of Oxygen | 54.3584 K | Phase equilibrium |
| Triple Point of Argon | 83.8058 K | Phase equilibrium |
| Triple Point of Mercury | 234.3156 K | Phase equilibrium |
| Triple Point of Water | 273.16 K | Phase equilibrium (defines 0.01 °C) |
| Freezing Point of Zinc | 692.677 K | Phase equilibrium |
| Freezing Point of Silver | 1234.93 K | Phase equilibrium |

---

### 1.2 Thermocouples (in Cryogenic Context)

**Formal Definition:** A thermocouple is a temperature sensor made of two dissimilar thermoelectric conductors joined at one end (the *measuring junction* or *hot junction*), where the open-circuit voltage at the other end (the *reference junction* or *cold junction*) is a known, reproducible function of the temperature difference between the two junctions, governed by the **Seebeck Effect**.

**Conceptual Analogy:** Think of two metals as two "racing tracks" for electrons. When one end is heated, electrons in the hotter track move faster and diffuse toward the cooler end. Since one metal "pushes" electrons more than the other, a net voltage appears at the cold end — like water sloshing to one side of a tilted container. The voltage reading tells you how much the hot end is hotter than the cold end.

> [!NOTE]
> Common cryogenic thermocouples include **Type E (Chromel-Constantan)**, **Type K (Chromel-Alumel)**, and special low-temperature pairs like **Au-Fe / Chromel** and **Cu / Au-Fe**, all calibrated against ITS-90 fixed points.

---

### 1.3 Magnetic Thermometers (Paramagnetic & Nuclear)

**Formal Definition:** A magnetic thermometer measures temperature through the **Curie Law** relationship between a material's magnetic susceptibility and absolute temperature, exploiting the temperature-dependent alignment of atomic magnetic dipoles (paramagnetic salt thermometers) or nuclear spin systems (nuclear magnetic resonance / nuclear orientation thermometers) in an applied magnetic field.

**Conceptual Analogy:** Picture millions of tiny compass needles (atomic dipoles) inside a sample. At high temperature, they jiggle randomly and point every which way. As you cool them, the "jiggling" decreases and they line up with the applied magnetic field more obediently. The *degree of alignment* (magnetic susceptibility $\chi$) tells you exactly how cold the sample is — like judging how still water is by how well a paper boat on it remains motionless.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Magnetic thermometers are *primary* thermometers in the deep cryogenic regime (typically **1 mK to 4 K**), meaning they do not require external calibration — the relationship $\chi = C/T$ is derivable from fundamental physics.

---

### 1.4 Vapor Pressure Thermometers

**Formal Definition:** A vapor pressure thermometer measures temperature by exploiting the **monotonic, single-valued relationship** between the saturation vapor pressure of a pure condensed substance (liquid or solid) and its absolute temperature, in thermodynamic equilibrium with its own vapor phase.

**Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine a sealed pot of water on a stove. As you heat it, you can tell how hot it is just by *listening* to the pressure of steam pushing on the lid — more pressure means more heat. Vapor pressure thermometers do exactly this: they measure the *pressure* of vapor above a liquid (helium, hydrogen, neon, nitrogen, etc.) and "translate" that pressure into temperature.

> [!NOTE]
> Vapor pressure thermometry is a **primary thermometric method** for the range **0.65 K to 5 K** (using ³He) and **13 K to 36 K** (using ²H₂, Ne, H₂, or N₂), per ITS-90 stipulations.

---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 ITS-90 — Structural Sub-Divisions

The ITS-90 scale is **divided into sub-ranges**, each with its own defining instrument and interpolation method:

1. **0.65 K to 3.2 K:** Helium-3 vapor pressure thermometry, $T_{90}$ defined by an equation of the form $\ln p = \sum a_i (T_{90})^i$.
2. **1.25 K to 24.5561 K:** Interpolating Constant Volume Gas Thermometer (ICVGT) using Helium-3 or Helium-4.
3. **3.2 K to 24.5561 K (overlap):** Helium-4 vapor pressure thermometry.
4. **13.8033 K to 1234.93 K (Ne TP to Ag FP):** **Platinum Resistance Thermometer (SPRT)** with interpolation using a defined reference function and deviation functions.
5. **1234.93 K and above:** Spectral radiation pyrometer using **Planck's radiation law**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why sub-divisions?** No single instrument is accurate across the entire range. A thermocouple may drift at 1 K, and a radiation pyrometer cannot be used at 10 K. Each sub-range uses the most reproducible instrument for that regime.

---

### 2.2 Thermocouple Theory — The Seebeck Effect

When two dissimilar metals A and B are joined:

- Electrons at the hot junction gain thermal energy, diffuse toward the cold end.
- Because A and B have different electron densities and Fermi energies, the diffusion fluxes differ.
- A net **Seebeck EMF** is generated: $E = \int_{T_{\text{cold}}}^{T_{\text{hot}}} (S_A - S_B)\, dT$, where $S_A$ and $S_B$ are the absolute Seebeck coefficients of materials A and B.

The Seebeck coefficient is itself temperature-dependent: $S(T) = dE/dT$ — this is why each thermocouple type has a unique calibration curve.

> [!NOTE]
> In **cryogenics**, the **Au-Fe (0.07 at% Fe) / Chromel-P** thermocouple is widely used down to **1 K** because of its high sensitivity (≈ 15 µV/K at 20 K). Type E is preferred between 20 K and 300 K for its high Seebeck coefficient (≈ 60 µV/K at 100 K).

---

### 2.3 Magnetic Thermometers — Curie Law and Beyond

For a paramagnetic salt (e.g., Cerium Magnesium Nitrate, **CMN**), the **Curie Law** governs low-field magnetic susceptibility:

$$\chi = \frac{C}{T}$$

where $\chi$ is the **magnetic susceptibility** (dimensionless, SI), $C$ is the **Curie constant** (material-specific), and $T$ is absolute temperature (K).

For higher fields or lower temperatures, the **Curie-Weiss law** applies:

$$\chi = \frac{C}{T - \theta}$$

where $\theta$ is the **Weiss constant** (a measure of internal exchange interactions).

In **Nuclear Magnetic Resonance (NMR) thermometers**, the resonance frequency $f$ depends on magnetic field $B$ and nuclear gyromagnetic ratio $\gamma$:

$$f = \gamma B$$

The temperature dependence enters through the **Curie-law spin polarization** of the nuclear (or electronic) moment, which alters the local field. A common configuration uses **Pt or Cu** as the probe nucleus, calibrated from **0.5 mK to 100 mK**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Nuclear Orientation Thermometers (NÖT)** use radioactive nuclei (e.g., ⁶⁰Co, ⁵⁴Mn, ⁵⁷Co) whose $\gamma$-ray anisotropy is a function of $1/T$ down to **sub-mK** ranges — the coldest temperatures ever measured in a lab.

---

### 2.4 Vapor Pressure Thermometers — Clausius-Clapeyron Foundation

The fundamental relation is the **Clausius-Clapeyron equation**, integrated assuming the vapor behaves as an ideal gas and the molar volume of liquid is negligible:

$$\ln p = -\frac{L}{R} \cdot \frac{1}{T} + A$$

where $p$ is vapor pressure, $L$ is latent heat of vaporization, $R$ is the universal gas constant, and $A$ is an integration constant.

For higher accuracy, the ITS-90 vapor pressure scale uses an empirical polynomial:

$$\ln p = \sum_{i=0}^{n} a_i \left[ \frac{T - T_{\text{ref}}}{T} \right]^i$$

The ³He vapor pressure scale is **explicitly defined** as part of ITS-90 from **0.65 K to 3.2 K**.

---

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula / Relation | Symbol Meaning | Applicable Range | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E = \int_{T_c}^{T_h} (S_A - S_B)\,dT$ | Seebeck EMF of a thermocouple | 1 K – 1500 K | V |
| 2 | $S = dE/dT$ | Differential Seebeck coefficient | All T | V/K |
| 3 | $\chi = C / T$ | Curie law (paramagnetic) | $T \ll \Theta_D$ | dimensionless |
| 4 | $\chi = C / (T - \theta)$ | Curie-Weiss law | 0.001 K – 4 K | dimensionless |
| 5 | $M = C \cdot B / T$ | Magnetization of paramagnet | 0.001 K – 4 K | A/m |
| 6 | $f_{\text{NMR}} = \gamma B$ | NMR resonance frequency | < 1 K | Hz |
| 7 | $\ln p = -L/(RT) + A$ | Clausius-Clapeyron (ideal) | 0.65 K – 36 K | p in Pa |
| 8 | $T_{90} = A_0 + \sum A_i \left[ \frac{\ln p - B}{\ln(B') - \ln(B)} \right]^i$ | ITS-90 vapor pressure scale | 0.65 K – 3.2 K (³He) | K |
| 9 | $R_t = R_0 [1 + At + Bt^2 + C(t-100)t^3]$ | SPRT Callendar-Van Dusen eqn. | 13.8 K – 273.16 K | $\Omega$ |
| 10 | $L_{\lambda} = c_1 / [\lambda^5 (\exp(c_2/\lambda T) - 1)]$ | Planck radiation law | > 1234.93 K | W·m⁻²·sr⁻¹ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> $c_1 = 2hc^2$ and $c_2 = hc/k_B$ are the first and second radiation constants. The constant $c_2 = 0.014387768$ m·K in the current SI definition.

---

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **LNG storage tanks:** Type E and Au-Fe thermocouples monitor bulk cargo temperature during marine transport — millions of dollars of cargo depend on these sensors.
- **MRI superconducting magnets (1.8 K – 4.2 K):** Vapor pressure thermometers using Helium-4 and Cernox™ sensors (a thin-film RTD calibrated to ITS-90) maintain the Nb-Ti coils below critical temperature.
- **Space cryogenics (IR telescopes, JWST at 40 K):** Au-Fe/Chromel thermocouples are used because of their radiation hardness and stability under vacuum.
- **Dilution refrigerators (sub-10 mK):** NMR and NÖT thermometers are the *only* practical primary sensors — they read the actual nuclear spin temperature.
- **Calibration laboratories (NABL, NIST, NPL-India):** SPRTs and fixed-point cells realize ITS-90 directly for industrial traceability.

---

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Seebeck EMF (Thermocouple Working Equation)

**Step 1 — Define the Seebeck coefficient of a single material.**
The Seebeck coefficient $S$ of a homogeneous conductor is defined as the entropy transported per unit charge:

$$S = -\frac{\pi^2 k_B^2 T}{3e} \cdot \frac{d \ln \sigma(\varepsilon)}{d \varepsilon}\bigg|_{\varepsilon = \varepsilon_F}$$

where $\sigma(\varepsilon)$ is the electrical conductivity evaluated at the Fermi energy $\varepsilon_F$, $k_B$ is Boltzmann's constant, and $e$ is the electron charge.

**Step 2 — Form the thermocouple EMF as a difference.**
For a thermocouple with two legs A and B, the relative Seebeck coefficient is:

$$S_{AB}(T) = S_A(T) - S_B(T)$$

**Step 3 — Integrate to obtain the open-circuit voltage.**
The total EMF generated is the integral of the differential Seebeck coefficient from the reference (cold) junction at $T_c$ to the measuring (hot) junction at $T_h$:

$$
E_{AB}(T_h, T_c) = \int_{T_c}^{T_h} S_{AB}(T)\, dT
$$

**Step 4 — Practical polynomial calibration form.**
In practice, the integral is evaluated numerically and tabulated. For engineering use, a polynomial fit of degree $n$ is provided:

$$
E_{AB}(T) = \sum_{i=0}^{n} a_i \, T^i
$$

with reference junction held at $T_c = 273.15$ K (ice point). Coefficients are tabulated in ITS-90 reference tables (e.g., NIST ITS-90 Thermocouple Database).

**Step 5 — Cold-junction (reference) compensation.**
The actual hot-junction temperature is recovered by inverting:

$$
E_{\text{measured}}(T_h, T_c) = E(T_h, 0) - E(T_c, 0)
$$

In modern instruments, $T_c$ is sensed by an internal thermistor and the software reconstructs $T_h$ directly.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Curie Susceptibility (Magnetic Thermometer Working Equation)

**Step 1 — Boltzmann distribution of magnetic dipoles.**
For a paramagnet with $N$ non-interacting dipoles of moment $\mu$ in field $B$, the population of energy level $E = -\mu B \cos\theta$ follows Boltzmann statistics. The average magnetization $M$ is:

$$
M = N \mu \left[ \coth\left( \frac{\mu B}{k_B T} \right) - \frac{k_B T}{\mu B} \right] = N \mu \, L\!\left( \frac{\mu B}{k_B T} \right)
$$

where $L(x)$ is the **Langevin function** $L(x) = \coth(x) - 1/x$.

**Step 2 — Low-field limit (Curie Law regime).**
When $\mu B \ll k_B T$, expand the Langevin function as a Taylor series: $L(x) \approx x/3 - x^3/45 + \dots$ Truncating at first order:

$$
M \approx \frac{N \mu^2 B}{3 k_B T}
$$

**Step 3 — Define the magnetic susceptibility.**
By definition, $\chi = M / B$. Therefore:

$$
\chi = \frac{N \mu^2}{3 k_B T} = \frac{C}{T}
$$

This is the **Curie Law**, with the **Curie constant** $C = N\mu^2 / (3 k_B)$. The temperature is recovered by:

$$
T = \frac{C}{\chi}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> For CMN (Cerium Magnesium Nitrate), the Curie constant $C$ has been measured to ≈ 0.0107 K·(SI volume susceptibility unit), allowing the thermometer to be used *without* external calibration — making it a true primary thermometer.

**Step 4 — Curie-Weiss extension (higher accuracy below 1 K).**
At very low temperatures, weak interactions between dipoles introduce a temperature shift:

$$
\chi = \frac{C}{T - \theta}
$$

Inverting:

$$
T = \theta + \frac{C}{\chi}
$$

For CMN, $\theta \approx -6 \times 10^{-6}$ K, which is non-negligible below 100 mK.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of the Vapor Pressure Relation (Clausius-Clapeyron)

**Step 1 — Start from thermodynamic equilibrium.**
At the liquid-vapor interface, the chemical potentials of the two phases are equal: $\mu_l(p, T) = \mu_v(p, T)$.

**Step 2 — Differentiate both sides with respect to T at constant p.**

$$
\left( \frac{\partial \mu_l}{\partial T} \right)_p = \left( \frac{\partial \mu_v}{\partial T} \right)_p
$$

Using the Maxwell relation $(\partial \mu/\partial T)_p = -S_m$ (entropy per mole):

$$
S_l = S_v \quad \Rightarrow \quad S_v - S_l = \frac{L}{T}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Clapeyron relation.**
The slope of the phase boundary is:

$$
\frac{dp}{dT} = \frac{L}{T \Delta v} = \frac{L}{T (v_v - v_l)}
$$

where $v_v$ and $v_l$ are the molar volumes of vapor and liquid respectively.

**Step 4 — Ideal gas + negligible liquid volume approximations.**
For vapor at low pressure, $v_v \approx RT/p$ from the ideal gas law. Also $v_l \ll v_v$ near the triple point. Substituting:

$$
\frac{dp}{dT} = \frac{L p}{R T^2}
$$

**Step 5 — Separate variables and integrate (L = const).**

$$
\int \frac{dp}{p} = \int \frac{L}{R} \cdot \frac{dT}{T^2}
$$

$$
\ln p = -\frac{L}{R T} + A
$$

where $A$ is an integration constant determined experimentally at one reference point. For higher accuracy, $L$ is itself taken as $L(T) = L_0 + L_1 T + L_2 T^2$, which leads to the polynomial form used in ITS-90 vapor pressure tables.

---

### 3.4 ITS-90 Platinum Resistance Thermometer (SPRT) Interpolation

For the range 13.8033 K to 273.16 K, ITS-90 specifies:

$$
W(T_{90}) = \frac{R(T_{90})}{R(273.16\,K)}
$$

A reference function $W_r(T_{90})$ is provided, and the deviation function is:

$$
\Delta W(T_{90}) = W(T_{90}) - W_r(T_{90}) = a [W(T_{90}) - 1] + b [W(T_{90}) - 1]^2 + \sum_{i=1}^{5} c_i [\ln W(T_{90})]^i
$$

Coefficients $a, b, c_i$ are determined by calibration at four fixed points (Ne, O₂, Ar, Hg triple points + water triple point). This is the mathematical heart of how ITS-90 is *realized* in national labs.

---

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 ITS-90 Scale Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[ITS-90 Temperature Scale] --> B[0.65 K to 3.2 K]
    A --> C[1.25 K to 24.5561 K]
    A --> D[3.2 K to 24.5561 K overlap]
    A --> E[13.8033 K to 1234.93 K]
    A --> F[Above 1234.93 K]
    B --> B1[He-3 Vapor Pressure Thermometer]
    C --> C1[Interpolating CVGT He-3 or He-4]
    D --> D1[He-4 Vapor Pressure Thermometer]
    E --> E1[Platinum Resistance Thermometer SPRT]
    E --> E2[Sub ranges with deviation functions]
    F --> F1[Spectral Radiation Pyrometer]
    F --> F2[Planck radiation law interpolation]
    E2 --> E2a[13.8 K to 273.16 K]
    E2 --> E2b[0 to 961.78 C Ag freezing]
    E2 --> E2c[0 to 660.323 C Al freezing]
```

### 4.2 Thermocouple Measurement Circuit

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Hot Junction T hot] --> B[Conductor A]
    A --> C[Conductor B]
    B --> D[Reference Junction T cold]
    C --> D
    D --> E[Lead Wires Cu]
    E --> F[Nanovoltmeter or ADC]
    F --> G[Cold Junction Compensation Sensor]
    F --> H[Display T hot]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Calibrated against ITS-90 reference tables]
```

### 4.3 Magnetic Thermometer Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Sample of CMN or Pt Nuclear Probe] --> B[Magnetization Coil B field]
    B --> C[AC Susceptometer or NMR Pickup Coil]
    C --> D[Lock-in Amplifier]
    D --> E[Signal Processing]
    E --> F[Inverse Curie Calculation T equals C over chi]
    F --> G[Display in mK]
    A --> H[Thermal Link to Cold Stage]
    H --> I[Dilution Refrigerator at sub 10 mK]
```

### 4.4 Vapor Pressure Thermometer Configuration

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Cryogenic Liquid or Solid He 3 He 4 H2 Ne N2] --> B[Liquid Reservoir Bulb]
    B --> C[Capillary Tube to Warm Side]
    C --> D[Pressure Transducer]
    D --> E[Signal Conditioner]
    E --> F[Vapor Pressure to Temperature Conversion]
    F --> G[Apply ITS-90 Equation]
    G --> H[Display in K]
    B --> I[Immersed in Cold Region to be Measured]
    D --> J[Calibrated against ITS-90 fixed points]
```

### 4.5 Comparative Selection Logic for Cryogenic Sensors

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Choose a Cryogenic Temperature Sensor] --> B{Temperature Range}
    B -->|0.65 to 5 K| C1[Vapor Pressure Thermometer He 3]
    B -->|0.001 to 1 K| C2[Magnetic Thermometer CMN or NÖT]
    B -->|1 to 300 K| C3[Thermocouple Au Fe or Type E]
    B -->|1.5 to 300 K| C4[Resistive Cernox or Germanium RTD]
    B -->|13.8 to 1234 K| C5[Platinum SPRT ITS 90 standard]
    A --> D{Required Accuracy}
    D -->|Best 0.001 K| E1[Primary Thermometer NÖT or SPRT]
    D -->|Industrial 0.1 to 1 K| E2[Secondary Sensor Thermocouple or RTD]
```

---

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023 (Model)]**
**Define the International Temperature Scale of 1990 (ITS-90). Why is it necessary to define a practical temperature scale when thermodynamic temperature already exists?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

ITS-90 is a defined practical temperature scale adopted by the **BIPM in 1989**, realized through a set of reproducible fixed points (triple points and freezing points of pure substances) and specified interpolation instruments (helium vapor pressure, gas thermometer, platinum resistance thermometer, and radiation pyrometer) for various sub-ranges from **0.65 K upward**.

Although thermodynamic temperature $T$ is the "true" quantity, it cannot be measured directly. Primary thermometers (e.g., gas thermometers, NÖT, total radiation pyrometers) are slow, complex, and inconvenient for routine use. ITS-90 provides **interpolation equations and reference functions** that allow secondary sensors (thermocouples, SPRTs, vapor pressure bulbs) to be calibrated to closely approximate $T$ with high precision and reproducibility. (3 marks)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 (Model)]**
**State the Curie Law of paramagnetic susceptibility and explain how it is used to measure temperature in a magnetic thermometer.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Curie Law states that for a paramagnetic material with non-interacting dipoles, the magnetic susceptibility is inversely proportional to absolute temperature:

$$\chi = \frac{C}{T}$$

where $C = N\mu^2 / (3k_B)$ is the Curie constant. In a magnetic thermometer, a salt such as **CMN (Cerium Magnesium Nitrate)** is placed in a weak AC magnetic field, and the induced magnetization $M = \chi B$ is measured via a pickup coil and lock-in amplifier. The temperature is computed as $T = C/\chi$. Because $C$ is calculable from fundamental constants, the thermometer is **primary** and requires no external calibration. (3 marks)

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern, Module 4, Q. 4(a) or 4(b)]**

**(a) (7 marks)** Describe the construction and working principle of a **vapor pressure thermometer** as used in cryogenic temperature measurement. Explain with reference to the **Clausius-Clapeyron relation** how temperature is inferred from the measured saturation pressure of a pure fluid such as ³He, ⁴He, or hydrogen.

**(b) (7 marks)** A cryogenic laboratory uses a ⁴He vapor pressure bulb immersed in a liquid helium dewar. The pressure transducer reads **250 torr**. Using the **ITS-90 ⁴He vapor pressure table**, determine the temperature of the bath. Given the relevant points from the ⁴He vapor pressure equation $T_{90} = A_0 + A_1 y + A_2 y^2 + A_3 y^3 + A_4 y^4$, where $y = \ln(p/p_{\text{ref}})$ and $p_{\text{ref}}$ is a reference pressure, with $A_0 = 3.6333$ K, $A_1 = 0.99362$ K, $A_2 = 0.00010$ K, and pressures at 1 atm ≈ 760 torr corresponding to **4.2221 K**.

---

**Model Solution to (a) — 7 marks:**

A vapor pressure thermometer consists of:
1. A small **bulb** containing a pure condensed fluid (³He, ⁴He, H₂, Ne, N₂) immersed in the region whose temperature is to be measured.
2. A thin **capillary tube** (often evacuated or filled with the same gas) connecting the bulb to a **pressure transducer** at room temperature.
3. A calibrated **pressure readout** that converts the saturation vapor pressure into a temperature reading.

**Working Principle:** The condensed liquid in the bulb is in thermodynamic equilibrium with its saturated vapor at the local temperature. As the bath warms, more molecules evaporate and the vapor pressure rises. The pressure is measured remotely, and the temperature is computed by inverting the empirical ITS-90 vapor pressure equation (or table).

**Connection to Clausius-Clapeyron:** The integrated form is:

$$\ln p = -\frac{L}{R T} + A$$

A higher temperature means a smaller $1/T$, hence a larger $\ln p$ and hence a larger $p$. Inverting:

$$T = \frac{L}{R (A - \ln p)}$$

This monotonic relationship allows unique determination of $T$ from $p$. For ⁴He between 1.0 K and 4.2 K, this is the *primary* ITS-90 method. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution to (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Convert pressure to standard units.**

$$
p = 250 \text{ torr} = \frac{250}{760} \text{ atm} = 0.3289 \text{ atm}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute y.** Using $p_{\text{ref}} = 1 \text{ atm} = 760 \text{ torr}$:

$$
y = \ln \left( \frac{p}{p_{\text{ref}}} \right) = \ln \left( \frac{250}{760} \right) = \ln(0.3289) = -1.1112
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the polynomial.**

$$
T_{90} = A_0 + A_1 y + A_2 y^2 + A_3 y^3 + A_4 y^4
$$

For this problem, with the given coefficients and $A_3, A_4$ negligible (small corrections), we compute:

$$
T_{90} = 3.6333 + (0.99362)(-1.1112) + (0.00010)(-1.1112)^2
$$

$$
T_{90} = 3.6333 - 1.1041 + 0.000123
$$

$$
\boxed{T_{90} \approx 2.529 \text{ K}}
$$

**Step 4 — Cross-check.** From a standard ⁴He vapor pressure table, $p = 250$ torr corresponds to ≈ 2.53 K, confirming the answer. (7 marks)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative**

**(a) (7 marks)** With the help of a clear schematic and the underlying theory, explain the **Seebeck Effect** in a thermocouple. Derive the relationship between the generated EMF and the temperatures of the hot and cold junctions. Mention **two cryogenic-specific thermocouple pairs** and their useful temperature ranges.

**(b) (7 marks)** A **Type E (Chromel-Constantan) thermocouple** is used to monitor the temperature of a cryogenic specimen. The cold junction is held at 0 °C, and the measured EMF is **3.615 mV**. Using the inverse ITS-90 polynomial approximation $T = a_0 + a_1 E + a_2 E^2 + a_3 E^3 + a_4 E^4$ (with $a_0 = 0$, $a_1 = 25.0$ °C/mV, $a_2 = -0.50$ °C/mV², $a_3 = 0.04$ °C/mV³, $a_4 = 0$ °C/mV⁴), determine the specimen temperature in **Kelvin**.

---

**Model Solution to (a) — 7 marks:**

The **Seebeck Effect** is the direct conversion of a temperature gradient into an electrical voltage at the junction of two dissimilar conductors. Constructionally, a thermocouple has two wires A and B of different materials joined at the *measuring junction* (hot end, $T_h$); the other ends are joined to copper lead wires at the *reference junction* (cold end, $T_c$).

The generated EMF is:

$$
E_{AB}(T_h, T_c) = \int_{T_c}^{T_h} \left[ S_A(T) - S_B(T) \right] dT
$$

This comes from the entropy-transport definition of the Seebeck coefficient $S = dE/dT$, which itself arises from the temperature dependence of the Fermi–Dirac distribution in each metal. Because $S_A \neq S_B$, the integrals from $T_c$ to $T_h$ do not cancel, leaving a measurable voltage.

**Cryogenic-specific pairs:**

| Pair | Useful Range | Sensitivity (typical) |
|---|---|---|
| **Au-Fe (0.07 at%) / Chromel-P** | 1 K – 300 K | ≈ 15 µV/K at 20 K |
| **Type E (Chromel-Constantan)** | 20 K – 1100 K | ≈ 60 µV/K at 100 K |
| **Type K (Chromel-Alumel)** | 20 K – 1100 K | ≈ 40 µV/K at 100 K |
| **Cu / Au-Fe** | 1 K – 200 K | ≈ 10 µV/K at 20 K |

(7 marks)

---

**Model Solution to (b) — 7 marks:**

**Given:** $E = 3.615$ mV; $T_c = 0$ °C = 273.15 K.

**Step 1 — Apply the polynomial:**

$$
T(°C) = a_0 + a_1 E + a_2 E^2 + a_3 E^3 + a_4 E^4
$$

$$
T(°C) = 0 + (25.0)(3.615) + (-0.50)(3.615)^2 + (0.04)(3.615)^3 + 0
$$

**Step 2 — Compute each term:**

$$
a_1 E = 25.0 \times 3.615 = 90.375 \text{ °C}
$$

$$
a_2 E^2 = -0.50 \times 13.068 = -6.534 \text{ °C}
$$

$$
a_3 E^3 = 0.04 \times 47.241 = 1.890 \text{ °C}
$$

**Step 3 — Sum:**

$$
T(°C) = 90.375 - 6.534 + 1.890 = 85.731 \text{ °C}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to Kelvin:**

$$
T(K) = 85.731 + 273.15 = \boxed{358.88 \text{ K}}
$$

**Sanity check:** From standard Type E tables, 3.615 mV (cold junction at 0 °C) corresponds to ≈ 85 °C, confirming the answer. (7 marks)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit conversion errors:** Students frequently forget to convert torr to Pa or atm, or fail to convert °C to K. KTU examiners specifically *deduct 0.5–1 mark* for inconsistent units.
> 2. **Cold-junction compensation:** Students often assume the cold junction is at 0 K. Always state $T_c$ explicitly. If the cold junction is not at 0 °C, you must add the Seebeck voltage correction $E(T_c, 0)$.
> 3. **Sign of y in vapor pressure equations:** $\ln(p/p_{\text{ref}})$ is **negative** when $p < p_{\text{ref}}$. Forgetting the sign leads to wildly wrong temperatures (e.g., 100 K instead of 2.5 K).
> 4. **ITS-90 vs. IPTS-68:** A common trap. KTU asks specifically about ITS-90. Citing IPTS-68 fixed points (e.g., oxygen boiling point = 90.188 K) instead of the ITS-90 triple point of argon (83.8058 K) loses 1 mark.
> 5. **Curie Law range:** The Curie law $\chi = C/T$ is only valid in the limit $\mu B \ll k_B T$. Applying it at 10 mK with a 1 T field (where $\mu B / k_B T \approx 0.7$) without the Brillouin/Langevin correction loses 1 mark.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **ITS-90** is the *current* international temperature scale, replacing IPTS-68 since **January 1, 1990**. It ranges from **0.65 K to the highest measurable temperature** and is divided into **sub-ranges**, each with its own defining instrument.
- ✅ **Fixed points of ITS-90** are reproducible phase equilibria (triple points, freezing points). Key cryogenic ones: ³He TP (0.65 K), Ne TP (24.5561 K), O₂ TP (54.3584 K), Ar TP (83.8058 K), Hg TP (234.3156 K), and the **water triple point (273.16 K)** which is the central definition.
- ✅ **Thermocouples** rely on the **Seebeck Effect**; EMF = $\int S_{AB}\,dT$. For cryogenics, **Au-Fe/Chromel** (1 K–300 K) and **Type E** (20 K–1100 K) are most common.
- ✅ **Magnetic thermometers** use **Curie's Law** $\chi = C/T$. They are **primary thermometers** in the mK range. **NMR and Nuclear Orientation thermometers** extend down to **sub-mK** temperatures.
- ✅ **Vapor pressure thermometers** use the **Clausius-Clapeyron** relation. They are ITS-90 primary thermometers for ³He (0.65 K – 3.2 K) and ⁴He (1.25 K – 5.0 K) and ²H₂, Ne, N₂ (13 K – 36 K).
- ✅ **Platinum SPRT** is the ITS-90 standard from 13.8033 K to 1234.93 K, using the **Callendar-Van Dusen** equation and a **deviation function** calibrated at fixed points.
- ✅ **Above 1234.93 K**, the scale is defined by the **Planck radiation law** using a spectral radiation pyrometer with $c_2 = 0.014387768$ m·K.
- ✅ Always check **units** — KTU examiners deduct marks for °C/K/Pascal/torr mix-ups.
- ✅ Always state the **cold junction temperature** explicitly in thermocouple problems.
- ✅ Always remember the **sign convention** of $\ln(p/p_{\text{ref}})$ in vapor pressure problems.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Safety in Cryogenic Fluid Handling, Storage, and Use
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET861 - CRYOGENIC ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Safety in Cryogenic Fluid Handling, Storage, and Use

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cryogenic Safety** is the discipline of engineering, operational practice, and regulatory compliance that governs the identification, assessment, and mitigation of hazards arising from the production, storage, transfer, and use of fluids at temperatures below **120 K (−153 °C)**. Per **KTU 2024 syllabus (PEMET861 – Module 4)**, it encompasses hazard classification, material compatibility, personal protective equipment (PPE), pressure-relief philosophy, fire/explosion control, asphyxiation prevention, and adherence to codes such as ASME BPVC Section VIII, NFPA 55, CGA G-4.1, and IS 8478.

The four fundamental hazard families of any cryogenic fluid are:

1. **Cryogenic (Cold) Burn Hazard** — caused by skin/eye contact with cold surfaces, splashing liquids, or cold vapours.
2. **Asphyxiation Hazard** — caused by displacement of breathable oxygen in confined spaces by cold boil-off gas.
3. **Flammability / Explosion Hazard** — relevant for LH₂, LNG, and other combustible cryogens.
4. **Over-pressurization Hazard** — caused by thermal in-leak, phase change, or trapped liquid thermal expansion (TML).

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Thresholds (per OSHA / NIOSH / CGA):**
> - Oxygen Lower Flammability Limit (LFL) = **19.5 %–23.5 %** (safe band).
> - LEL of H₂ in air = **4 %** (volume).
> - LEL of CH₄ in air = **5 %** (volume).
> - Auto-ignition of H₂ = **858 K (585 °C)**.
> - Skin contact damage threshold below **−25 °C**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a pressurized pressure-cooker that is simultaneously (a) cold enough to instantly freeze your skin like liquid nitrogen does to a banana, (b) boiling off invisibly like dry ice in a closed room, silently pushing breathable air out, and (c) — if the fluid is LNG or LH₂ — capable of igniting in mid-air with a flame you cannot see in daylight. **Cryogenic safety is the complete engineering system that ensures the cooker's contents remain controlled** — cold but not destructive, sealed but never pressurized to rupture, contained but vented safely, and flammable only if deliberately ignited with the right safeguards.

A second intuition: think of a **cryogenic dewar as the “thermos bottle from hell.”** A thermos keeps your coffee hot safely. A cryogenic dewar keeps a fluid at −196 °C (LN₂) or −253 °C (LH₂). The fluids are at temperatures 200–300 °C colder than the surroundings, so heat *always* leaks in. The liquid therefore *always* boils and produces pressure. Safety systems exist because **the second law of thermodynamics makes continuous venting mandatory**, not optional.

## 1.3 Classification of Cryogenic Fluids by Hazard Profile

| Cryogen | Boiling Point (K) | Primary Hazard(s) | Hazard Tier |
|---|---|---|---|
| Helium (LHe) | 4.2 | Asphyxiation, cold burn | High |
| Hydrogen (LH₂) | 20.3 | Flammability + asphyxiation + cold burn | Extreme |
| Neon (LNe) | 27.1 | Asphyxiation, cold burn | High |
| Nitrogen (LN₂) | 77.4 | Asphyxiation, cold burn, O₂ enrichment (in spills) | High |
| Oxygen (LOX) | 90.2 | Fire/Explosion (oxidizer), cold burn | Extreme |
| Methane/LNG | 111.7 | Flammability, cold burn | Extreme |
| Argon (LAr) | 87.3 | Asphyxiation, cold burn | High |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Insight:** When asked to "list hazards of LN₂ vs LH₂" students commonly omit the **oxygen-enrichment hazard** unique to LN₂ spills. Liquid nitrogen condenses atmospheric oxygen on contact — a spilled LN₂ pool can become an **oxygen-enriched explosive mixture**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Hazard Decomposition

### 2.1.1 Cryogenic (Cold) Burn Mechanism
- Skin contact at temperatures below **−25 °C** causes irreversible tissue freezing → cellular ice-crystal rupture → frostbite equivalent to thermal burns.
- Cold burns are deceptive: the affected area feels numb, not painful → victims may continue exposure unconsciously.
- Cold vapour (invisible for LH₂, white plume for LN₂) at **−180 °C** can cause lung tissue damage on inhalation → pulmonary oedema.

### 2.1.2 Asphyxiation Mechanism
- Most cryogenic boil-off gases (N₂, He, Ar, Ne, H₂) are non-toxic but inert/asphyxiant.
- A 1 m³ spill of LN₂ yields ≈ **700 m³** of nitrogen gas at STP → rapidly drops ambient O₂ below **19.5 %** (safe threshold) inside a closed room.
- **Universal rule:** Treat any cold vapour cloud in a confined space as an asphyxiant; do not enter without SCBA (Self-Contained Breathing Apparatus).

### 2.1.3 Flammability / Explosion Mechanism (LH₂, LNG)
- LH₂ has an extremely wide flammability range: **4 % to 75 % in air** (broadest of any fuel).
- LH₂ flame is nearly invisible in daylight → thermal-imaging or flame-ionization detectors required.
- LNG pool spreads on water (cryogenic spills behave like water spills — flash vaporization, *not* sinking).
- **Detonation vs Deflagration:** Unconfined LH₂ deflagration; confined LH₂ can transition to detonation (DDT — Deflagration-to-Detonation Transition).

### 2.1.4 Over-Pressurization Mechanism
- Heat in-leak into vacuum-insulated storage → continuous boil-off → pressure rise.
- Pressure Relief Valve (PRV) sizing governed by **API 520 / ASME BPVC §VIII**.
- Trapped-liquid thermal expansion (e.g., between two closed valves) — water expands 10 % but cryogens expand **hundreds of times** between liquid and gas phase.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet — Cryogenic Safety Engineering

> [!NOTE]
> Use `\vert` instead of `|` for absolute values to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Formula / Equation | Symbol Meaning | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\dot{m}_{boil} = \dfrac{Q_{leak}}{\lambda}$ | Boil-off mass rate (kg/s), heat in-leak $Q_{leak}$ (W), latent heat $\lambda$ (J/kg) | Sizing PRVs & vent lines |
| 2 | $Q_{leak} = \dfrac{kA(T_{amb}-T_{c})}{\ell}$ | Conduction through neck / pipe (W) | Dewar & transfer line heat leak |
| 3 | $V_{gas} = \dot{m}_{boil} \cdot \dfrac{v_{g}}{M}$ | Vent volume flow (m³/s) at STP | Vent-line diameter sizing |
| 4 | $W_{PRV} = \dfrac{A}{10.5} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{k+1}} \cdot \sqrt{kP_{1}v_{1}\left(\dfrac{2}{k+1}\right)^{\frac{2}{k-1}}}$ | CGA / API-520 mass flux through PRV (kg/s) | PRV orifice sizing |
| 5 | $\dot{V}_{vent} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{boil}}{\rho_{gas}(T_{vent})}$ | Required vent volumetric flow (m³/s) | Building ventilation design |
| 6 | $\%O_2 = \dfrac{0.2095 \cdot V_{room}}{\;V_{room} + V_{N_2,boil}\;}$ | Oxygen % after spill in closed room | Confined-space safety check |
| 7 | $T_{film} = T_{sat} + \Delta T_{film}$ | Cold-burn contact temperature (K) | PPE rating selection |
| 8 | $P_{final} = P_{atm} \cdot \exp\left(\dfrac{V_{\ell}}{v_{\ell}}\left[\dfrac{1}{T_1}-\dfrac{1}{T_2}\right]R_{gas}\right)$ | Trapped-liquid pressure (Pa) | TRAPPED-LIQUID ANALYSIS |
| 9 | $f_{O_2} = \dfrac{n_{O_2,spill}}{n_{O_2,spill} + n_{N_2,air}}$ | O₂ enrichment factor after LN₂ spill | Fire/explosion hazard check |
| 10 | $A_{PRV} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{max}}{C_d K_d P_1 K_b K_c} \sqrt{\dfrac{TZ}{M}}$ | API-520 effective orifice area (mm²) | Mandatory PRV sizing per code |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Unit Reminders for KTU Board:**
> - $\lambda$ for LN₂ = **199 J/g** = 199 000 J/kg.
> - $\lambda$ for LH₂ = **445 J/g**.
> - $\lambda$ for LHe = **20.4 J/g** (very small — venting helium is *expensive*).
> - $v_g$ for N₂ at STP ≈ **0.86 m³/kg**.
> - For LNG: $v_{\ell}/v_g$ ratio ≈ **1/600** → 1 L LNG → 600 L methane gas.

## 2.3 Material Compatibility — Why Standard Steel Fails

- Carbon steel undergoes **ductile-to-brittle transition** below ~ **220 K (ASTM A36)**.
- For LH₂ service, austenitic stainless steels (304, 304L, 316, 321) are mandatory.
- Aluminium alloys (5083, 6061, 2219) are standard for LH₂ tank shells (NASA / ISRO cryogenic stage practice).
- **Thermal contraction** must be accommodated: aluminium contracts **~1.4 %** between 300 K and 4 K; design bellows or sliding supports.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **LNG Carrier Safety (Moss-Rosenberg & GTT Mark III tanks)** — design governed by IGC Code + NFPA 59A.
- **Space Launch Vehicles (ISRO GSLV Mk-III C25 stage, SpaceX Falcon 9)** — LH₂/LOX propellant handling demands the strictest safety protocols in cryogenic practice.
- **MRI Hospitals** — 1500 L LHe dewar refill requires lock-out/tag-out, O₂ monitoring, SCBA standby.
- **Industrial Gas Plants (INOX Air, Linde, Air Liquide)** — bulk LN₂/LOX/LAr storage yards follow CGA G-4.1 + IS 8478.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — PRV Mass Flow Through a Nozzle (CGA / API-520 Form)

We derive the critical-flow mass rate through a pressure-relief device sized for a cryogenic dewar experiencing heat in-leak.

**Given (KTU Board standard problem template):**
- Dewar vacuum-jacketed, holding LN₂ at $P_1$ = **400 kPa** gauge (i.e. $P_1,abs = 501.325$ kPa).
- Ambient temperature $T_{amb} = 300$ K; liquid nitrogen saturation $T_{sat} = 77.4$ K.
- Heat in-leak $Q_{leak}$ = **150 W** (realistic for a 1000 L research dewar).
- Back-pressure $P_2$ = atmospheric (101.325 kPa).
- Specific heat ratio of N₂ gas $k$ = **1.4**.
- Discharge coefficient $C_d$ = **0.9** (typical for PRV).

**Required:** Minimum PRV orifice area (mm²) so dewar pressure never exceeds design limit during a full cool-down from 300 K to 77 K + steady-state boil-off.

### Step 1 — Boil-off Rate from Heat In-leak
The boil-off mass rate is governed by energy conservation; the energy required to vaporize liquid is its latent heat.

$$
\dot{m}_{boil} = \frac{Q_{leak}}{\lambda_{LN_2}}
$$

Substitute $Q_{leak} = 150\ \text{W}$ and $\lambda_{LN_2} = 199\,000\ \text{J/kg}$:

$$
\dot{m}_{boil} = \frac{150}{199\,000}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{boil} = 7.538 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{kg/s}
$$

Convert to kg/h for engineering intuition:

$$
\dot{m}_{boil,h} = 7.538 \times 10^{-4} \times 3600 = 2.714\ \text{kg/h}
$$

### Step 2 — Convert to Volumetric Flow at Vent Conditions
For a vent discharging to atmosphere at approximately 300 K, use ideal-gas law:

$$
\dot{V}_{vent} = \frac{\dot{m}_{boil}\, R\, T_{vent}}{P_{vent}\, M_{N_2}}
$$

Constants: $R = 8.314$ J/mol·K, $T_{vent} = 300$ K, $P_{vent} = 101\,325$ Pa, $M_{N_2} = 0.028$ kg/mol.

$$
\dot{V}_{vent} = \frac{7.538 \times 10^{-4} \times 8.314 \times 300}{101\,325 \times 0.028}
$$

Numerator:

$$
7.538 \times 10^{-4} \times 8.314 \times 300 = 1.880
$$

Denominator:

$$
101\,325 \times 0.028 = 2837.1
$$

$$
\dot{V}_{vent} = \frac{1.880}{2837.1}
$$

$$
\dot{V}_{vent} = 6.63 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m}^3/\text{s} = 0.663\ \text{L/s}
$$

### Step 3 — Apply API-520 / CGA Orifice Equation

$$
A = \frac{\dot{m}_{boil}}{C_d \, K_d \, P_1 \, K_b \, K_c} \sqrt{\frac{TZ}{M}}
$$

For saturated N₂ gas at 400 kPa gauge: $T = 110\ \text{K}$ (interpolated saturation), compressibility $Z \approx 0.95$, $K_d = K_b = K_c = 1$ (no superheat, no back-pressure derating), $C_d = 0.9$.

$$
A = \frac{7.538 \times 10^{-4}}{0.9 \times 1 \times 501\,325 \times 1 \times 1} \sqrt{\frac{110 \times 0.95}{0.028}}
$$

Inner-root:

$$
\frac{110 \times 0.95}{0.028} = \frac{104.5}{0.028} = 3732.1
$$

$$
\sqrt{3732.1} = 61.09
$$

Denominator product:

$$
0.9 \times 501\,325 = 451\,192.5
$$

$$
A = \frac{7.538 \times 10^{-4} \times 61.09}{451\,192.5}
$$

Numerator:

$$
7.538 \times 10^{-4} \times 61.09 = 0.04605
$$

$$
A = \frac{0.04605}{451\,192.5}
$$

$$
A = 1.02 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{m}^2
$$

Convert to mm²:

$$
A = 1.02 \times 10^{-7} \times 10^{6} = 0.102\ \text{mm}^2
$$

### Step 4 — Apply Engineering Safety Factor
Codes (ASME §VIII) mandate a **minimum PRV size of 6 mm orifice (≈ 28 mm²)** regardless of calculation, since cryogenic systems must tolerate transient block-valve closures and external fire cases. The calculated area is therefore **not the final design** — it is a sanity check.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Takeaway:** For an LN₂ dewar under steady boil-off, the actual code-mandated PRV is far larger than the thermodynamic minimum because **fire-case heat in-leak** (per API-520 §4.5.6) requires heat input ≈ **10 925 × A_vessel}^{0.566}$ W**, which for a 1 m² surface area = 10 925 W → requires a 50× larger PRV.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Confined-Space Oxygen Depletion After LN₂ Spill

**Given:** A laboratory of volume $V_{room} = 50\ \text{m}^3$. A 5 L spill of LN₂ occurs (≈ 4.51 kg). Assume complete vaporization in the room.

**Required:** Does the room remain above OSHA 19.5 % O₂ safe limit?

### Step 1 — Mass of LN₂ Spilled
$$
\rho_{LN_2} = 808\ \text{kg/m}^3
$$
$$
m_{LN_2} = \rho \cdot V = 808 \times 0.005 = 4.04\ \text{kg}
$$

### Step 2 — Equivalent Gas Volume at STP
$$
V_{N_2,STP} = \frac{m_{LN_2}}{\rho_{N_2,STP}} = \frac{4.04}{1.2506} = 3.23\ \text{m}^3
$$

### Step 3 — Final Oxygen Concentration (Neglecting Ventilation)
$$
\%O_2 = \frac{0.2095 \cdot V_{room}}{V_{room} + V_{N_2,STP}} \times 100
$$

$$
\%O_2 = \frac{0.2095 \times 50}{50 + 3.23} \times 100
$$

Numerator:

$$
0.2095 \times 50 = 10.475
$$

Denominator:

$$
50 + 3.23 = 53.23
$$

$$
\%O_2 = \frac{10.475}{53.23} \times 100
$$

$$
\%O_2 = 19.68\ \%
$$

**Result:** O₂ falls just above the 19.5 % OSHA limit → borderline condition. In a tighter room (e.g. 20 m³), the same spill would drop O₂ to 18.0 % → immediate asphyxiation hazard.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often compute this without the 100× multiplier or forget the volume ratio. Show full unit cancellations.

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use Which PPE / Detector

| Scenario | Hazard | Mandatory PPE / Detection |
|---|---|---|
| LN₂ transfer in open lab | Cold splash, O₂ depletion | Cryogenic gloves, face shield, O₂ alarm (set at 19.5 %) |
| LH₂ transfer | Flammability + cold burn | Anti-static clothing, H₂ detector (LEL ≤ 25 %), flame detector (UV/IR), grounding straps |
| LHe dewar refill (MRI room) | Asphyxiation (He rises to ceiling first) | SCBA standby, ceiling-mounted O₂ alarms, **forced ventilation ≥ 6 ACH** |
| LNG carrier bunkering | Cold burn + flammable vapour | Flame-imaging camera, gas detector (IR), thermal-imaging PPE |
| LOX transfer | Fire enrichment | LOX-compatible (clean, oil-free) PPE, no organics, O₂-cleaned tools |
| Vacuum-jacketed line repair | Implosion + cold | Pressure-test before cool-down, torque to OEM spec, leak-check with He mass-spec |

## 3.4 Trapped-Liquid Pressure Rise — Worked Proof

Consider an isolated segment of pipe between two closed valves at $T_1 = 300\ \text{K}$ filled with LH₂. If ambient heat raises it to $T_2 = 350\ \text{K}$:

$$
P_{final} = P_{atm} \cdot \exp\left(\frac{V_{\ell}}{v_{\ell}}\left[\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right]R_{gas}\right)
$$

With $V_{\ell}/v_{\ell} = n$, ideal-gas phase change:

$$
P_{final} = \frac{n R T_2}{V_{gas,final}} \cdot \text{(correction for liquid phase still present)}
$$

For LH₂ in a 1 L volume, the trapped-liquid pressure rise is **> 100 MPa within seconds** → pipe rupture. **Hence: every cryogenic line segment must have either a PRV or a continuous vent path.** Codes prohibit dead-headed liquid traps.

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow — Cryogenic Safety System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Cryogenic Fluid Source] --> B[Primary Storage Vessel]
    B --> C[Pressure Relief Valve PRV]
    B --> D[Vacuum Jacket Integrity Monitor]
    B --> E[Level Instrumentation]
    B --> F[Transfer Line]
    F --> G[Emergency Shutoff Valve ESOV]
    F --> H[Vent Stack]
    F --> I[End User / Process]
    J[Oxygen Deficiency Hazard ODH Sensor] --> K[Safety Logic Controller SLC]
    L[Hydrocarbon LEL Detector] --> K
    M[Flame Detector UV/IR] --> K
    N[Smoke and Heat Detector] --> K
    K --> O[Alarm and Annunciation Panel]
    K --> P[Automatic Shutdown System ESD]
    K --> Q[Emergency Ventilation Fan]
    P --> G
    K --> R[SCBA Staging Area Access Control]
    P --> S[Firewater Deluge System]
    P --> T[Emergency Response Team Notification]
```

**Interpretation of the diagram:**

- The **Primary Storage Vessel** has three parallel safety paths: pressure relief (PRV), vacuum integrity (D), and level monitoring (E).
- The **Safety Logic Controller (SLC)** acts as the SIL-2/SIL-3 rated central brain, fusing inputs from ODH, LEL, flame, and smoke detectors.
- On confirmed hazard, the SLC triggers ESD (Emergency Shutdown) by closing the ESOV, starting firewater deluge, and notifying the response team.
- The vent stack (H) discharges cold vapour safely above the building ridge, away from ignition sources.

## 4.2 Decision Tree — Hazard Triage Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Alarm Triggered] --> B{Is ODH Confined Space Alarm Active?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Evacuate All Personnel]
    B -- No --> D{Is LEL Detector in Alarm?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Isolate LH2 or LNG Source]
    D -- No --> F{Is Flame Detector Triggered?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Activate Firewater Deluge]
    F -- No --> H{Is Pressure High Alarm Active?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Verify PRV Operation]
    H -- No --> J[Log Event and Reset]
    C --> K[SCBA Entry Team]
    K --> L[Ventilate Confined Space]
    L --> M[Restore O2 Above 19.5 percent]
    E --> N[Vent Residual Gas Safely]
    G --> O[Cool Adjacent Structures]
    I --> P[If PRV Failed Initiate ESD]
    P --> Q[Notify Emergency Response Team]
```

**Interpretation:** Each alarm channel has a unique isolation response. ODH → evacuation; LEL → isolation; flame → deluge; pressure → PRV verification. The system prevents cascade failures by ensuring one hazard does not mask another.

## 4.3 Hierarchy of Cryogenic Safety Controls

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Inherent Safe Design] --> B[Engineering Controls]
    B --> C[Administrative Controls]
    C --> D[PPE]
    A1[Use non flammable fluid where possible] --> A
    A2[Limit inventory to minimum required] --> A
    A3[Minimize piping length] --> A
    B1[Double walled vacuum jacketed tanks] --> B
    B2[Pressure relief devices on every vessel] --> B
    B3[Oxygen and LEL detectors with SIL ratings] --> B
    B4[Forced ventilation in storage rooms] --> B
    C1[Operator training and certification] --> C
    C2[Lock out tag out LOTO procedures] --> C
    C3[Hazard communication and labeling] --> C
    C4[Hot work permits during maintenance] --> C
    D1[Cryogenic gloves and face shield] --> D
    D2[Flame resistant anti static clothing] --> D
    D3[SCBA for confined space entry] --> D
```

**Interpretation:** This is the standard **Nolan-Heaney hierarchy** used in process-safety engineering (analogous to the hierarchy of controls in occupational safety). Inherent design (e.g., use He instead of H₂) > engineering (PRVs, detectors) > administrative (training, LOTO) > PPE.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]

**Q: List any three distinct hazards associated with the handling of liquid hydrogen and state the corresponding safeguard for each.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Flammability (broadest range 4–75 % in air):** safeguard = LEL detectors set at ≤ 25 % LEL, hydrogen-compatible flame detectors (UV/IR), inert-gas purge before liquid transfer, anti-static grounding straps on all transfer equipment.
2. **Cold burn (−253 °C):** safeguard = cryogenic-rated multi-layer insulated gloves, full-face shield, flame-resistant anti-static apron, and emergency warm-water drench shower within 10 m of every LH₂ handling station.
3. **Asphyxiation (rapid boil-off displaces breathable air):** safeguard = continuous oxygen-deficiency monitoring (alarm at 19.5 % O₂), forced mechanical ventilation ≥ 6 air changes/hour, and mandatory SCBA for any confined-space entry within 30 m of an LH₂ vent stack.

(Each correctly-paired hazard-safeguard carries **1 mark**; total = 3 marks.)

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — May 2023]

**Q: Why is liquid nitrogen uniquely capable of creating an oxygen-enriched atmosphere after a spill? Cite a single practical consequence.**

**Model Answer:** LN₂ has a boiling point (77.4 K) lower than the boiling point of oxygen (90.2 K). When LN₂ is spilled on a surface, the surface cools below 90.2 K and begins to **condense atmospheric oxygen** directly from the surrounding air. The liquid pool becomes surrounded by an oxygen-enriched layer that can reach 35–50 % O₂. **Practical consequence:** any nearby combustible material (oil on a pump, paper rags, organic insulation) can ignite spontaneously or burn explosively at temperatures 5–10× lower than normal — leading to a fire even when no ignition source is present. (2 marks for the mechanism + 1 mark for consequence.)

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Do NOT list hazards without naming the corresponding code/standard.** Examiners explicitly look for "NFPA 55," "ASME §VIII," "CGA G-4.1," "IS 8478" citations — omitting them costs 2 marks.
> 2. **Do NOT confuse LEL and LFL.** LEL (Lower Explosive Limit) and LFL (Lower Flammable Limit) are numerically the same but terminology matters in formal answers.
> 3. **Do NOT present PRV sizing without including the fire-case heat-in-leak term** (≈ 10 925 × A^{0.566} W per API-520 §4.5.6). Steady-state boil-off alone is insufficient.
> 4. **Do NOT omit the trapped-liquid expansion scenario.** It is the single most-tested safety concept in KTU ESE for the past four years.
> 5. **Units must appear at every numerical step.** A bare number without units loses 0.5 mark per occurrence.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question Bank]

**(a)** Describe in detail the **four primary hazard categories** associated with cryogenic fluid handling. For each hazard, identify **two engineering safeguards** mandated by international codes. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 1000 L cryogenic dewar contains liquid nitrogen at saturation temperature 77.4 K and operates at 400 kPa gauge. Heat in-leak into the vessel during normal operation is 150 W. The latent heat of vaporization of LN₂ is 199 kJ/kg and the density of N₂ gas at STP is 1.25 kg/m³. **(i)** Calculate the boil-off mass flow rate (kg/h). **(ii)** Calculate the equivalent volume of boil-off gas at STP per day (m³/day). **(iii)** If the storage room is 8 m × 6 m × 4 m with no forced ventilation, compute the time (in minutes) for the O₂ concentration to drop from 20.95 % to the OSHA lower threshold of 19.5 % assuming complete accumulation of boil-off gas. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Question A

**Part (a) — Seven Marks:**

*Category 1: Cryogenic (Cold) Burn Hazard (≈ 2 marks)*
- Mechanism: contact with cold surface or splash → tissue freezing, frostbite equivalent to thermal burn; cold-vapour inhalation → pulmonary oedema.
- Safeguards: (i) cryogenic-insulated gloves rated to −200 °C, full-face shield, closed-toe leather footwear, emergency warm-water drench shower (per **NFPA 55 §6.3**); (ii) insulation on all cold piping ≥ 25 mm thickness with vapour barrier, signages per **IS 8478**.

*Category 2: Asphyxiation Hazard (≈ 2 marks)*
- Mechanism: cold boil-off gas displaces breathable O₂; 1 L LN₂ → ~700 L N₂ gas at STP.
- Safeguards: (i) continuous oxygen-deficiency monitoring with alarm at 19.5 % O₂ (per **CGA G-4.1 §5.2**); (ii) forced mechanical ventilation ≥ 6 air changes/hour in any enclosed cryogenic storage room (per **NFPA 55 §6.4**).

*Category 3: Flammability / Explosion Hazard (≈ 1.5 marks)*
- Mechanism: LH₂ (LEL 4 %, UEL 75 % in air), LNG (LEL 5 %), LOX as oxidizer.
- Safeguards: (i) explosion-proof electrical fittings (Class I Div 1, per **NFPA 70 NEC**), LEL detectors set at 25 % LEL, flame detectors (UV/IR); (ii) inert-gas purge of all transfer lines before/after cryogen flow, anti-static grounding of all components.

*Category 4: Over-Pressurization Hazard (≈ 1.5 marks)*
- Mechanism: continuous heat in-leak → boil-off pressure rise; trapped-liquid thermal expansion.
- Safeguards: (i) Pressure Relief Valve (PRV) on every vessel sized per **API-520 / ASME BPVC §VIII**; (ii) rupture disc in series with PRV for fail-safe redundancy (per **ASME §VIII UG-127**); (iii) prohibit dead-headed liquid traps between two closed valves.

*Conclusion mark (awarded for at least one code/standard citation):* the student should reference **NFPA 55, CGA G-4.1, ASME BPVC §VIII, API-520, IS 8478, IGC Code**.

---

**Part (b) — Seven Marks:**

**(i) Boil-off mass flow rate (2 marks):**
$$
\dot{m}_{boil} = \frac{Q_{leak}}{\lambda_{LN_2}} = \frac{150\ \text{W}}{199\,000\ \text{J/kg}}
$$
$$
\dot{m}_{boil} = 7.538 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{kg/s} \times 3600\ \text{s/h}
$$
$$
\boxed{\dot{m}_{boil} = 2.714\ \text{kg/h}}
$$

**(ii) Equivalent volume of boil-off gas per day (2 marks):**
Daily mass: $m_{day} = 2.714 \times 24 = 65.14\ \text{kg/day}$
$$
V_{day} = \frac{m_{day}}{\rho_{N_2,STP}} = \frac{65.14}{1.25} = \boxed{52.11\ \text{m}^3/\text{day}}
$$

**(iii) Time for O₂ to fall from 20.95 % to 19.5 % (3 marks):**

Room volume: $V_{room} = 8 \times 6 \times 4 = 192\ \text{m}^3$.

Set up the equation — the boil-off mass entering per unit time is $\dot{m}_{boil}$. The instantaneous O₂ fraction (no ventilation) is:
$$
\%O_2(t) = \frac{0.2095 \cdot V_{room}}{V_{room} + \dot{V}_{boil} \cdot t}
$$
where $\dot{V}_{boil}$ is the volumetric boil-off rate (m³/s). We calculated $\dot{V}_{boil} \approx 6.03 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m}^3/\text{s}$ in §3.1.

Solve for $t$ when $\%O_2 = 0.195$:
$$
0.195 = \frac{0.2095 \times 192}{192 + 6.03 \times 10^{-4} \cdot t}
$$

Numerator: $0.2095 \times 192 = 40.224$.

$$
192 + 6.03 \times 10^{-4} \cdot t = \frac{40.224}{0.195} = 206.28
$$

$$
6.03 \times 10^{-4} \cdot t = 14.28
$$

$$
t = \frac{14.28}{6.03 \times 10^{-4}} = 23\,682\ \text{s}
$$

$$
t = \frac{23\,682}{60} \approx \boxed{394.7\ \text{minutes}}
$$

**Conclusion (1 mark for §b overall):** Approximately **6.6 hours** — meaning an unventilated LN₂ dewar room reaches OSHA alarm threshold within a single work shift. **Mandatory forced ventilation is non-negotiable.**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question Bank]

**(a)** With neat labelled schematic sketches, explain the **construction and working of a vacuum-insulated cryogenic storage dewar**. Highlight the safety features embedded in such a dewar. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** (i) Define **trapped-liquid expansion** and explain why it is uniquely dangerous in cryogenic systems. (ii) A 2 L segment of cryogenic piping is isolated between two closed valves and contains liquid nitrogen at 77 K and 101 kPa. If the segment warms uniformly to 300 K without any venting, estimate the final pressure in the piping. State clearly any assumptions. Use $v_{\ell, N_2} = 1.197 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}^3/\text{kg}$ and $R_{N_2} = 296.8\ \text{J/kg·K}$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Question B

**Part (a) — Seven Marks:**

*Sketch (3 marks for a clearly-labelled schematic showing):*
- Inner vessel (cold) holding cryogen.
- Outer vessel (warm) at ambient.
- Vacuum annulus between the two vessels (≤ 1 × 10⁻³ Pa) — primary insulator.
- Multi-Layer Insulation (MLI) — aluminized mylar blankets (10–50 layers).
- Support structure: low-conductivity fibreglass/epoxy struts.
- Neck tube connecting inner and outer vessels.
- Instrumentation ports: level gauge, pressure gauge, thermometers.
- Safety ports: PRV, rupture disc, vacuum-jacket safety valve.
- Liquid inlet/outlet fittings.

*Working principle (2 marks):* heat in-leak is reduced by combining vacuum (eliminating gas conduction + convection) + MLI (radiation shield, low emissivity) + low-conductivity supports. The result: < 1 % loss per day for modern dewars.

*Safety features (2 marks):*
- Vacuum integrity gauge (Pirani or ion gauge) — alerts if vacuum degrades → heat in-leak rises.
- Dual PRV + rupture disc redundancy — one spring-loaded, one failsafe bursting.
- Liquid-level instrumentation with redundant sensors (differential pressure + capacitance).
- Bursting disc upstream of PRV to prevent PRV chatter against cryogenic ice.
- Outer vessel pressure relief — protects from vacuum-jacket collapse (implosion).
- Grounding lugs for lightning and static protection.

**Part (b) — Seven Marks:**

**(i) Definition + danger (3 marks):**
> [!NOTE]
> **Trapped-liquid expansion** is the phenomenon where a cryogenic liquid trapped between two closed valves or in a blocked section expands as it warms, generating extremely high pressures because the liquid-to-gas density ratio is several hundred-fold.

**Uniquely dangerous in cryogenics because:**
- Water expands ≈ 4 % from 4 °C to 100 °C → low pressure rise in closed pipe.
- LN₂ expands from liquid (808 kg/m³) to gas (1.25 kg/m³) → factor of ~650.
- LH₂ expansion factor ≈ 845; LHe expansion factor ≈ 757.
- Pressure rise can reach **tens to hundreds of MPa** in seconds, exceeding yield strength of any pipe.

**(ii) Calculation (4 marks):**

**Assumption:** The volume is rigid (steel pipe is incompressible); the LN₂ liquid initially fills the volume completely. As it warms to 300 K, a fraction vaporizes; we model the final state as saturated liquid + vapour at 300 K. Simplified energy-balance model:

Mass of LN₂ initially:
$$
m = \frac{V}{v_{\ell}} = \frac{2 \times 10^{-3}}{1.197 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.671\ \text{kg}
$$

Using ideal-gas law for vapour at final T, equating volume = initial 2 L (since liquid shrinks negligibly):

Total moles: $n = m/M = 1.671/0.028 = 59.68$ mol.

If **all** the LN₂ vaporized (worst case, no liquid remaining) at 300 K:
$$
P_{final} = \frac{n R T}{V} = \frac{59.68 \times 8.314 \times 300}{2 \times 10^{-3}}
$$

Numerator: $59.68 \times 8.314 \times 300 = 148\,853$.

$$
P_{final} = \frac{148\,853}{2 \times 10^{-3}} = 7.44 \times 10^{7}\ \text{Pa}
$$

$$
\boxed{P_{final} \approx 74.4\ \text{MPa}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> This pressure **vastly exceeds** the burst rating of any commercial cryogenic pipe (typically 10–25 MPa). The pipe would rupture violently with explosive force — a critical reason why **NO cryogenic line may be isolated between two closed valves** without an active vent or PRV.

**Engineering takeaway (1 mark):** Every cryogenic piping segment must have either (i) a vented path to a safe location, (ii) a pressure relief device, or (iii) thermal relief valves of 1/4" minimum on the trapped segment. This is enforced by **ASME B31.3 §A330** and **CGA G-4.1 §6.3**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Points to Memorize for KTU ESE / Internal Evaluation:**

- [ ] **Cryogenic** is defined as T < **120 K (−153 °C)** per KTU syllabus.
- [ ] **Four hazard categories**: cold burn, asphyxiation, flammability, over-pressurization.
- [ ] **LH₂ flammability range 4–75 %** is the broadest of any fuel.
- [ ] **1 L LN₂ → ~700 L N₂ gas** at STP; memorize this ratio for confined-space calculations.
- [ ] **LOX spill = O₂-enrichment hazard**, often missed by students.
- [ ] **Trapped-liquid pressure rise**: water = 4 %; LN₂/LH₂/LHe = **hundreds of %** → pipe rupture.
- [ ] **Mandatory codes**: ASME BPVC §VIII (vessels), API-520 (PRV sizing), NFPA 55 (cryogen storage), CGA G-4.1 (general cryogen safety), IS 8478 (Indian Standard for cryogenic storage), IGC Code (LNG carriers).
- [ ] **PRV orifice equation** uses fire-case heat in-leak = 10 925 × A^{0.566} W per API-520 §4.5.6.
- [ ] **Material rule**: austenitic stainless (304/304L/316) or aluminium alloy (5083/6061/2219) for cryogenic piping; never use carbon steel below 220 K.
- [ ] **Hierarchy of controls**: Inherent → Engineering → Administrative → PPE. Examiners reward answers that follow this sequence.
- [ ] **LH₂ flame is invisible in daylight** → use IR / UV / flame-ionization detectors.
- [ ] **MLI + Vacuum** are the two pillars of dewar insulation; either alone is insufficient.
- [ ] **Forced ventilation ≥ 6 ACH** is mandatory for enclosed cryogenic storage rooms.
- [ ] **Confined-space entry**: SCBA mandatory whenever O₂ < 19.5 % or unknown.
- [ ] **Anti-static grounding** is critical for LH₂ and LNG transfer (static charge is a documented ignition source).
- [ ] **Lock-Out / Tag-Out (LOTO)** is mandatory before any maintenance on cryogenic lines, including vacuum-jacket servicing.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for KTU Viva:** **"C-A-F-O"** — **C**ryoburn, **A**sphyxiation, **F**lammability, **O**verpressure. Recall this in any "list the hazards" question to earn full marks instantly.
